howToUseInApp.note: Use data.DECKS as your deck array. Each deck follows your current code structure and adds multiplechoice and exam100 arrays.

cards.q: What are the three elements of the classic software trilemma?
cards.a: Good, Fast, and Cheap ‚ÄĒ you can only pick two.

cards.q: What are the main requirements elicitation techniques?
cards.a: Interviews, focus groups, surveys, shadowing/observation, copycatting, document analysis, and prototyping.

cards.q: What are functional requirements? Give examples.
cards.a: Behaviors the system must perform ‚ÄĒ e.g., Search, Authentication, Add to Cart, Take Payment.

cards.q: What are Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs)? Give examples.
cards.a: Quality attributes the system must have ‚ÄĒ e.g., Performance, Security, Compatibility, Compliance.

cards.q: What is the standard user story format?
cards.a: "As a [user persona], I want [describe need] so that [describe benefit]"

cards.q: What is an Epic in agile story writing?
cards.a: A large, high-level requirement that describes a broad feature, meant to be broken into smaller user stories later.

cards.q: What are the two types of project decomposition (slicing)?
cards.a: Vertical (fully functional features end-to-end) and Horizontal (layer-based implementation).

cards.q: List four common requirements gathering challenges.
cards.a: Lack of stakeholder involvement, ambiguous/unclear input, conflicting data, feature/scope creep, and unrealistic expectations.

cards.q: What is the difference between an Epic and a User Story?
cards.a: An Epic is a large, broad requirement; a User Story is a smaller, specific requirement derived from an Epic that can be completed in a sprint.

truefalse.q: The classic trilemma states you can have Good, Fast, AND Cheap all at once.
truefalse.exp: You can only pick two ‚ÄĒ Good+Fast is expensive, Good+Cheap is slow, Fast+Cheap is lower quality.

truefalse.q: Shadowing and observation are valid requirements elicitation techniques.
truefalse.exp: Shadowing (watching users in their natural environment) is a key elicitation method.

truefalse.q: Non-functional requirements include things like Search and Authentication.
truefalse.exp: Search and Authentication are functional requirements. NFRs are quality attributes like Performance and Security.

truefalse.q: Vertical slicing means implementing one layer (e.g., UI only) at a time.
truefalse.exp: Vertical slicing means delivering a fully functional feature end-to-end. Horizontal slicing is layer-based.

truefalse.q: Feature/Scope Creep is a common requirements gathering challenge.
truefalse.exp: Scope creep ‚ÄĒ uncontrolled growth of requirements ‚ÄĒ is one of the most common project challenges.

fillinblank.q: The classic software trilemma consists of Good, Fast, and ___.
As a [user persona], I want [describe need] so that ___.
A large requirement broken into smaller user stories is called an ___.
___ requirements include performance, security, and compliance.
Two types of project slicing are Vertical and ___.

multiplechoice.q: Which is a NON-functional requirement?

options.B: The cart shows the running total
options.C: The system must respond within 200 ms under normal load

multiplechoice.exp: Performance/latency is a non-functional requirement (a quality). The others describe behaviors (functional).
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: The system must respond within 200 ms under normal load

exam100.a: Good, Fast, and Cheap ‚ÄĒ you can only pick two.

exam100.q: What are the main requirements elicitation techniques?
exam100.a: Interviews, focus groups, surveys, shadowing/observation, copycatting, document analysis, and prototyping.

exam100.a: Quality attributes the system must have ‚ÄĒ e.g., Performance, Security, Compatibility, Compliance.

cards.q: What is Agile?
cards.a: An iterative and incremental approach to project management that prioritizes flexibility, collaboration, and customer feedback.

cards.q: When was the Agile Manifesto published and who wrote it?
cards.a: 2001, by 17 software practitioners who met in Snowbird, Utah.

cards.q: What are the 4 core values of the Agile Manifesto?
cards.a:
1) Individuals & interactions over processes/tools
2) Working software over documentation
3) Customer collaboration over contracts
4) Responding to change over following a plan

cards.q: When was Scrum formalized and by whom?
cards.a: 1995 by Jeff Sutherland and Ken Schwaber.

cards.q: What 1940s concept influenced Agile methodology?
cards.a: Kanban, developed at Toyota with Lean roots.

cards.q: What is the goal of Agile?
cards.a: Deliver increments of working software quickly, with close collaboration instead of rigid planning.

cards.q: What is SAFe and when was it published?
cards.a: Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe), published in 2011 by Dean Leffingwell.

cards.q: What is Extreme Programming (XP) and who created it?
cards.a: XP is an Agile methodology created by Kent Beck, originating from the Chrysler C3 project (1996‚Äď1999).

truefalse.q: Agile prioritizes rigid planning over responding to change.
truefalse.exp: Agile values "Responding to change over following a plan" ‚ÄĒ flexibility is core.

truefalse.q: Scrum was formalized in 1995 by Sutherland and Schwaber.
truefalse.exp: Correct. Though Scrum inspiration came from a 1986 Takeuchi/Nonaka paper.

truefalse.q: The Agile Manifesto was published in 2001.
truefalse.exp: Correct. It formally unified various lightweight methodologies.

truefalse.q: Agile values processes and tools over individuals and interactions.
truefalse.exp: The Manifesto states the opposite: "Individuals and interactions over processes and tools."

truefalse.q: Kanban originated in the 1940s at Toyota.
truefalse.exp: Correct. Kanban grew from Toyota's Lean manufacturing roots in the 1940s.

fillinblank.q: Agile is ___ and incremental approach to project management.
The Agile Manifesto was published in ___.
Scrum was formalized by Sutherland and ___.
Agile values customer ___ over contracts.
Kanban originated at ___ with Lean roots in the 1940s.

multiplechoice.q: Which is NOT one of the four values of the Agile Manifesto?

options.A: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
options.C: Following a detailed plan over responding to change

multiplechoice.exp: Agile VALUES responding to change OVER following a plan ‚ÄĒ the reverse of the option shown.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Following a detailed plan over responding to change

multiplechoice.q: Which Scrum event focuses on the PROCESS rather than the product?
multiplechoice.exp: Retrospectives discuss the process ‚ÄĒ what to keep, change, improve. Reviews discuss the product itself.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Sprint Retrospective

multiplechoice.q: Which is a TYPICAL responsibility of a Scrum Master?

options.B: Coach the team, facilitate events, remove impediments, advocate Agile

multiplechoice.exp: Scrum Master is a servant-leader: coach, facilitator, protector. The Product Owner manages the backlog.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Coach the team, facilitate events, remove impediments, advocate Agile

multiplechoice.q: Which is TRUE about an MVP?

options.A: It is the final, polished product
options.B: It is the same as a prototype
options.C: It is usable and demonstrable, delivering enough value to test key assumptions
options.D: It must include every planned feature

multiplechoice.exp: MVP is minimum, viable (works), product (can be used or handed off). It is NOT a prototype or the final product.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: It is usable and demonstrable, delivering enough value to test key assumptions

cards.q: What does SCM stand for and what is it?
cards.a: Source Control Management (also called Version Control Systems/VCS). It tracks and manages changes to source code over time.

cards.q: What is the difference between centralized and distributed VCS?
cards.a: Centralized (SVN, Perforce): single central repository, always needs network. Distributed (Git, Mercurial): every user has a full copy, full offline work possible.

cards.q: What was the first centralized VCS and when was it created?
cards.a: SCCS (Source Code Control System) in the 1970s‚Äď1980s.

cards.q: When did Git emerge and what type of VCS is it?
cards.a: 2000s. Git is a distributed VCS and is currently the most widely used.

cards.q: Name three modern cloud-based SCM platforms.
cards.a: GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket (also Azure DevOps).

cards.q: What are the key advantages of distributed VCS over centralized?
cards.a: Full offline work, fast branching, local commits, full history clone, better merging.

cards.q: In distributed VCS, how does branching differ from centralized?
cards.a: Branching is fast and cheap ‚ÄĒ branches are "first-class citizens" with no network needed. In centralized (e.g., SVN), branching is often slow and painful.

cards.q: What is CVS and what replaced it?
cards.a: Concurrent Versions System ‚ÄĒ a centralized VCS from the 1990s. It was replaced by SVN and later Git.

truefalse.q: Git is a centralized version control system.
truefalse.exp: Git is distributed ‚ÄĒ every user has a full clone of the repository including full history.

truefalse.q: In a centralized VCS, you can commit code without network access.
truefalse.exp: Centralized VCS requires network for most actions including commits.

truefalse.q: Distributed VCS allows fully offline operations including commit, branch, and log.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ distributed systems like Git store the full repo locally.

truefalse.q: GitHub is a version control system.
truefalse.exp: GitHub is a cloud platform for hosting Git repositories, not a VCS itself. Git is the VCS.

truefalse.q: In Git, branching is fast and inexpensive compared to SVN.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ Git branches are lightweight pointers, making branching nearly instant.

fillinblank.q: SCM stands for Source Control ___.
Git is a ___ version control system (not centralized).
GitHub, GitLab, and ___ are modern cloud SCM platforms.
In centralized VCS, commits go directly to the ___ repository.
SCCS, RCS, CVS, and SVN are all examples of ___ VCS.

multiplechoice.q: Which Git command would you use to apply a single specific commit from one branch onto another?
multiplechoice.exp: Cherry-pick applies a specific commit from one branch onto another. Rebase replays a series; squash collapses commits; tag marks a release.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: git cherry-pick

multiplechoice.q: Which SCM type is BEST for an open-source project with worldwide contributors?

options.D: Manual file copies labeled v1, v2, v3

multiplechoice.exp: Distributed VCS like Git gives each contributor a full repo with history ‚ÄĒ perfect for distributed teams and offline work.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Distributed (e.g., Git)

multiplechoice.q: Which Git operation REWRITES history?
multiplechoice.exp: Rebase replays commits onto another branch, rewriting history. Merge preserves history with a merge commit.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Rebase

exam100.q: What does SCM stand for and what is it?
exam100.a: Source Control Management (also called Version Control Systems/VCS). It tracks and manages changes to source code over time.

exam100.q: What is the difference between centralized and distributed VCS?
exam100.a: Centralized (SVN, Perforce): single central repository, always needs network. Distributed (Git, Mercurial): every user has a full copy, full offline work possible.

exam100.q: What was the first centralized VCS and when was it created?

exam100.q: When did Git emerge and what type of VCS is it?
exam100.a: 2000s. Git is a distributed VCS and is currently the most widely used.

exam100.a: GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket (also Azure DevOps).

cards.q: What is Greenfield development?
cards.a: Starting a project from scratch with no existing systems, codebases, or constraints. Developers have complete freedom in technology stack, architecture, and design.

cards.q: What is Brownfield development?
cards.a: Working within or building on top of an existing system, often with legacy code, infrastructure, or organizational processes. Involves refactoring, modernizing, or extending existing solutions.

cards.q: Which type of project typically has higher flexibility: Greenfield or Brownfield?
cards.a: Greenfield ‚ÄĒ developers have high freedom to choose any stack or design. Brownfield has low flexibility due to existing integrations.

cards.q: What type of risk is more common in Greenfield vs Brownfield projects?
cards.a: Greenfield: market risk (will it succeed?). Brownfield: technical risk (can we modernize safely?).

cards.q: What skill set is most demanded in Brownfield projects?
cards.a: Strong debugging, refactoring, and system analysis skills.

cards.q: What type of organizations typically do Greenfield development?
cards.a: Startups and new product teams. Brownfield is more common in enterprises and mature industries.

truefalse.q: Greenfield development means working with an existing legacy codebase.
truefalse.exp: Greenfield means starting from scratch ‚ÄĒ "green fields" with no existing constraints.

truefalse.q: Brownfield projects typically involve refactoring, modernizing, or extending existing systems.
truefalse.exp: Correct. Brownfield = previously developed systems, like previously industrial land.

truefalse.q: Greenfield projects have low flexibility in choosing technology stacks.
truefalse.exp: Greenfield has HIGH flexibility ‚ÄĒ developers can choose any stack or design.

truefalse.q: Brownfield development is more common in startups.
truefalse.exp: Brownfield is more common in enterprises and mature industries. Startups typically do Greenfield.

truefalse.q: Technical risk (can we modernize safely?) is the primary risk in Brownfield projects.
truefalse.exp: Correct. Market risk (will it succeed?) is the primary Greenfield risk.

fillinblank.q: ___ development means starting a project from scratch with no existing systems.
___ development involves working on an existing legacy system.
Brownfield requires strong ___, refactoring, and system skills.
Greenfield has ___ flexibility, Brownfield has low flexibility.
Greenfield faces ___ risk; Brownfield faces technical risk.

multiplechoice.q: An e-commerce site needs to keep working while you incrementally replace its legacy checkout system. Which pattern is ideal?
multiplechoice.topic: Green vs Brown / Refactor vs Rewrite
multiplechoice.exp: The Strangler Fig pattern gradually replaces pieces of a legacy system while keeping it running ‚ÄĒ minimal downtime, reversible.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Strangler Fig

multiplechoice.q: A startup builds a new product with no legacy systems. This is:
multiplechoice.exp: Greenfield = new project, no legacy constraints. Brownfield = building within existing systems.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Greenfield development

multiplechoice.q: A monolith is MOST appropriate when:

options.A: You have hundreds of developers worldwide
options.B: You need extreme regional latency optimization
options.C: You are building an MVP with a small team and a tight deadline
options.D: Multiple parts of the system must scale independently

multiplechoice.exp: Monoliths shine for MVPs, small teams, simple domains, and tight deadlines. Large/varied scale favors microservices.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: You are building an MVP with a small team and a tight deadline

cards.q: What is Code Quality?
cards.a: A measure of how well code adheres to standards, practices, and maintainability principles.

cards.q: What is the difference between internal and external code quality?
cards.a: Internal: structure, readability, maintainability. External: correctness, performance, security, usability.

cards.q: List the 7 attributes of code quality from the slides.
cards.a: Readability, Maintainability, Reliability, Performance, Testability, Scalability, Appropriateness.

cards.q: What is a linter?
cards.a: A static analysis tool that analyzes source code to flag potential errors, bugs, stylistic issues, and suspicious constructs. It acts as an automated code reviewer.

cards.q: What is Cyclomatic Complexity?
cards.a: A complexity metric that measures the number of linearly independent paths through a program ‚ÄĒ higher values indicate more complex, harder-to-test code.

cards.q: Name four static analysis tools mentioned in the slides.
cards.a: SonarQube, ESLint, Pylint, PMD.

cards.q: What are examples of "Dead or Redundant Code" flagged by static analysis?
cards.a: Unused variables, unused functions/methods, unreachable code after return/throw, duplicate code fragments.

cards.q: What naming convention issues does static analysis flag?
cards.a: Names not following camelCase/PascalCase/snake_case, single-letter names for non-loop variables, magic numbers/strings instead of named constants.

truefalse.q: External code quality refers to internal structure and readability.
truefalse.exp: External quality is about correctness, performance, security, and usability ‚ÄĒ things the user sees.

truefalse.q: A linter is an automated code reviewer that flags stylistic and error-prone code.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ a linter analyzes source code for issues without executing it.

truefalse.q: SonarQube and ESLint are examples of static analysis tools.
truefalse.exp: Correct. Pylint and PMD are also mentioned in the slides.

truefalse.q: Cyclomatic complexity measures code readability.
truefalse.exp: Cyclomatic complexity measures the number of independent paths through code ‚ÄĒ it's a complexity/testability metric.

truefalse.q: Magic numbers/strings instead of named constants is flagged by static analysis tools.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ "magic" literal values make code harder to understand and maintain.

fillinblank.q: Code Quality measures how well code adheres to standards and ___ principles.
fillinblank.hint: How easy it is to change

fillinblank.q: A ___ is a tool that analyzes source code to flag errors and stylistic issues.
___ complexity measures the number of independent paths through code.
Internal code quality covers structure, readability, and ___.
SonarQube, ESLint, Pylint, and ___ are static analysis tools.

multiplechoice.q: Which BEST defines technical debt?

options.A: Money owed to a software vendor
options.B: The future cost of quick decisions today (shortcuts, hacks)
options.C: Unpaid feature requests in the backlog
options.D: Code that has not yet been deployed

multiplechoice.exp: Technical debt (coined by Ward Cunningham) is the future cost of shortcuts taken to ship faster today.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: The future cost of quick decisions today (shortcuts, hacks)

multiplechoice.q: Which is the BEST kind of cohesion?
multiplechoice.exp: Functional cohesion = all parts work toward one purpose (matches SRP). Coincidental is the worst.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Functional

multiplechoice.q: Which is a code SMELL rather than a bug?

options.A: A null pointer exception in production
options.B: A 600-line method that compiles and runs correctly

multiplechoice.exp: A long method is a SMELL ‚ÄĒ the code works, but the design is weak. Smells suggest deeper issues without being defects themselves.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: A 600-line method that compiles and runs correctly

exam100.q: What is Code Quality?
exam100.a: A measure of how well code adheres to standards, practices, and maintainability principles.

exam100.q: What is the difference between internal and external code quality?
exam100.a: Internal: structure, readability, maintainability. External: correctness, performance, security, usability.

cards.q: What are software patterns?
cards.a: Reusable solutions to recurring problems. Not code copy-paste, but generalizable templates that exist at multiple levels (low, mid, high).

cards.q: What are the three levels of software patterns?
cards.a: Low-level (language idioms/syntax), Mid-level (design patterns like MVC/Observer/Factory), High-level (architecture patterns like Microservices/CQRS).

cards.q: What is a language idiom in programming?
cards.a: A natural, commonly accepted way of solving problems in a specific language ‚ÄĒ makes code readable, concise, and "native-feeling."

cards.q: What is the difference between an Idiom and a Design Pattern?
cards.a: Idioms are language-specific and make code readable. Patterns are language-agnostic and solve architectural/design problems.

cards.q: Give examples of mid-level (design) patterns.
cards.a: Observer, Factory, MVC (Model-View-Controller), Strategy, Builder, Repository, Adapter.

cards.q: Give examples of high-level (architecture) patterns.
cards.a: Microservices, CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation), Onion Architecture.

cards.q: Why use software patterns?
cards.a: They accelerate development by following a proven path, improve communication between developers, and provide tested, reliable solutions.

truefalse.q: Software patterns are meant to be copied and pasted directly into code.
truefalse.exp: Patterns are generalizable templates ‚ÄĒ they guide thinking, not copy-paste solutions.

truefalse.q: MVC (Model-View-Controller) is a mid-level design pattern.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ MVC is a classic mid-level design pattern for structuring applications.

truefalse.q: Microservices is an example of a low-level language idiom.
truefalse.exp: Microservices is a high-level architecture pattern, not a language idiom.

truefalse.q: Idioms are language-agnostic and solve architectural problems.
truefalse.exp: Patterns are language-agnostic. Idioms are language-specific ways of writing natural code.

truefalse.q: The Observer, Factory, and Strategy are examples of design patterns.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ these are well-known mid-level design patterns.

fillinblank.q: Software patterns exist at three levels: low, mid, and ___.
___ are language-specific ways of solving problems naturally in a given language.
Observer, Factory, and MVC are examples of ___ patterns.
Microservices and CQRS are examples of ___ patterns.
Patterns are ___ (not language-specific), while idioms are language-specific.

multiplechoice.q: Which is the BEST description of the Repository pattern's purpose?

options.B: Abstract data-access logic so the rest of the app doesn't care about the storage mechanism

multiplechoice.exp: Repository abstracts storage details (file, DB, API) behind a domain-friendly interface. Improves testability.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Abstract data-access logic so the rest of the app doesn't care about the storage mechanism

exam100.q: What are software patterns?
exam100.a: Reusable solutions to recurring problems. Not code copy-paste, but generalizable templates that exist at multiple levels (low, mid, high).

exam100.q: What are the three levels of software patterns?
exam100.a: Low-level (language idioms/syntax), Mid-level (design patterns like MVC/Observer/Factory), High-level (architecture patterns like Microservices/CQRS).

exam100.q: What is a language idiom in programming?
exam100.a: A natural, commonly accepted way of solving problems in a specific language ‚ÄĒ makes code readable, concise, and "native-feeling."

exam100.q: What is the difference between an Idiom and a Design Pattern?
exam100.a: Idioms are language-specific and make code readable. Patterns are language-agnostic and solve architectural/design problems.

exam100.q: Give examples of mid-level (design) patterns.
exam100.a: Observer, Factory, MVC (Model-View-Controller), Strategy, Builder, Repository, Adapter.

cards.q: What is TDD?
cards.a: A development approach where you write tests BEFORE writing code. Follows the Red-Green-Refactor (RGR) cycle. Tests are not an afterthought.

cards.q: What is the Red-Green-Refactor (RGR) cycle?
cards.a: RED: Write a failing test. GREEN: Write just enough code to make it pass. REFACTOR: Clean up the code while keeping tests passing.

cards.q: Who founded TDD and when?
cards.a: Kent Beck established TDD in 2002 with his book "Test-Driven Development: By Example." Rooted in Extreme Programming (XP).

cards.q: What are the six types of testing covered in the slides?
cards.a: Unit, Integration, Regression, Stress/Load, End-To-End (E2E), UI/Usability.

cards.q: What is the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern?
cards.a: A testing pattern that divides each test into three sections: Arrange (set up data), Act (invoke the function), Assert (verify the result).

cards.q: When should you NOT use TDD?
cards.a: Exploratory or prototype development, highly visual/UI-intensive apps, frequently changing requirements, or when high uncertainty exists.

cards.q: What software patterns are a GOOD fit for TDD?
cards.a: Strategy, Repository, Dependency Injection, Factory, Builder, Adapter, Observer ‚ÄĒ they enable natural decoupling and clear boundaries.

cards.q: What software patterns are a BAD fit for TDD?
cards.a: Singleton, Service Locator, Facade ‚ÄĒ they tend to create tight coupling that makes testing difficult.

truefalse.q: In TDD, you write code first and then write tests to verify it.
truefalse.exp: TDD flips the workflow ‚ÄĒ tests are written BEFORE the code, not after.

truefalse.q: The RGR cycle stands for Red-Green-Refactor.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ Red (failing test) ‚Üí Green (passing test) ‚Üí Refactor (clean code).

truefalse.q: Kent Beck formalized TDD in 2002.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ his book "Test-Driven Development: By Example" established the foundation.

truefalse.q: TDD is ideal for highly visual, UI-intensive applications.
truefalse.exp: TDD is NOT recommended for highly visual/UI-intensive apps ‚ÄĒ visual components are hard to unit test.

truefalse.q: The Singleton pattern is a good fit for TDD.
truefalse.exp: Singleton is listed as a BAD fit ‚ÄĒ it creates global state that makes isolated testing very difficult.

fillinblank.q: TDD stands for Test-___ Development.
The RGR cycle is: Red, Green, and ___.
The ___ pattern divides tests into Arrange, Act, and Assert sections.
TDD was founded by Kent ___ in 2002.
TDD encourages writing only what's needed to fulfill a ___.

multiplechoice.q: A team writes its tests AFTER the code is complete, just to confirm everything works. Is this TDD?

options.A: Yes ‚ÄĒ any unit testing is TDD
options.B: No ‚ÄĒ TDD writes tests BEFORE the code
options.C: Only if the tests pass on the first run
options.D: Only if integration tests are also included

multiplechoice.exp: TDD inverts the workflow: write a failing test (RED) ‚Üí write minimum code to pass (GREEN) ‚Üí refactor. Tests after code is NOT TDD.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: No ‚ÄĒ TDD writes tests BEFORE the code

multiplechoice.q: You are choosing patterns and want them to support TDD well. Which combination is BEST?
multiplechoice.exp: DI, Strategy, and Repository decouple dependencies and allow easy mocking ‚ÄĒ ideal for TDD. The first option HIDES dependencies.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Dependency Injection + Strategy + Repository

multiplechoice.q: Which BEST describes the difference between Mocks and Stubs?

options.A: Mocks return canned data; stubs record interactions
options.B: Stubs return canned data; mocks record how they were used

multiplechoice.exp: Stubs return canned/fixed values. Mocks RECORD interactions, letting tests assert on call patterns.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Stubs return canned data; mocks record how they were used

multiplechoice.q: Which is a common test framework for Java?
multiplechoice.exp: JUnit (and TestNG) for Java; xUnit/NUnit/MSTest for C#; Jest/Mocha for JavaScript.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: JUnit

multiplechoice.q: A test that exercises the FULL stack ‚ÄĒ UI ‚Üí API ‚Üí DB ‚ÄĒ by simulating real user behavior is:
multiplechoice.exp: E2E tests cover user journeys across the full system. Slower, but essential for critical workflows.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: End-to-End (E2E) test

exam100.q: What is TDD?
exam100.a: A development approach where you write tests BEFORE writing code. Follows the Red-Green-Refactor (RGR) cycle. Tests are not an afterthought.

exam100.q: What is the Red-Green-Refactor (RGR) cycle?
exam100.a: RED: Write a failing test. GREEN: Write just enough code to make it pass. REFACTOR: Clean up the code while keeping tests passing.

exam100.q: Who founded TDD and when?
exam100.a: Kent Beck established TDD in 2002 with his book "Test-Driven Development: By Example." Rooted in Extreme Programming (XP).

exam100.q: What are the six types of testing covered in the slides?
exam100.a: Unit, Integration, Regression, Stress/Load, End-To-End (E2E), UI/Usability.

exam100.q: What is the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern?
exam100.a: A testing pattern that divides each test into three sections: Arrange (set up data), Act (invoke the function), Assert (verify the result).

cards.q: What is a software process model?
cards.a: A framework that defines how a software project is planned, developed, tested, and delivered. Used to manage people, time, cost, and quality.

cards.q: What are the phases of the Waterfall model?
cards.a: Requirements ‚Üí Design ‚Üí Implementation ‚Üí Testing ‚Üí Deployment ‚Üí Maintenance. Each phase must be complete before the next begins.

cards.q: When should you use Waterfall?
cards.a: Requirements are clear/fixed, client unavailable for feedback, regulated industries (aerospace, defense, medical), strong documentation emphasis.

cards.q: When should you use Agile?
cards.a: Requirements are unclear or evolving, frequent iterations needed, collaborative self-managed team, MVPs, startups, R&D, SaaS.

cards.q: What is a Hybrid/Spiral process model?
cards.a: Combines elements of Waterfall and Agile ‚ÄĒ iterative cycles with structured phases, balancing flexibility and documentation.

cards.q: What is DevOps in the context of process models?
cards.a: A process model that combines development and operations, emphasizing continuous integration, delivery, and deployment (CI/CD).

cards.q: What are the Agile core practices listed in the slides?
cards.a: Scrum, Kanban, XP, Scrumban, continuous delivery, frequent retrospectives, cross-functional teams.

truefalse.q: In Waterfall, you can start Implementation before Requirements are complete.
truefalse.exp: Waterfall is strictly linear ‚ÄĒ each phase must be complete before the next begins.

truefalse.q: Agile is best used when requirements are unclear or evolving.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ Agile is designed for environments where requirements change frequently.

truefalse.q: Waterfall is ideal for startups and R&D teams.
truefalse.exp: Agile/iterative approaches are better for startups. Waterfall suits regulated industries with fixed requirements.

truefalse.q: DevOps emphasizes continuous integration, delivery, and deployment.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ DevOps bridges development and operations through CI/CD pipelines.

truefalse.q: A Hybrid/Spiral model combines elements of Waterfall and Agile.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ Spiral/Hybrid models blend structured phases with iterative flexibility.

multiplechoice.exp: Dogfooding ('eat your own dog food') means employees use the product themselves to catch bugs and build empathy.

multiplechoice.exp: Waterfall: sequential phases, fixed plan. Agile: iterative cycles, embrace change. Aerospace tends Waterfall; startups tend Agile.

cards.q: What does SDLC stand for and what is it?
cards.a: Software Development Life Cycle. A structured methodology for developing software through defined phases.

cards.q: What does ALM stand for and what is it?
cards.a: Application Lifecycle Management. A broader discipline encompassing the complete lifespan of an application from inception to retirement.

cards.q: How does SDLC relate to ALM?
cards.a: SDLC is a subset of ALM. ALM extends beyond development to include planning, release, operations, governance, and sunset/retirement.

cards.q: What are the stages of ALM?
cards.a: Ideation ‚Üí Planning ‚Üí Development (SDLC) ‚Üí Release ‚Üí Operations ‚Üí Governance ‚Üí Sunset.

cards.q: What is the real-world analogy for SDLC vs ALM?
cards.a: SDLC = designing and building a car in the factory. ALM = managing the car's full lifecycle: manufacturing, selling, servicing, upgrading, and decommissioning.

cards.q: What are the benefits of SDLC?
cards.a: Clearly defined phases, project tracking and accountability, quality assurance through testing, easier developer onboarding.

cards.q: What are the benefits of ALM?
cards.a: End-to-end traceability, better visibility for product managers, enables auditing/regulatory compliance, supports product roadmap planning.

truefalse.q: ALM is a subset of SDLC.
truefalse.exp: It's the opposite ‚ÄĒ SDLC is a subset of ALM. ALM is the broader discipline.

truefalse.q: SDLC phases include Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, and Maintenance.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ these are the key phases of the Software Development Life Cycle.

truefalse.q: ALM covers only the development phase of an application.
truefalse.exp: ALM covers the entire lifespan: Ideation ‚Üí Planning ‚Üí Development ‚Üí Release ‚Üí Operations ‚Üí Governance ‚Üí Sunset.

truefalse.q: SDLC helps with project tracking, accountability, and quality assurance.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ these are listed as key benefits of using SDLC.

truefalse.q: Managing a banking platform across multiple regulatory regions is an ALM use case.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ ALM is suited for long-lived, complex, regulated systems.

cards.q: What was the old definition of a Full Stack Developer (2010s)?
cards.a: "One person who builds the entire web app ‚ÄĒ HTML/CSS, JS, backend, and database."

cards.q: What is the modern definition of a Full Stack Developer (2020s)?
cards.a: "An engineer who understands and can navigate multiple layers of the stack, even if they specialize in one."

cards.q: What is a T-shaped developer?
cards.a: A developer with broad knowledge across multiple layers of the stack but deep expertise in one specific area.

cards.q: What are the three tiers of the full stack?
cards.a: Front-end (client/UI), Middle tier (API/business logic), and Back-end (database/persistence).

cards.q: What is "Full Stack + DevOps" sometimes called?
cards.a: FSD ‚ÄĒ Full Stack Developer expanded to include DevOps, CI/CD, and sometimes cloud knowledge.

cards.q: Why does Full Stack development matter for debugging?
cards.a: A full-stack mindset lets you trace bugs through the entire request-response lifecycle ‚ÄĒ from UI assumption to API error to inefficient query.

cards.q: What were predecessor technologies before the full stack era (1970s-1990s)?
cards.a: Monolithic (one-tier) applications, client-server model (two-tier, n-tier), mainframe terminals, desktop apps with direct DB access, early web with HTML + CGI scripts.

truefalse.q: Modern Full Stack Developers must build the entire app alone.
truefalse.exp: Modern definition: they navigate multiple layers and understand them, even if they specialize in one.

truefalse.q: A T-shaped developer has broad knowledge across layers but is deep in one area.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ the "T" shape: wide horizontal bar = broad knowledge, vertical bar = deep specialty.

truefalse.q: Full Stack development reduces blind spots between front-end, API, and database teams.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ holistic understanding helps developers see how layers interact.

truefalse.q: CGI (Common Gateway Interface) was a modern full-stack technology from the 2020s.
truefalse.exp: CGI was an early web technology from the 1990s ‚ÄĒ each request launched a new process.

truefalse.q: Full Stack developers can trace issues end-to-end from UI to database.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ this cross-layer visibility is a key benefit of full-stack knowledge.

The three tiers are front-end, ___, and back-end.

multiplechoice.exp: SPAs load once, then dynamically update the page (Gmail, Notion, Slack). SSR pages are generated per request.

cards.q: What is an API?
cards.a: Application Programming Interface ‚ÄĒ a defined way for software systems to communicate. It encapsulates complexity and exposes controlled functionality.

cards.q: What is REST and who defined it?
cards.a: REpresentational State Transfer, defined by Roy Fielding in 2000 in his dissertation. It is stateless, uses HTTP, and is resource-based (nouns, not verbs).

cards.q: What are the 5 REST principles?
cards.a:
1) Stateless communication
2) Client-server separation
3) Uniform interface
4) Resource-based (nouns)
5) Cacheable responses

cards.q: What is SOAP and what does it use?
cards.a: Simple Object Access Protocol ‚ÄĒ uses XML + WSDL (Web Services Description Language). Strong typing but verbose and brittle.

cards.q: What are the common HTTP verbs used in REST?
cards.a: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE

cards.q: What are GraphQL and gRPC, and which companies developed them?
cards.a: GraphQL was developed by Facebook for structured querying. gRPC was developed by Google using binary transport ‚ÄĒ both are 2020s-era API technologies.

cards.q: What is RPC (Remote Procedure Call)?
cards.a: An early (1980s-90s) protocol that allows one program to execute code on another machine as if it were local. Foundation for many distributed systems.

cards.q: What is the restaurant menu analogy for APIs?
cards.a: An API is like a restaurant menu ‚ÄĒ it lists what you can request (the interface) but hides how the kitchen (implementation) actually works.

truefalse.q: REST was defined by Roy Fielding in 2000.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ Fielding described REST in his doctoral dissertation at UC Irvine.

truefalse.q: REST APIs use verbs in URLs (e.g., /getUser, /deletePost).
truefalse.exp: REST is resource-based (nouns) ‚ÄĒ e.g., GET /users/1 rather than /getUser/1.

truefalse.q: SOAP uses JSON for data exchange.
truefalse.exp: SOAP uses XML with WSDL. JSON/RESTful APIs came later and are more human-readable.

truefalse.q: GraphQL was developed by Facebook.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ Facebook developed GraphQL for structured data querying.

truefalse.q: REST requires the server to store session state between requests.
truefalse.exp: REST is stateless ‚ÄĒ each request contains all information needed; the server stores no session state.

multiplechoice.q: Which of these REST verbs is NOT idempotent?
multiplechoice.exp: POST creates new resources on each call ‚ÄĒ not idempotent. GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: POST

options.B: Authentication = who you are; Authorization = what you can do

cards.q: What is Domain-Driven Design (DDD) and who introduced it?
cards.a: A strategic and architectural philosophy that lets the business domain drive software structure (not technology or database). Introduced by Eric Evans in 2003.

cards.q: What are the DDD Building Blocks?
cards.a: Entities (identity), Value Objects (immutable, identity by value), Aggregates & Aggregate Roots, Repositories, Services, Events.

cards.q: What is a DDD Entity?
cards.a: An object with a unique identity that persists over time ‚ÄĒ e.g., Player, Order, Customer. Identity is what makes two entities different even if their attributes are the same.

cards.q: What is a DDD Value Object?
cards.a: An immutable object identified by its value, not an ID ‚ÄĒ e.g., Money($10), Coordinates(lat, lng). Best for enforcing semantic typing.

cards.q: What is a DDD Aggregate Root?
cards.a: The root entity of an Aggregate cluster. All access to the aggregate goes through the root, which enforces consistency boundaries.

cards.q: What is the "city plan vs. buildings" analogy for DDD vs. patterns?
cards.a: DDD is the city plan (how neighborhoods connect, where roads go). Patterns are the individual buildings (how each is constructed). DDD tells you which patterns to use and where.

cards.q: What is ubiquitous language in DDD?
cards.a: A shared vocabulary between developers and domain experts, used consistently in both code and conversation to model the business domain accurately.

cards.q: What does SOLID stand for?
cards.a: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion.

truefalse.q: DDD (Domain-Driven Design) is itself a design pattern.
truefalse.exp: DDD is a strategic and architectural philosophy ‚ÄĒ it uses patterns but is not itself one.

truefalse.q: Eric Evans introduced DDD in 2003.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ in his book "Domain-Driven Design: Tackling Complexity in the Heart of Software."

truefalse.q: A DDD Value Object has a unique ID that persists over time.
truefalse.exp: Value Objects are identified by their value, not an ID. Entities have persistent identity.

truefalse.q: The Ubiquitous Language in DDD is a shared vocabulary between devs and domain experts.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ this shared language bridges the gap between technical and business understanding.

truefalse.q: An Aggregate Root controls all access to its cluster of entities.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ all interactions with the aggregate go through the root to maintain consistency.

multiplechoice.q: Which is the BEST description of a Value Object in DDD?

options.A: A database table with auto-incrementing IDs
options.B: An immutable object identified by its values, not identity
options.D: A class that handles dependency injection

multiplechoice.exp: Value Objects (e.g., Money, Coordinates) are immutable and identified by their values. Entities have identity (e.g., User #42).
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: An immutable object identified by its values, not identity

multiplechoice.q: What does the 'I' in SOLID stand for?
multiplechoice.exp: Interface Segregation: no client should be forced to depend on methods it does not use. Prefer many small interfaces.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Interface Segregation

multiplechoice.q: Which BEST describes the Open/Closed Principle?

options.A: Open for refactoring, closed for modification
options.B: Open for extension, closed for modification
options.C: Open for inheritance, closed for composition
options.D: Open for users, closed for developers

multiplechoice.exp: Open for EXTENSION (via abstraction/inheritance), closed for MODIFICATION (don't edit existing code).
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Open for extension, closed for modification

multiplechoice.q: A class is responsible for authentication, sending emails, AND logging. Which SOLID principle is violated?
multiplechoice.exp: Single Responsibility says a class should do one thing well. Three unrelated jobs violates SRP.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Single Responsibility (S)

multiplechoice.q: Which BEST describes the difference between an Entity and a Value Object in DDD?

options.A: Entity has identity; Value Object is immutable and identified by value
options.B: Entity is immutable; Value Object has identity

multiplechoice.exp: Entity = identity (User #42). Value Object = immutable, identity by value (Money($5)).
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Entity has identity; Value Object is immutable and identified by value

multiplechoice.q: Which BEST describes a Bounded Context in DDD?

options.B: A sub-section of the domain with its own model and language

multiplechoice.exp: Bounded Contexts split large domains into autonomous areas (e.g., Inventory vs Sales) each with its own model.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: A sub-section of the domain with its own model and language

multiplechoice.q: Which is FALSE about DDD?

options.B: It uses Entities, Value Objects, and Aggregates
options.C: It is itself a single design pattern
options.D: It uses Bounded Contexts to split large domains

multiplechoice.exp: DDD is NOT a pattern. It is a strategic/architectural philosophy that tells you WHICH patterns to use.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: It is itself a single design pattern

multiplechoice.q: Which describes the Open Closed Principle's typical implementation strategy?

options.B: Favor abstraction and inheritance/composition over modifying existing code

multiplechoice.exp: OCP encourages adding behavior via new types (abstraction, polymorphism), not by modifying existing classes.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Favor abstraction and inheritance/composition over modifying existing code

exam100.a: A strategic and architectural philosophy that lets the business domain drive software structure (not technology or database). Introduced by Eric Evans in 2003.

exam100.q: What are the DDD Building Blocks?

exam100.q: What is a DDD Entity?

cards.q: What is persistence in software?
cards.a: Persistence is the art of making data outlive the program that created it ‚ÄĒ saving state so it survives beyond a single program execution.

cards.q: What is the difference between volatile and non-volatile memory?
cards.a: Volatile (RAM): disappears when power is off. Non-volatile (Disk, SSD, Flash): survives power loss. Persistence relies on non-volatile storage.

cards.q: What does the persistence layer do?
cards.a: Manages where and how data is stored and retrieved. Converts in-memory objects to storable formats and back. Handles save, update, delete, query. Protects upper layers from storage details.

cards.q: How does the persistence layer fit in layered architecture?
cards.a: UI ‚Üí Application ‚Üí Domain ‚Üí Persistence ‚Üí Infrastructure. The repository hides whether data lives in a file, database, or API.

cards.q: Why do we need persistence?
cards.a: When a program ends, its variables disappear. Persistence saves state ‚ÄĒ users, settings, progress, messages ‚ÄĒ so it survives between program runs.

cards.q: Give real-world examples of persistence.
cards.a: Game saves (player progress ‚Üí file on disk), Contacts app (names/numbers ‚Üí internal database), Notes app (text ‚Üí cloud or file), Web browser (history/cookies ‚Üí local files or databases).

truefalse.q: RAM is considered non-volatile storage.
truefalse.exp: RAM is volatile ‚ÄĒ it loses all data when the power is off. SSDs and disks are non-volatile.

truefalse.q: The persistence layer protects upper layers from knowing how storage works.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ this abstraction is a key purpose of the persistence layer.

truefalse.q: Persistence allows data to outlive the program that created it.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ this is the definition of persistence from the slides.

truefalse.q: In layered architecture, the UI layer directly accesses the database.
truefalse.exp: The UI calls the Application layer, which calls the Domain/Repository, which calls the Persistence layer.

truefalse.q: Cloud storage behaves the same as local non-volatile storage.
truefalse.exp: The slides note that "cloud storage feels different" ‚ÄĒ it has its own abstraction and failure modes.

fillinblank.q: Persistence is the art of making data ___ the program that created it.
___ memory (RAM) disappears when power is off.
Non-___ memory like SSDs and disks survives power loss.
The layered architecture order is: UI ‚Üí Application ‚Üí Domain ‚Üí ___ ‚Üí Infrastructure.
A ___ hides whether data lives in a file, database, or accessed via API.

multiplechoice.q: Which ACID property guarantees that, after a successful commit, data is NOT lost even if power fails?
multiplechoice.exp: Durability: once a transaction is confirmed, it stays saved (even across crashes).
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Durability

exam100.q: What is persistence in software?
exam100.a: Persistence is the art of making data outlive the program that created it ‚ÄĒ saving state so it survives beyond a single program execution.

exam100.q: What is the difference between volatile and non-volatile memory?
exam100.a: Volatile (RAM): disappears when power is off. Non-volatile (Disk, SSD, Flash): survives power loss. Persistence relies on non-volatile storage.

exam100.q: What does the persistence layer do?
exam100.a: Manages where and how data is stored and retrieved. Converts in-memory objects to storable formats and back. Handles save, update, delete, query. Protects upper layers from storage details.

exam100.q: How does the persistence layer fit in layered architecture?
exam100.a: UI ‚Üí Application ‚Üí Domain ‚Üí Persistence ‚Üí Infrastructure. The repository hides whether data lives in a file, database, or API.

exam100.q: Why do we need persistence?
exam100.a: When a program ends, its variables disappear. Persistence saves state ‚ÄĒ users, settings, progress, messages ‚ÄĒ so it survives between program runs.

cards.q: What is synchronous execution?
cards.a: Tasks run one after another ‚ÄĒ each must finish before the next starts. Simple and predictable but can be slow. Analogy: waiting in line.

cards.q: What is asynchronous execution?
cards.a: Tasks can start and finish later ‚ÄĒ work continues while waiting. Improves responsiveness and efficiency. Analogy: starting laundry while doing other things.

cards.q: What is the difference between blocking and non-blocking?
cards.a: Blocking: program halts until a task completes. Non-blocking: can start other work while waiting. Analogy: one chef cooks while others wait (blocking) vs. everyone multitasking (non-blocking).

cards.q: What is AJAX?
cards.a: Asynchronous JavaScript and XML. Fetches data without reloading the page. Originally used XMLHttpRequest; evolved to use fetch() or Axios/React Query.

cards.q: What is a thread?
cards.a: The smallest unit of execution inside a program. Multiple threads allow concurrent execution within a single process.

cards.q: What are the tradeoffs of async vs sync?
cards.a: Sync: simpler, predictable, slower under load. Async: requires callbacks/events/promises, scales better, more complex.

cards.q: Give an example of AJAX in JavaScript.
cards.a: fetch("/api/data").then(r => r.json()).then(showData); ‚ÄĒ fetches data from a server without reloading the page.

truefalse.q: In synchronous execution, the next task starts before the current one finishes.
truefalse.exp: Synchronous means each task must FINISH before the next one starts.

truefalse.q: Asynchronous programming improves responsiveness by not blocking while waiting.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ async allows other work to continue while waiting for I/O or network responses.

truefalse.q: AJAX stands for Asynchronous JavaScript and XML.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ though modern AJAX typically uses JSON, not XML.

truefalse.q: Blocking I/O allows a program to do other work while waiting for a response.
truefalse.exp: Blocking I/O HALTS the program until the task completes. Non-blocking allows other work.

truefalse.q: A thread is the smallest unit of execution inside a program.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ threads are what allow concurrent execution within a process.

fillinblank.q: In ___ execution, tasks run one after another with no overlap.
___ execution allows tasks to start and finish later while other work continues.
AJAX stands for Asynchronous JavaScript and ___.
A ___ program halts until a task completes before continuing.
A ___ is the smallest unit of execution inside a program.

multiplechoice.q: Two threads update the same balance variable. Without synchronization, the final value is unpredictable. This is called a:
multiplechoice.exp: Race condition: outcome depends on the timing of concurrent accesses to shared data. Prevent with locks, immutability, message passing, etc.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Race condition

multiplechoice.q: Which describes ASYNCHRONOUS execution?

options.A: Tasks run one after another, each waiting
options.B: Tasks can start, then finish later; work continues while waiting
options.D: All tasks run in parallel by default

multiplechoice.exp: Async = non-blocking; the caller continues other work while waiting. Sync = step-by-step, blocking.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Tasks can start, then finish later; work continues while waiting

multiplechoice.q: Which BEST distinguishes a Queue from a Pub/Sub model?

options.A: Queue is one-to-many; Pub/Sub is one-to-one
options.B: Queue is one-to-one; Pub/Sub is one-to-many
options.D: Queues use HTTP only; Pub/Sub uses TCP

multiplechoice.exp: Queues deliver one message to one consumer; Pub/Sub fans out to all subscribers.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Queue is one-to-one; Pub/Sub is one-to-many

cards.q: What is the historical timeline of cross-platform development?
cards.a: Assembly (hardware-specific) ‚Üí C (compile anywhere) ‚Üí Java/JVM 1995 (write once, run anywhere) ‚Üí .NET CLR, Flash, Qt (2000s) ‚Üí Flutter, React Native, WebAssembly (2010s+)

cards.q: What is the JVM and what does it enable?
cards.a: Java Virtual Machine ‚ÄĒ executes platform-neutral bytecode. Enables "write once, run anywhere." Supports Java, Kotlin, Scala, Groovy.

cards.q: What is the difference between Native, Bytecode, and Interpreted execution?
cards.a: Native: compiled directly for CPU (C/C++). Bytecode: intermediate form executed by VM (Java, .NET). Interpreted: read line-by-line (Python, JS). Hybrid (JIT/AOT): compiles some code at runtime.

cards.q: What is WebAssembly (WASM)?
cards.a: A binary instruction format that allows code written in languages like C++, Rust, or Go to run in web browsers at near-native speed.

cards.q: What are examples of cross-platform frameworks?
cards.a: Flutter (Google), React Native (Meta), MAUI (.NET), Kotlin Multiplatform.

cards.q: What are examples of native development tools?
cards.a: SwiftUI for iOS/macOS, Kotlin Compose for Android.

cards.q: What inspired .NET CLR and Android ART/Dalvik?
cards.a: Both were inspired by the JVM concept ‚ÄĒ intermediate bytecode executed by a virtual machine. .NET uses Intermediate Language (IL); Android uses ART/Dalvik for mobile.

truefalse.q: Java was designed with "write once, run anywhere" as a key goal.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ the JVM enables Java bytecode to run on any platform that has a JVM.

truefalse.q: Native applications run faster than interpreted code.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ native code is compiled directly to machine code, which is the fastest execution method.

truefalse.q: Flutter and React Native are examples of native development frameworks.
truefalse.exp: Flutter and React Native are cross-platform frameworks. SwiftUI and Kotlin Compose are native.

truefalse.q: WebAssembly allows near-native performance in web browsers.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ WASM is a binary format that enables high-performance code in browsers.

truefalse.q: Interpreted code (Python, JavaScript) is compiled to machine code before execution.
truefalse.exp: Interpreted code is read line-by-line at runtime. Native code is compiled to machine code ahead of time.

multiplechoice.exp: Electron bundles Chromium + Node.js. Used by VS Code, Slack, Discord, Notion, Postman.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Electron

cards.q: What is refactoring?
cards.a: Improving the internal structure of code WITHOUT changing its external behavior. Examples: simplify functions, rename variables, extract classes, add tests. "Clean up the house without moving out."

cards.q: What is rewriting?
cards.a: Rebuilding the system from scratch, often with new tech or design principles. Examples: replacing PHP with Node.js, monolith ‚Üí microservices. "Demolish and rebuild ‚ÄĒ same address, new foundation."

cards.q: Why do teams refactor code?
cards.a: Code smells (long methods, duplication), hard to add features safely, poor readability, no tests, gradual performance degradation. Goal: incremental improvement without breaking behavior.

cards.q: Why do teams rewrite software?
cards.a: Tech stack is obsolete, architecture can't scale, legacy frameworks/security issues, team doesn't understand old code, integrating new platforms.

cards.q: Compare refactor vs rewrite on risk and cost.
cards.a: Refactor: lower risk, lower upfront cost, incremental delivery. Rewrite: higher risk, high upfront cost, all-at-once delivery.

cards.q: What are "code smells"?
cards.a: Indicators of poor code quality that suggest refactoring is needed ‚ÄĒ e.g., long methods, code duplication, tangled logic, inconsistent style, no tests.

truefalse.q: Refactoring changes the external behavior of the software.
truefalse.exp: Refactoring improves INTERNAL structure while preserving external behavior ‚ÄĒ this is its core definition.

truefalse.q: A rewrite is lower risk than refactoring.
truefalse.exp: Rewrites are HIGHER risk ‚ÄĒ they involve rebuilding from scratch, with the danger of losing features and introducing new bugs.

truefalse.q: Migrating from a monolith to microservices is an example of a rewrite.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ this is a significant architectural change that constitutes a rewrite.

truefalse.q: Code smells indicate that refactoring may be needed.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ code smells like long methods, duplication, and tangled logic signal refactoring opportunities.

truefalse.q: Refactoring delivers improvements incrementally over time.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ refactoring is incremental, unlike rewrites which tend to be all-at-once.

fillinblank.q: Refactoring improves internal structure without changing ___ behavior.
A ___ rebuilds the system from scratch with new tech or design.
Code smells like long methods and ___ code signal the need for refactoring.
Refactor has ___ risk; rewrite has higher risk.
Replacing PHP with Node.js or migrating monolith ‚Üí microservices is an example of a ___.

cards.q: What is defensive programming?
cards.a: A mindset and set of techniques that make software behave predictably even under unexpected conditions. Goal: anticipate failures, prevent/limit damage, communicate errors clearly.

cards.q: What are the key principles of defensive programming?
cards.a: Validate all inputs, avoid assumptions about data/environment/state, fail fast and clearly, use guard clauses instead of deep nesting, handle edge cases (nulls, empty arrays, limits), minimize side effects.

cards.q: What does "fail fast, fail clearly" mean?
cards.a: When an error occurs, detect it immediately and communicate it explicitly (via exceptions, asserts) rather than silently continuing in an invalid state.

cards.q: What is a guard clause?
cards.a: An early return or check at the start of a function that handles invalid/edge-case inputs immediately, avoiding deep nesting (if-else trees).

cards.q: Why does defensive programming matter?
cards.a: Prevents subtle bugs and crashes, saves debugging time, improves maintainability, reduces security vulnerabilities, makes code safer to modify or reuse.

cards.q: What is the core philosophy of defensive programming?
cards.a: "Hope for the best, code for the worst." Good code "fails safe," not "fails hard."

truefalse.q: Defensive programming assumes all inputs will be valid.
truefalse.exp: Defensive programming assumes inputs may be INVALID and validates them explicitly.

truefalse.q: Guard clauses help avoid deep nesting by handling edge cases early.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ a guard clause returns early for invalid conditions instead of wrapping code in nested ifs.

truefalse.q: "Fail fast, fail clearly" means silently ignoring errors and continuing.
truefalse.exp: "Fail fast" means detecting errors IMMEDIATELY and communicating them explicitly ‚ÄĒ the opposite of silent failures.

truefalse.q: Defensive programming complements testing but does not replace it.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ from the slides: it "complements testing, not replaces it."

truefalse.q: Good defensive code "fails safe" rather than "fails hard."
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ the goal is controlled, predictable failure rather than crashes or undefined behavior.

fillinblank.q: The core philosophy: "Hope for the best, ___ for the worst."
Defensive programming: ___ all inputs from users, APIs, and files.
Use ___ clauses instead of deep nesting to handle edge cases early.
"Fail ___, fail clearly" ‚ÄĒ detect errors immediately and communicate them.
Defensive programming reduces security ___ by catching invalid inputs.

multiplechoice.q: A function has many nested if statements that check for valid inputs before doing the real work. Which defensive technique should refactor this?
multiplechoice.exp: Guard clauses do early returns for invalid conditions, flattening deep nesting and reducing missed branches.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Guard clauses (early returns)

multiplechoice.q: Defensive programming and testing differ in that:

options.A: Testing replaces the need for defensive code
options.B: Defensive programming prevents problems inside the code; testing detects them afterwards

multiplechoice.exp: Defensive code prevents bad states; tests detect them. They complement each other.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Defensive programming prevents problems inside the code; testing detects them afterwards

cards.q: What is a feature flag?
cards.a: A switch that controls whether a feature is enabled. The code exists even when the feature is hidden or disabled. Flags can be changed by environment, user, role, tier, region, or runtime condition.

cards.q: What is the difference between deployment and release?
cards.a: Deployment: code is installed in an environment. Release: users are allowed to access it. Feature flags let these happen at different times.

cards.q: What are the 6 common categories of feature flags?
cards.a: Release flags, Experiment flags, Operations flags, Permission/entitlement flags, Tier/subscription flags, Kill switches.

cards.q: What are release flags used for?
cards.a: To hide new functionality until it is ready. Common for UI redesigns, new workflows, new services. Allows code to be merged and deployed before public availability.

cards.q: What are experiment flags used for?
cards.a: To compare multiple versions of a feature ‚ÄĒ A/B testing or controlled evaluation of layouts, flows, algorithms, or engagement strategies.

cards.q: What is a kill switch?
cards.a: A flag category that allows a feature to be rapidly disabled if it causes problems in production ‚ÄĒ providing an emergency off switch.

cards.q: What are the common reasons to use feature flags?
cards.a: Hide incomplete work, reduce risk during rollout, run experiments, limit features to certain users, support premium tiers, disable unstable functionality quickly.

truefalse.q: When a feature flag is off, the feature's code is deleted from the codebase.
truefalse.exp: The code EXISTS even when the feature is hidden or disabled ‚ÄĒ the flag just controls whether it's active.

truefalse.q: Deployment and release always happen at the same time.
truefalse.exp: Feature flags allow deployment and release to happen at DIFFERENT times ‚ÄĒ this is one of their key benefits.

truefalse.q: Experiment flags are used for A/B testing different feature versions.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ experiment flags compare multiple versions for controlled evaluation.

truefalse.q: A kill switch is a type of feature flag that disables functionality quickly.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ kill switches provide an emergency off switch for problematic features.

truefalse.q: Feature flags can be changed based on user, role, region, or runtime condition.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ this granular control is what makes feature flags so powerful.

fillinblank.q: A feature flag is a ___ that controls whether a feature is enabled.
Deployment means code is installed; ___ means users can access it.
Experiment flags are used for ___ testing (comparing two versions).
A ___ switch is a flag that rapidly disables a problematic feature.
___ flags hide new functionality until it is ready for users.

multiplechoice.q: Which is a HIGH-priority feature flag used to quickly shut off a misbehaving feature in production?
multiplechoice.exp: Kill switches are emergency off-switches for features causing problems. Faster than a redeploy.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Kill switch

multiplechoice.q: Which feature flag type would you use to compare two layouts and choose the one with better engagement?
multiplechoice.exp: Experiment flags drive A/B tests ‚ÄĒ comparing variants on real user behavior.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Experiment flag

multiplechoice.q: Which is NOT a feature flag category?
multiplechoice.exp: Common types include release, experiment, operations, permission/entitlement, tier, and kill switches. 'Authentication flag' is not a standard category.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Authentication flag

multiplechoice.q: Which is TRUE about feature flags?

options.A: Hiding a button in the UI is sufficient access control
options.B: They allow deployment and release to happen at different times
options.C: They eliminate the need for testing
options.D: Old flags should never be removed

multiplechoice.exp: Decouple DEPLOY (code present) from RELEASE (users granted access). UI hiding is NOT security; backend enforces.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: They allow deployment and release to happen at different times

cards.q: What does MVP stand for and what does it really mean?
cards.a: Minimum Viable Product. Minimum = only essential functionality. Viable = reliable, stable, demonstrable. Product = can be handed off, shown, or used. NOT a prototype.

cards.q: What is the "Late-Stage Trap" near the end of a project?
cards.a: Common behaviors: adding "just one more feature," neglecting documentation/polish, ignoring testing debt, over-optimizing things users will never see.

cards.q: What is a Code Freeze?
cards.a: A period when no new features are added ‚ÄĒ only fixes, tests, or documentation updates are allowed.

cards.q: What are the 3 questions to ask when deciding what to cut or postpone?
cards.a:
1) Does this directly improve the user's core experience?
2) Can we test it in time?
3) Does it risk breaking something stable?
If no ‚Üí move to "Future Enhancements."

cards.q: What are the priorities for the Final Sprint?
cards.a: All intended features working, all problematic features addressed (fixed/modified/workaround/removed), comprehensive regression testing, prepare demo platform, all team members familiar with all layers.

cards.q: Why is "Cutting is not quitting"?
cards.a: Cutting features during code freeze is about focusing ‚ÄĒ delivering a smaller but working product beats a large unfinished one.

truefalse.q: MVP means building as many features as possible before launch.
truefalse.exp: MVP = MINIMUM viable ‚ÄĒ only essential functionality needed for the user stories.

truefalse.q: A Code Freeze allows new features to be added as long as they're small.
truefalse.exp: Code Freeze means NO new features ‚ÄĒ only fixes, tests, and documentation updates.

truefalse.q: "Cutting is not quitting" ‚ÄĒ cutting features during code freeze is a form of focus.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ a smaller working product beats a large unfinished one.

truefalse.q: A viable MVP must be reliable, stable, and demonstrable.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ "Viable" means it can actually be shown or handed off.

truefalse.q: During the final sprint, only the lead developer needs to test all parts.
truefalse.exp: ALL team members should test all parts and be familiar with all layers.

options.C: Stabilize the build ‚ÄĒ only fixes, tests, and doc updates allowed

multiplechoice.exp: Code freeze = no new features; only fixes/tests/docs. Goal: stabilize for release.
