cards.q: What percentage of engineering time is spent diagnosing issues according to the slides?
cards.a: 70‚Äď80% of engineering time is spent diagnosing, not coding.

cards.q: What is the Troubleshooting Mindset?
cards.a: Be curious (not defensive), assume unknowns (not incompetence), verify assumptions with data (not anecdotes), treat symptoms as clues (not conclusions), keep notes.

cards.q: What are the 5 categories of software problems?
cards.a: Code logic bugs, Integration issues, Configuration/Environment issues, Performance issues, Process/Team issues.

cards.q: What tools are used for integration issues?
cards.a: Postman, Swagger, and mocks ‚ÄĒ to test API mismatches and version drift.

cards.q: What tools are used for performance issues?
cards.a: Profilers and traces ‚ÄĒ to identify slow queries and memory leaks.

cards.q: What is Root Cause Analysis (RCA)?
cards.a: A systematic method for identifying the underlying cause of a problem, not just the symptom. Used for both technical failures and human/process errors.

cards.q: What is the quote that opens the troubleshooting lecture?
cards.a: "Think of a bug as a story ‚ÄĒ your job is to trace its plot."

truefalse.q: According to the slides, 70‚Äď80% of engineering time is spent coding new features.
truefalse.exp: 70-80% is spent DIAGNOSING, not coding ‚ÄĒ this is why troubleshooting skills are so valuable.

truefalse.q: Postman and Swagger are tools for diagnosing integration issues.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ these tools help test API mismatches and version drift between services.

truefalse.q: A good troubleshooter treats symptoms as conclusions rather than clues.
truefalse.exp: The mindset says "treat symptoms as CLUES, not conclusions" ‚ÄĒ investigate further.

truefalse.q: Profilers and traces are used to diagnose performance issues like slow queries.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ profilers and distributed traces help identify bottlenecks.

truefalse.q: RCA (Root Cause Analysis) only applies to technical issues, not team/process issues.
truefalse.exp: RCA applies to both technical failures AND human/process errors ‚ÄĒ from the slides.

fillinblank.q: ___ to 80% of engineering time is spent diagnosing, not coding.
"Think of a bug as a ___ ‚ÄĒ your job is to trace its plot."
Postman and Swagger are tools for diagnosing ___ issues.
Profilers and traces help diagnose ___ issues like slow queries.
___ Root Cause Analysis identifies the underlying cause, not just the symptom.

multiplechoice.q: You discover an outage was caused because a background job loaded too much data into memory. Asking 'why?' repeatedly until you trace it to a missing review checklist is an example of:
multiplechoice.exp: 5 Whys repeatedly drills into 'why?' until you reach a root cause (often a process gap), not just the symptom.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: 5 Whys

multiplechoice.q: Which is a DEBUGGING anti-pattern?

options.C: Adding regression tests after the fix
options.D: Changing several variables at once to 'see what happens'

multiplechoice.exp: Change one variable at a time ‚ÄĒ otherwise you can't isolate the cause.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Changing several variables at once to 'see what happens'

multiplechoice.q: Which is the FIRST step in systematic debugging?
multiplechoice.exp: Reproduce first; without reproduction you're guessing. Then isolate, instrument, hypothesize, validate, document.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Reproduce the issue reliably

exam100.q: What percentage of engineering time is spent diagnosing issues according to the slides?
exam100.a: 70‚Äď80% of engineering time is spent diagnosing, not coding.

exam100.q: What is the Troubleshooting Mindset?
exam100.a: Be curious (not defensive), assume unknowns (not incompetence), verify assumptions with data (not anecdotes), treat symptoms as clues (not conclusions), keep notes.

exam100.q: What are the 5 categories of software problems?
exam100.a: Code logic bugs, Integration issues, Configuration/Environment issues, Performance issues, Process/Team issues.

exam100.q: What tools are used for integration issues?
exam100.a: Postman, Swagger, and mocks ‚ÄĒ to test API mismatches and version drift.

cards.q: What is Semantic Versioning (SemVer) and what is its format?
cards.a: A versioning standard with format MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g., v2.4.6). MAJOR = breaking changes, MINOR = new compatible features, PATCH = bug fixes.

cards.q: What does each SemVer component mean?
cards.a: MAJOR: breaking changes (users must update code). MINOR: new features, still backward-compatible. PATCH: bug fixes, no behavior change.

cards.q: What are SemVer pre-release suffixes?
cards.a: -alpha, -beta, -rc.1 (release candidate). E.g., v2.0.0-beta.1 signals an unreleased, pre-production version.

cards.q: What are the four common versioning approaches?
cards.a: Sequential (1, 2, 3), Date-based (2025.11.11), Build-based (1.0.1342), Semantic/SemVer (1.2.3).

cards.q: Why does versioning exist?
cards.a: Every release should be traceable and reproducible. Teams, testers, and users need to know what changed. Version numbers connect code ‚Üí commits ‚Üí releases ‚Üí issues.

cards.q: How does SemVer help with npm package management?
cards.a: ^ notation (e.g., ^1.2.0) allows auto-updates for safe PATCH and MINOR versions while blocking MAJOR breaking changes.

cards.q: Which organizations typically use date-based versioning?
cards.a: Ubuntu and JetBrains ‚ÄĒ great for frequent releases where the date conveys freshness.

truefalse.q: In SemVer, a MAJOR version bump means backward-compatible new features were added.
truefalse.exp: MINOR = new compatible features. MAJOR = breaking changes that require users to update their code.

truefalse.q: SemVer format is MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ e.g., v2.4.6 where 2=major, 4=minor, 6=patch.

truefalse.q: A PATCH version bump in SemVer indicates a breaking API change.
truefalse.exp: PATCH = bug fixes with no behavior change. Breaking changes require a MAJOR version bump.

truefalse.q: Date-based versioning is used by Ubuntu and JetBrains.
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ e.g., Ubuntu 22.04 (April 2022). Great for frequent releases.

truefalse.q: Without versioning, you cannot meaningfully say "this bug was fixed in 1.2.3."
truefalse.exp: Correct ‚ÄĒ this is from the slides as a key reason versioning exists.

fillinblank.q: SemVer format is ___.MINOR.PATCH.
MAJOR = breaking changes, MINOR = new features, PATCH = ___ fixes.
SemVer pre-release suffixes include -alpha, -beta, and ___.
Ubuntu uses ___ versioning (e.g., 22.04 = April 2022).
The ^ notation in npm (e.g., ^1.2.0) allows auto-updates for safe ___ and MINOR versions.

multiplechoice.q: You bump your library from 1.4.7 to 2.0.0. What does this signal?
multiplechoice.exp: MAJOR version bumps signal breaking changes. MINOR = new features (compatible). PATCH = bug fixes.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: Breaking API changes

multiplechoice.q: A team adds an /api/v2/ endpoint while keeping /api/v1/ alive for existing clients. This is an example of:
multiplechoice.exp: Versioning in the URL path (/v1/, /v2/) is the most visible and common method.
multiplechoice.correctAnswerText: URL-path API versioning

multiplechoice.q: A team uses /api/v1/ today. They release a backward-incompatible v2. Per SemVer, the library version most likely became:
multiplechoice.exp: Breaking changes bump the MAJOR version: 1.x ‚Üí 2.0.0.

exam100.q: What is Semantic Versioning (SemVer) and what is its format?
exam100.a: A versioning standard with format MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g., v2.4.6). MAJOR = breaking changes, MINOR = new compatible features, PATCH = bug fixes.

exam100.q: What does each SemVer component mean?
exam100.a: MAJOR: breaking changes (users must update code). MINOR: new features, still backward-compatible. PATCH: bug fixes, no behavior change.

exam100.q: What are SemVer pre-release suffixes?
exam100.a: -alpha, -beta, -rc.1 (release candidate). E.g., v2.0.0-beta.1 signals an unreleased, pre-production version.

exam100.q: What are the four common versioning approaches?
exam100.a: Sequential (1, 2, 3), Date-based (2025.11.11), Build-based (1.0.1342), Semantic/SemVer (1.2.3).

questions.q: Which Git command would you use to apply a single specific commit from one branch onto another?
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Cherry-pick applies a specific commit from one branch onto another. Rebase replays a series; squash collapses commits; tag marks a release.
questions.correctAnswerText: git cherry-pick

questions.q: An e-commerce site needs to keep working while you incrementally replace its legacy checkout system. Which pattern is ideal?
questions.answer: B
questions.topic: Green vs Brown / Refactor vs Rewrite
questions.exp: The Strangler Fig pattern gradually replaces pieces of a legacy system while keeping it running ‚ÄĒ minimal downtime, reversible.
questions.correctAnswerText: Strangler Fig

questions.q: A function has many nested if statements that check for valid inputs before doing the real work. Which defensive technique should refactor this?
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Guard clauses do early returns for invalid conditions, flattening deep nesting and reducing missed branches.
questions.correctAnswerText: Guard clauses (early returns)

questions.q: Which is the BEST description of a Value Object in DDD?

options.A: A database table with auto-incrementing IDs
options.B: An immutable object identified by its values, not identity
options.D: A class that handles dependency injection

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Value Objects (e.g., Money, Coordinates) are immutable and identified by their values. Entities have identity (e.g., User #42).
questions.correctAnswerText: An immutable object identified by its values, not identity

questions.q: You bump your library from 1.4.7 to 2.0.0. What does this signal?
questions.answer: C
questions.exp: MAJOR version bumps signal breaking changes. MINOR = new features (compatible). PATCH = bug fixes.
questions.correctAnswerText: Breaking API changes

questions.q: Which best describes 'dogfooding'?

options.A: Stress-testing a system at peak load
options.B: Using your own product internally before/during release
options.C: Compiling code for multiple platforms at once
options.D: Running automated regression tests after every commit

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Dogfooding ('eat your own dog food') means employees use the product themselves to catch bugs and build empathy.
questions.correctAnswerText: Using your own product internally before/during release

questions.q: Which of these REST verbs is NOT idempotent?
questions.answer: D
questions.exp: POST creates new resources on each call ‚ÄĒ not idempotent. GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent.
questions.correctAnswerText: POST

questions.q: A team writes its tests AFTER the code is complete, just to confirm everything works. Is this TDD?

options.A: Yes ‚ÄĒ any unit testing is TDD
options.B: No ‚ÄĒ TDD writes tests BEFORE the code
options.C: Only if the tests pass on the first run
options.D: Only if integration tests are also included

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: TDD inverts the workflow: write a failing test (RED) ‚Üí write minimum code to pass (GREEN) ‚Üí refactor. Tests after code is NOT TDD.
questions.correctAnswerText: No ‚ÄĒ TDD writes tests BEFORE the code

questions.q: What does the 'I' in SOLID stand for?
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Interface Segregation: no client should be forced to depend on methods it does not use. Prefer many small interfaces.
questions.correctAnswerText: Interface Segregation

questions.q: Which is a NON-functional requirement?

options.B: The cart shows the running total
options.C: The system must respond within 200 ms under normal load

questions.answer: C
questions.exp: Performance/latency is a non-functional requirement (a quality). The others describe behaviors (functional).
questions.correctAnswerText: The system must respond within 200 ms under normal load

questions.q: Which is a HIGH-priority feature flag used to quickly shut off a misbehaving feature in production?
questions.answer: C
questions.exp: Kill switches are emergency off-switches for features causing problems. Faster than a redeploy.
questions.correctAnswerText: Kill switch

questions.q: Two threads update the same balance variable. Without synchronization, the final value is unpredictable. This is called a:
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Race condition: outcome depends on the timing of concurrent accesses to shared data. Prevent with locks, immutability, message passing, etc.
questions.correctAnswerText: Race condition

questions.q: Which best captures the difference between SDLC and ALM?

options.A: SDLC = the entire app lifespan; ALM = just development
options.B: ALM is a subset of SDLC
options.C: SDLC develops the software; ALM manages the entire application from idea to sunset

questions.answer: C
questions.exp: SDLC is a subset of ALM. SDLC focuses on building; ALM includes ideation, ops, governance, sunset.
questions.correctAnswerText: SDLC develops the software; ALM manages the entire application from idea to sunset

questions.q: Which SCM type is BEST for an open-source project with worldwide contributors?

options.D: Manual file copies labeled v1, v2, v3

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Distributed VCS like Git gives each contributor a full repo with history ‚ÄĒ perfect for distributed teams and offline work.
questions.correctAnswerText: Distributed (e.g., Git)

questions.q: You discover an outage was caused because a background job loaded too much data into memory. Asking 'why?' repeatedly until you trace it to a missing review checklist is an example of:
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: 5 Whys repeatedly drills into 'why?' until you reach a root cause (often a process gap), not just the symptom.
questions.correctAnswerText: 5 Whys

questions.q: Which BEST defines technical debt?

options.A: Money owed to a software vendor
options.B: The future cost of quick decisions today (shortcuts, hacks)
options.C: Unpaid feature requests in the backlog
options.D: Code that has not yet been deployed

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Technical debt (coined by Ward Cunningham) is the future cost of shortcuts taken to ship faster today.
questions.correctAnswerText: The future cost of quick decisions today (shortcuts, hacks)

questions.q: A SPA (Single Page Application) is best described as:

options.B: An app that loads once and updates dynamically via JavaScript
options.D: A page generated server-side per request

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: SPAs load once, then dynamically update the page (Gmail, Notion, Slack). SSR pages are generated per request.
questions.correctAnswerText: An app that loads once and updates dynamically via JavaScript

questions.q: Which framework bundles Chromium and Node.js to run web apps as desktop apps?
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Electron bundles Chromium + Node.js. Used by VS Code, Slack, Discord, Notion, Postman.
questions.correctAnswerText: Electron

questions.q: You are choosing patterns and want them to support TDD well. Which combination is BEST?
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: DI, Strategy, and Repository decouple dependencies and allow easy mocking ‚ÄĒ ideal for TDD. The first option HIDES dependencies.
questions.correctAnswerText: Dependency Injection + Strategy + Repository

questions.q: Which ACID property guarantees that, after a successful commit, data is NOT lost even if power fails?
questions.answer: D
questions.exp: Durability: once a transaction is confirmed, it stays saved (even across crashes).
questions.correctAnswerText: Durability

questions.q: Which is NOT one of the four values of the Agile Manifesto?

options.A: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
options.C: Following a detailed plan over responding to change

questions.answer: C
questions.exp: Agile VALUES responding to change OVER following a plan ‚ÄĒ the reverse of the option shown.
questions.correctAnswerText: Following a detailed plan over responding to change

questions.q: A team adds an /api/v2/ endpoint while keeping /api/v1/ alive for existing clients. This is an example of:
questions.answer: A
questions.exp: Versioning in the URL path (/v1/, /v2/) is the most visible and common method.
questions.correctAnswerText: URL-path API versioning

questions.q: Which is the BEST kind of cohesion?
questions.answer: C
questions.exp: Functional cohesion = all parts work toward one purpose (matches SRP). Coincidental is the worst.
questions.correctAnswerText: Functional

questions.q: Defensive programming and testing differ in that:

options.A: Testing replaces the need for defensive code
options.B: Defensive programming prevents problems inside the code; testing detects them afterwards

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Defensive code prevents bad states; tests detect them. They complement each other.
questions.correctAnswerText: Defensive programming prevents problems inside the code; testing detects them afterwards

questions.q: Which feature flag type would you use to compare two layouts and choose the one with better engagement?
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Experiment flags drive A/B tests ‚ÄĒ comparing variants on real user behavior.
questions.correctAnswerText: Experiment flag

questions.q: Which BEST describes WebAssembly (WASM)?

options.A: A new JavaScript dialect for browsers only
options.B: A binary module format compiled from C/C++/Rust/Go/.NET that runs at near-native speed, sandboxed and portable

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: WASM is a portable binary format compiled from many languages; runs in browsers, servers, and edge environments.
questions.correctAnswerText: A binary module format compiled from C/C++/Rust/Go/.NET that runs at near-native speed, sandboxed and portable

questions.q: Which is a code SMELL rather than a bug?

options.A: A null pointer exception in production
options.B: A 600-line method that compiles and runs correctly

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: A long method is a SMELL ‚ÄĒ the code works, but the design is weak. Smells suggest deeper issues without being defects themselves.
questions.correctAnswerText: A 600-line method that compiles and runs correctly

questions.q: Which Scrum event focuses on the PROCESS rather than the product?
questions.answer: D
questions.exp: Retrospectives discuss the process ‚ÄĒ what to keep, change, improve. Reviews discuss the product itself.
questions.correctAnswerText: Sprint Retrospective

questions.q: What does CORS stand for, and which configuration is recommended?

options.A: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing; configure specific allowed origins, not '*' for sensitive APIs
options.B: Cross-Origin Request Standard; allow any origin
options.C: Cross-Object Reference Schema; disable for performance
options.D: Centralized Origin Resource Sync; rotate keys daily

questions.answer: A
questions.exp: CORS = Cross-Origin Resource Sharing. Configure SPECIFIC origins (not '*' in production) for security.
questions.correctAnswerText: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing; configure specific allowed origins, not '*' for sensitive APIs

questions.q: Which BEST describes the difference between Mocks and Stubs?

options.A: Mocks return canned data; stubs record interactions
options.B: Stubs return canned data; mocks record how they were used

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Stubs return canned/fixed values. Mocks RECORD interactions, letting tests assert on call patterns.
questions.correctAnswerText: Stubs return canned data; mocks record how they were used

questions.q: A 'T-shaped' developer is one who:

options.A: Has expertise in exactly two languages
options.B: Has broad knowledge across multiple layers and deep expertise in one

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: T-shaped = broad horizontal + deep vertical specialty. Common framing of modern full-stack devs.
questions.correctAnswerText: Has broad knowledge across multiple layers and deep expertise in one

questions.q: Which is the FBI's famous failed project, cited as a Waterfall failure due to changing requirements mid-build?
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: The FBI's Virtual Case File failed (Waterfall), losing $170M before being abandoned.
questions.correctAnswerText: Virtual Case File

questions.q: Which is the BEST description of the Repository pattern's purpose?

options.B: Abstract data-access logic so the rest of the app doesn't care about the storage mechanism

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Repository abstracts storage details (file, DB, API) behind a domain-friendly interface. Improves testability.
questions.correctAnswerText: Abstract data-access logic so the rest of the app doesn't care about the storage mechanism

questions.q: A startup builds a new product with no legacy systems. This is:
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Greenfield = new project, no legacy constraints. Brownfield = building within existing systems.
questions.correctAnswerText: Greenfield development

questions.q: Which API style uses Protocol Buffers and HTTP/2 for high-throughput internal microservices?
questions.answer: C
questions.exp: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers and HTTP/2 ‚ÄĒ binary, strongly typed, fast ‚ÄĒ great for internal microservice communication.
questions.correctAnswerText: gRPC

questions.q: Which Git operation REWRITES history?
questions.answer: D
questions.exp: Rebase replays commits onto another branch, rewriting history. Merge preserves history with a merge commit.
questions.correctAnswerText: Rebase

questions.q: Which BEST describes the Open/Closed Principle?

options.A: Open for refactoring, closed for modification
options.B: Open for extension, closed for modification
options.C: Open for inheritance, closed for composition
options.D: Open for users, closed for developers

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Open for EXTENSION (via abstraction/inheritance), closed for MODIFICATION (don't edit existing code).
questions.correctAnswerText: Open for extension, closed for modification

questions.q: Which best matches the Agile vs Waterfall trade-off?

options.A: Waterfall is iterative; Agile is sequential
options.B: Waterfall is phase-based with upfront planning; Agile is iterative with continuous feedback
options.D: Waterfall fits startups; Agile fits aerospace

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Waterfall: sequential phases, fixed plan. Agile: iterative cycles, embrace change. Aerospace tends Waterfall; startups tend Agile.
questions.correctAnswerText: Waterfall is phase-based with upfront planning; Agile is iterative with continuous feedback

questions.q: Which describes ASYNCHRONOUS execution?

options.A: Tasks run one after another, each waiting
options.B: Tasks can start, then finish later; work continues while waiting
options.D: All tasks run in parallel by default

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Async = non-blocking; the caller continues other work while waiting. Sync = step-by-step, blocking.
questions.correctAnswerText: Tasks can start, then finish later; work continues while waiting

questions.q: Which is NOT a feature flag category?
questions.answer: D
questions.exp: Common types include release, experiment, operations, permission/entitlement, tier, and kill switches. 'Authentication flag' is not a standard category.
questions.correctAnswerText: Authentication flag

questions.q: Which describes idempotency in REST?

options.B: Repeating the same request produces the same effect as one

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Idempotent: calling it once or many times yields the same result. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent.
questions.correctAnswerText: Repeating the same request produces the same effect as one

questions.q: A class is responsible for authentication, sending emails, AND logging. Which SOLID principle is violated?
questions.answer: A
questions.exp: Single Responsibility says a class should do one thing well. Three unrelated jobs violates SRP.
questions.correctAnswerText: Single Responsibility (S)

questions.q: Which is the BEST late-stage strategy when nearing a deadline?
questions.answer: C
questions.exp: Late stage = scope control. Cut what isn't essential, stabilize, polish, and rehearse the demo.
questions.correctAnswerText: Cut scope, stabilize, polish, rehearse

questions.q: Which is a TYPICAL responsibility of a Scrum Master?

options.B: Coach the team, facilitate events, remove impediments, advocate Agile

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Scrum Master is a servant-leader: coach, facilitator, protector. The Product Owner manages the backlog.
questions.correctAnswerText: Coach the team, facilitate events, remove impediments, advocate Agile

questions.q: Which BEST describes the difference between an Entity and a Value Object in DDD?

options.A: Entity has identity; Value Object is immutable and identified by value
options.B: Entity is immutable; Value Object has identity

questions.answer: A
questions.exp: Entity = identity (User #42). Value Object = immutable, identity by value (Money($5)).
questions.correctAnswerText: Entity has identity; Value Object is immutable and identified by value

questions.q: Which is the MAIN purpose of a code freeze?

options.C: Stabilize the build ‚ÄĒ only fixes, tests, and doc updates allowed
options.D: Switch to a new tech stack

questions.answer: C
questions.exp: Code freeze = no new features; only fixes/tests/docs. Goal: stabilize for release.
questions.correctAnswerText: Stabilize the build ‚ÄĒ only fixes, tests, and doc updates allowed

questions.q: Which BEST distinguishes a Queue from a Pub/Sub model?

options.A: Queue is one-to-many; Pub/Sub is one-to-one
options.B: Queue is one-to-one; Pub/Sub is one-to-many
options.D: Queues use HTTP only; Pub/Sub uses TCP

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Queues deliver one message to one consumer; Pub/Sub fans out to all subscribers.
questions.correctAnswerText: Queue is one-to-one; Pub/Sub is one-to-many

questions.q: Which is a DEBUGGING anti-pattern?

options.C: Adding regression tests after the fix
options.D: Changing several variables at once to 'see what happens'

questions.answer: D
questions.exp: Change one variable at a time ‚ÄĒ otherwise you can't isolate the cause.
questions.correctAnswerText: Changing several variables at once to 'see what happens'

questions.q: Which BEST describes a Bounded Context in DDD?

options.B: A sub-section of the domain with its own model and language

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Bounded Contexts split large domains into autonomous areas (e.g., Inventory vs Sales) each with its own model.
questions.correctAnswerText: A sub-section of the domain with its own model and language

questions.q: Which is TRUE about an MVP?

options.A: It is the final, polished product
options.B: It is the same as a prototype
options.C: It is usable and demonstrable, delivering enough value to test key assumptions
options.D: It must include every planned feature

questions.answer: C
questions.exp: MVP is minimum, viable (works), product (can be used or handed off). It is NOT a prototype or the final product.
questions.correctAnswerText: It is usable and demonstrable, delivering enough value to test key assumptions

questions.q: A team uses /api/v1/ today. They release a backward-incompatible v2. Per SemVer, the library version most likely became:
questions.answer: C
questions.exp: Breaking changes bump the MAJOR version: 1.x ‚Üí 2.0.0.

questions.q: Which is FALSE about DDD?

options.B: It uses Entities, Value Objects, and Aggregates
options.C: It is itself a single design pattern
options.D: It uses Bounded Contexts to split large domains

questions.answer: C
questions.exp: DDD is NOT a pattern. It is a strategic/architectural philosophy that tells you WHICH patterns to use.
questions.correctAnswerText: It is itself a single design pattern

questions.q: Which BEST describes the difference between Authentication and Authorization?

options.A: Authentication = what you can do; Authorization = who you are
options.B: Authentication = who you are; Authorization = what you can do

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Authentication identifies who you are (login, token). Authorization grants what you can do (roles, scopes).
questions.correctAnswerText: Authentication = who you are; Authorization = what you can do

questions.q: Which is a common test framework for Java?
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: JUnit (and TestNG) for Java; xUnit/NUnit/MSTest for C#; Jest/Mocha for JavaScript.
questions.correctAnswerText: JUnit

questions.q: A monolith is MOST appropriate when:

options.A: You have hundreds of developers worldwide
options.B: You need extreme regional latency optimization
options.C: You are building an MVP with a small team and a tight deadline
options.D: Multiple parts of the system must scale independently

questions.answer: C
questions.exp: Monoliths shine for MVPs, small teams, simple domains, and tight deadlines. Large/varied scale favors microservices.
questions.correctAnswerText: You are building an MVP with a small team and a tight deadline

questions.q: Which describes the Open Closed Principle's typical implementation strategy?

options.B: Favor abstraction and inheritance/composition over modifying existing code

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: OCP encourages adding behavior via new types (abstraction, polymorphism), not by modifying existing classes.
questions.correctAnswerText: Favor abstraction and inheritance/composition over modifying existing code

questions.q: Which is the FIRST step in systematic debugging?
questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Reproduce first; without reproduction you're guessing. Then isolate, instrument, hypothesize, validate, document.
questions.correctAnswerText: Reproduce the issue reliably

questions.q: Which is TRUE about feature flags?

options.A: Hiding a button in the UI is sufficient access control
options.B: They allow deployment and release to happen at different times
options.C: They eliminate the need for testing
options.D: Old flags should never be removed

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: Decouple DEPLOY (code present) from RELEASE (users granted access). UI hiding is NOT security; backend enforces.
questions.correctAnswerText: They allow deployment and release to happen at different times

questions.q: Which is the BEST description of REST?

options.A: An XML-based RPC protocol with WSDL contracts
options.B: A stateless, resource-based architectural style over HTTP, defined by Roy Fielding
options.C: A binary RPC protocol over HTTP/2
options.D: A query language for graph data

questions.answer: B
questions.exp: REST (Fielding, 2000) is stateless, resource-based, uniform-interface, cacheable, client-server. SOAP is XML-based; gRPC is binary; GraphQL is query language.
questions.correctAnswerText: A stateless, resource-based architectural style over HTTP, defined by Roy Fielding

questions.q: A test that exercises the FULL stack ‚ÄĒ UI ‚Üí API ‚Üí DB ‚ÄĒ by simulating real user behavior is:
questions.answer: C
questions.exp: E2E tests cover user journeys across the full system. Slower, but essential for critical workflows.
questions.correctAnswerText: End-to-End (E2E) test

Experiment flags drive A/B tests ‚ÄĒ comparing variants on real user behavior.
WASM is a portable binary format compiled from many languages; runs in browsers, servers, and edge environments.
A long method is a SMELL ‚ÄĒ the code works, but the design is weak. Smells suggest deeper issues without being defects themselves.
Retrospectives discuss the process ‚ÄĒ what to keep, change, improve. Reviews discuss the product itself.
CORS = Cross-Origin Resource Sharing. Configure SPECIFIC origins (not '*' in production) for security.
Stubs return canned/fixed values. Mocks RECORD interactions, letting tests assert on call patterns.
T-shaped = broad horizontal + deep vertical specialty. Common framing of modern full-stack devs.
The FBI's Virtual Case File failed (Waterfall), losing $170M before being abandoned.
Repository abstracts storage details (file, DB, API) behind a domain-friendly interface. Improves testability.
Greenfield = new project, no legacy constraints. Brownfield = building within existing systems.
gRPC uses Protocol Buffers and HTTP/2 ‚ÄĒ binary, strongly typed, fast ‚ÄĒ great for internal microservice communication.
Rebase replays commits onto another branch, rewriting history. Merge preserves history with a merge commit.
Open for EXTENSION (via abstraction/inheritance), closed for MODIFICATION (don't edit existing code).
Waterfall: sequential phases, fixed plan. Agile: iterative cycles, embrace change. Aerospace tends Waterfall; startups tend Agile.
Async = non-blocking; the caller continues other work while waiting. Sync = step-by-step, blocking.
Common types include release, experiment, operations, permission/entitlement, tier, and kill switches. 'Authentication flag' is not a standard category.
Idempotent: calling it once or many times yields the same result. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent.
Single Responsibility says a class should do one thing well. Three unrelated jobs violates SRP.
Late stage = scope control. Cut what isn't essential, stabilize, polish, and rehearse the demo.
Scrum Master is a servant-leader: coach, facilitator, protector. The Product Owner manages the backlog.
Entity = identity (User #42). Value Object = immutable, identity by value (Money($5)).
Code freeze = no new features; only fixes/tests/docs. Goal: stabilize for release.
Queues deliver one message to one consumer; Pub/Sub fans out to all subscribers.
Change one variable at a time ‚ÄĒ otherwise you can't isolate the cause.
Bounded Contexts split large domains into autonomous areas (e.g., Inventory vs Sales) each with its own model.
MVP is minimum, viable (works), product (can be used or handed off). It is NOT a prototype or the final product.
Breaking changes bump the MAJOR version: 1.x ‚Üí 2.0.0.
DDD is NOT a pattern. It is a strategic/architectural philosophy that tells you WHICH patterns to use.
Authentication identifies who you are (login, token). Authorization grants what you can do (roles, scopes).
JUnit (and TestNG) for Java; xUnit/NUnit/MSTest for C#; Jest/Mocha for JavaScript.
Monoliths shine for MVPs, small teams, simple domains, and tight deadlines. Large/varied scale favors microservices.
OCP encourages adding behavior via new types (abstraction, polymorphism), not by modifying existing classes.
Reproduce first; without reproduction you're guessing. Then isolate, instrument, hypothesize, validate, document.
Decouple DEPLOY (code present) from RELEASE (users granted access). UI hiding is NOT security; backend enforces.
REST (Fielding, 2000) is stateless, resource-based, uniform-interface, cacheable, client-server. SOAP is XML-based; gRPC is binary; GraphQL is query language.
E2E tests cover user journeys across the full system. Slower, but essential for critical workflows.

questions.question: What are the three elements of the classic software trilemma?
questions.answer: Good, Fast, and Cheap ‚ÄĒ you can only pick two.

questions.question: What are the main requirements elicitation techniques?
questions.answer: Interviews, focus groups, surveys, shadowing/observation, copycatting, document analysis, and prototyping.

questions.question: What are functional requirements? Give examples.
questions.answer: Behaviors the system must perform ‚ÄĒ e.g., Search, Authentication, Add to Cart, Take Payment.

questions.question: What are Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs)? Give examples.
questions.answer: Quality attributes the system must have ‚ÄĒ e.g., Performance, Security, Compatibility, Compliance.

questions.question: What is the standard user story format?
questions.answer: "As a [user persona], I want [describe need] so that [describe benefit]"

questions.question: What is Agile?
questions.answer: An iterative and incremental approach to project management that prioritizes flexibility, collaboration, and customer feedback.

questions.question: When was the Agile Manifesto published and who wrote it?
questions.answer: 2001, by 17 software practitioners who met in Snowbird, Utah.

questions.question: What are the 4 core values of the Agile Manifesto?
questions.answer:
1) Individuals & interactions over processes/tools
2) Working software over documentation
3) Customer collaboration over contracts
4) Responding to change over following a plan

questions.question: When was Scrum formalized and by whom?
questions.answer: 1995 by Jeff Sutherland and Ken Schwaber.

questions.question: What 1940s concept influenced Agile methodology?
questions.answer: Kanban, developed at Toyota with Lean roots.

questions.question: What does SCM stand for and what is it?
questions.answer: Source Control Management (also called Version Control Systems/VCS). It tracks and manages changes to source code over time.

questions.question: What is the difference between centralized and distributed VCS?
questions.answer: Centralized (SVN, Perforce): single central repository, always needs network. Distributed (Git, Mercurial): every user has a full copy, full offline work possible.

questions.question: What was the first centralized VCS and when was it created?
questions.answer: SCCS (Source Code Control System) in the 1970s‚Äď1980s.

questions.question: When did Git emerge and what type of VCS is it?
questions.answer: 2000s. Git is a distributed VCS and is currently the most widely used.

questions.question: Name three modern cloud-based SCM platforms.
questions.answer: GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket (also Azure DevOps).

questions.question: What is Greenfield development?
questions.answer: Starting a project from scratch with no existing systems, codebases, or constraints. Developers have complete freedom in technology stack, architecture, and design.

questions.question: What is Brownfield development?
questions.answer: Working within or building on top of an existing system, often with legacy code, infrastructure, or organizational processes. Involves refactoring, modernizing, or extending existing solutions.

questions.question: Which type of project typically has higher flexibility: Greenfield or Brownfield?
questions.answer: Greenfield ‚ÄĒ developers have high freedom to choose any stack or design. Brownfield has low flexibility due to existing integrations.

questions.question: What type of risk is more common in Greenfield vs Brownfield projects?
questions.answer: Greenfield: market risk (will it succeed?). Brownfield: technical risk (can we modernize safely?).

questions.question: What skill set is most demanded in Brownfield projects?
questions.answer: Strong debugging, refactoring, and system analysis skills.

questions.question: What is Code Quality?
questions.answer: A measure of how well code adheres to standards, practices, and maintainability principles.

questions.question: What is the difference between internal and external code quality?
questions.answer: Internal: structure, readability, maintainability. External: correctness, performance, security, usability.

questions.question: List the 7 attributes of code quality from the slides.
questions.answer: Readability, Maintainability, Reliability, Performance, Testability, Scalability, Appropriateness.

questions.question: What is a linter?
questions.answer: A static analysis tool that analyzes source code to flag potential errors, bugs, stylistic issues, and suspicious constructs. It acts as an automated code reviewer.

questions.question: What is Cyclomatic Complexity?
questions.answer: A complexity metric that measures the number of linearly independent paths through a program ‚ÄĒ higher values indicate more complex, harder-to-test code.

questions.question: What are software patterns?
questions.answer: Reusable solutions to recurring problems. Not code copy-paste, but generalizable templates that exist at multiple levels (low, mid, high).

questions.question: What are the three levels of software patterns?
questions.answer: Low-level (language idioms/syntax), Mid-level (design patterns like MVC/Observer/Factory), High-level (architecture patterns like Microservices/CQRS).

questions.question: What is a language idiom in programming?
questions.answer: A natural, commonly accepted way of solving problems in a specific language ‚ÄĒ makes code readable, concise, and "native-feeling."

questions.question: What is the difference between an Idiom and a Design Pattern?
questions.answer: Idioms are language-specific and make code readable. Patterns are language-agnostic and solve architectural/design problems.

questions.question: Give examples of mid-level (design) patterns.
questions.answer: Observer, Factory, MVC (Model-View-Controller), Strategy, Builder, Repository, Adapter.

questions.question: What is TDD?
questions.answer: A development approach where you write tests BEFORE writing code. Follows the Red-Green-Refactor (RGR) cycle. Tests are not an afterthought.

questions.question: What is the Red-Green-Refactor (RGR) cycle?
questions.answer: RED: Write a failing test. GREEN: Write just enough code to make it pass. REFACTOR: Clean up the code while keeping tests passing.

questions.question: Who founded TDD and when?
questions.answer: Kent Beck established TDD in 2002 with his book "Test-Driven Development: By Example." Rooted in Extreme Programming (XP).

questions.question: What are the six types of testing covered in the slides?
questions.answer: Unit, Integration, Regression, Stress/Load, End-To-End (E2E), UI/Usability.

questions.question: What is the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern?
questions.answer: A testing pattern that divides each test into three sections: Arrange (set up data), Act (invoke the function), Assert (verify the result).

questions.question: What is a software process model?
questions.answer: A framework that defines how a software project is planned, developed, tested, and delivered. Used to manage people, time, cost, and quality.

questions.question: What are the phases of the Waterfall model?
questions.answer: Requirements ‚Üí Design ‚Üí Implementation ‚Üí Testing ‚Üí Deployment ‚Üí Maintenance. Each phase must be complete before the next begins.

questions.question: When should you use Waterfall?
questions.answer: Requirements are clear/fixed, client unavailable for feedback, regulated industries (aerospace, defense, medical), strong documentation emphasis.

questions.question: When should you use Agile?
questions.answer: Requirements are unclear or evolving, frequent iterations needed, collaborative self-managed team, MVPs, startups, R&D, SaaS.

questions.question: What is a Hybrid/Spiral process model?
questions.answer: Combines elements of Waterfall and Agile ‚ÄĒ iterative cycles with structured phases, balancing flexibility and documentation.

questions.question: What does SDLC stand for and what is it?
questions.answer: Software Development Life Cycle. A structured methodology for developing software through defined phases.

questions.question: What does ALM stand for and what is it?
questions.answer: Application Lifecycle Management. A broader discipline encompassing the complete lifespan of an application from inception to retirement.

questions.question: How does SDLC relate to ALM?
questions.answer: SDLC is a subset of ALM. ALM extends beyond development to include planning, release, operations, governance, and sunset/retirement.

questions.question: What are the stages of ALM?
questions.answer: Ideation ‚Üí Planning ‚Üí Development (SDLC) ‚Üí Release ‚Üí Operations ‚Üí Governance ‚Üí Sunset.

questions.question: What is the real-world analogy for SDLC vs ALM?
questions.answer: SDLC = designing and building a car in the factory. ALM = managing the car's full lifecycle: manufacturing, selling, servicing, upgrading, and decommissioning.

questions.question: What was the old definition of a Full Stack Developer (2010s)?
questions.answer: "One person who builds the entire web app ‚ÄĒ HTML/CSS, JS, backend, and database."

questions.question: What is the modern definition of a Full Stack Developer (2020s)?
questions.answer: "An engineer who understands and can navigate multiple layers of the stack, even if they specialize in one."

questions.question: What is a T-shaped developer?
questions.answer: A developer with broad knowledge across multiple layers of the stack but deep expertise in one specific area.

questions.question: What are the three tiers of the full stack?
questions.answer: Front-end (client/UI), Middle tier (API/business logic), and Back-end (database/persistence).

questions.question: What is "Full Stack + DevOps" sometimes called?
questions.answer: FSD ‚ÄĒ Full Stack Developer expanded to include DevOps, CI/CD, and sometimes cloud knowledge.

questions.question: What is an API?
questions.answer: Application Programming Interface ‚ÄĒ a defined way for software systems to communicate. It encapsulates complexity and exposes controlled functionality.

questions.question: What is REST and who defined it?
questions.answer: REpresentational State Transfer, defined by Roy Fielding in 2000 in his dissertation. It is stateless, uses HTTP, and is resource-based (nouns, not verbs).

questions.question: What are the 5 REST principles?
questions.answer:
1) Stateless communication
2) Client-server separation
3) Uniform interface
4) Resource-based (nouns)
5) Cacheable responses

questions.question: What is SOAP and what does it use?
questions.answer: Simple Object Access Protocol ‚ÄĒ uses XML + WSDL (Web Services Description Language). Strong typing but verbose and brittle.

questions.question: What are the common HTTP verbs used in REST?
questions.answer: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE

questions.question: What is Domain-Driven Design (DDD) and who introduced it?
questions.answer: A strategic and architectural philosophy that lets the business domain drive software structure (not technology or database). Introduced by Eric Evans in 2003.

questions.question: What are the DDD Building Blocks?
questions.answer: Entities (identity), Value Objects (immutable, identity by value), Aggregates & Aggregate Roots, Repositories, Services, Events.

questions.question: What is a DDD Entity?
questions.answer: An object with a unique identity that persists over time ‚ÄĒ e.g., Player, Order, Customer. Identity is what makes two entities different even if their attributes are the same.

questions.question: What is a DDD Value Object?
questions.answer: An immutable object identified by its value, not an ID ‚ÄĒ e.g., Money($10), Coordinates(lat, lng). Best for enforcing semantic typing.

questions.question: What is a DDD Aggregate Root?
questions.answer: The root entity of an Aggregate cluster. All access to the aggregate goes through the root, which enforces consistency boundaries.

questions.question: What is persistence in software?
questions.answer: Persistence is the art of making data outlive the program that created it ‚ÄĒ saving state so it survives beyond a single program execution.

questions.question: What is the difference between volatile and non-volatile memory?
questions.answer: Volatile (RAM): disappears when power is off. Non-volatile (Disk, SSD, Flash): survives power loss. Persistence relies on non-volatile storage.

questions.question: What does the persistence layer do?
questions.answer: Manages where and how data is stored and retrieved. Converts in-memory objects to storable formats and back. Handles save, update, delete, query. Protects upper layers from storage details.

questions.question: How does the persistence layer fit in layered architecture?
questions.answer: UI ‚Üí Application ‚Üí Domain ‚Üí Persistence ‚Üí Infrastructure. The repository hides whether data lives in a file, database, or API.

questions.question: Why do we need persistence?
questions.answer: When a program ends, its variables disappear. Persistence saves state ‚ÄĒ users, settings, progress, messages ‚ÄĒ so it survives between program runs.

questions.question: What is synchronous execution?
questions.answer: Tasks run one after another ‚ÄĒ each must finish before the next starts. Simple and predictable but can be slow. Analogy: waiting in line.

questions.question: What is asynchronous execution?
questions.answer: Tasks can start and finish later ‚ÄĒ work continues while waiting. Improves responsiveness and efficiency. Analogy: starting laundry while doing other things.

questions.question: What is the difference between blocking and non-blocking?
questions.answer: Blocking: program halts until a task completes. Non-blocking: can start other work while waiting. Analogy: one chef cooks while others wait (blocking) vs. everyone multitasking (non-blocking).

questions.question: What is AJAX?
questions.answer: Asynchronous JavaScript and XML. Fetches data without reloading the page. Originally used XMLHttpRequest; evolved to use fetch() or Axios/React Query.

questions.question: What is a thread?
questions.answer: The smallest unit of execution inside a program. Multiple threads allow concurrent execution within a single process.

questions.question: What is the historical timeline of cross-platform development?
questions.answer: Assembly (hardware-specific) ‚Üí C (compile anywhere) ‚Üí Java/JVM 1995 (write once, run anywhere) ‚Üí .NET CLR, Flash, Qt (2000s) ‚Üí Flutter, React Native, WebAssembly (2010s+)

questions.question: What is the JVM and what does it enable?
questions.answer: Java Virtual Machine ‚ÄĒ executes platform-neutral bytecode. Enables "write once, run anywhere." Supports Java, Kotlin, Scala, Groovy.

questions.question: What is the difference between Native, Bytecode, and Interpreted execution?
questions.answer: Native: compiled directly for CPU (C/C++). Bytecode: intermediate form executed by VM (Java, .NET). Interpreted: read line-by-line (Python, JS). Hybrid (JIT/AOT): compiles some code at runtime.

questions.question: What is WebAssembly (WASM)?
questions.answer: A binary instruction format that allows code written in languages like C++, Rust, or Go to run in web browsers at near-native speed.

questions.question: What are examples of cross-platform frameworks?
questions.answer: Flutter (Google), React Native (Meta), MAUI (.NET), Kotlin Multiplatform.

questions.question: What is refactoring?
questions.answer: Improving the internal structure of code WITHOUT changing its external behavior. Examples: simplify functions, rename variables, extract classes, add tests. "Clean up the house without moving out."

questions.question: What is rewriting?
questions.answer: Rebuilding the system from scratch, often with new tech or design principles. Examples: replacing PHP with Node.js, monolith ‚Üí microservices. "Demolish and rebuild ‚ÄĒ same address, new foundation."

questions.question: Why do teams refactor code?
questions.answer: Code smells (long methods, duplication), hard to add features safely, poor readability, no tests, gradual performance degradation. Goal: incremental improvement without breaking behavior.

questions.question: Why do teams rewrite software?
questions.answer: Tech stack is obsolete, architecture can't scale, legacy frameworks/security issues, team doesn't understand old code, integrating new platforms.

questions.question: Compare refactor vs rewrite on risk and cost.
questions.answer: Refactor: lower risk, lower upfront cost, incremental delivery. Rewrite: higher risk, high upfront cost, all-at-once delivery.

questions.question: What is defensive programming?
questions.answer: A mindset and set of techniques that make software behave predictably even under unexpected conditions. Goal: anticipate failures, prevent/limit damage, communicate errors clearly.

questions.question: What are the key principles of defensive programming?
questions.answer: Validate all inputs, avoid assumptions about data/environment/state, fail fast and clearly, use guard clauses instead of deep nesting, handle edge cases (nulls, empty arrays, limits), minimize side effects.

questions.question: What does "fail fast, fail clearly" mean?
questions.answer: When an error occurs, detect it immediately and communicate it explicitly (via exceptions, asserts) rather than silently continuing in an invalid state.

questions.question: What is a guard clause?
questions.answer: An early return or check at the start of a function that handles invalid/edge-case inputs immediately, avoiding deep nesting (if-else trees).

questions.question: What is a feature flag?
questions.answer: A switch that controls whether a feature is enabled. The code exists even when the feature is hidden or disabled. Flags can be changed by environment, user, role, tier, region, or runtime condition.

questions.question: What is the difference between deployment and release?
questions.answer: Deployment: code is installed in an environment. Release: users are allowed to access it. Feature flags let these happen at different times.

questions.question: What are the 6 common categories of feature flags?
questions.answer: Release flags, Experiment flags, Operations flags, Permission/entitlement flags, Tier/subscription flags, Kill switches.

questions.question: What are release flags used for?
questions.answer: To hide new functionality until it is ready. Common for UI redesigns, new workflows, new services. Allows code to be merged and deployed before public availability.

questions.question: What does MVP stand for and what does it really mean?
questions.answer: Minimum Viable Product. Minimum = only essential functionality. Viable = reliable, stable, demonstrable. Product = can be handed off, shown, or used. NOT a prototype.

questions.question: What is the "Late-Stage Trap" near the end of a project?
questions.answer: Common behaviors: adding "just one more feature," neglecting documentation/polish, ignoring testing debt, over-optimizing things users will never see.

questions.question: What is a Code Freeze?
questions.answer: A period when no new features are added ‚ÄĒ only fixes, tests, or documentation updates are allowed.

questions.question: What are the 3 questions to ask when deciding what to cut or postpone?
questions.answer:
1) Does this directly improve the user's core experience?
2) Can we test it in time?
3) Does it risk breaking something stable?
If no ‚Üí move to "Future Enhancements."

questions.question: What percentage of engineering time is spent diagnosing issues according to the slides?
questions.answer: 70‚Äď80% of engineering time is spent diagnosing, not coding.

questions.question: What is the Troubleshooting Mindset?
questions.answer: Be curious (not defensive), assume unknowns (not incompetence), verify assumptions with data (not anecdotes), treat symptoms as clues (not conclusions), keep notes.

questions.question: What are the 5 categories of software problems?
questions.answer: Code logic bugs, Integration issues, Configuration/Environment issues, Performance issues, Process/Team issues.

questions.question: What tools are used for integration issues?
questions.answer: Postman, Swagger, and mocks ‚ÄĒ to test API mismatches and version drift.

questions.question: What is Semantic Versioning (SemVer) and what is its format?
questions.answer: A versioning standard with format MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g., v2.4.6). MAJOR = breaking changes, MINOR = new compatible features, PATCH = bug fixes.

questions.question: What does each SemVer component mean?
questions.answer: MAJOR: breaking changes (users must update code). MINOR: new features, still backward-compatible. PATCH: bug fixes, no behavior change.

questions.question: What are SemVer pre-release suffixes?
questions.answer: -alpha, -beta, -rc.1 (release candidate). E.g., v2.0.0-beta.1 signals an unreleased, pre-production version.

questions.question: What are the four common versioning approaches?
questions.answer: Sequential (1, 2, 3), Date-based (2025.11.11), Build-based (1.0.1342), Semantic/SemVer (1.2.3).

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: WHAT IS AGILE? - The Agile Manifesto - 12 Principles
slideIndex.extracted_text:
WHAT IS AGILE? - The Agile Manifesto - 12 Principles
1)
Customer satisfaction through early & continuous delivery.
2)
Welcome changing requirements, even late in development.
3)
Deliver working software frequently (weeks, not months).
4)
Business + developers must work together daily.
5)
Build projects around motivated individuals, give them support & trust.
6)
Prefer face-to-face communication (or closest equivalent).
7)
Working software = primary measure of progress.
8)
Promote sustainable pace (avoid burnout).
9)
Pay continuous attention to technical excellence & good design.
10)
Simplicity ‚ÄĒ don‚Äôt build more than needed.
11)
Best architectures and designs emerge from self-organizing teams.
12)
Team regularly reÔ¨āects & adapts (retrospectives).

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: WHAT IS SCM? Source Control Management
slideIndex.extracted_text:
WHAT IS SCM? Source Control Management
Source Control Management (SCM), also known as
Version Control Systems (VCS), is a critical
component in modern software development.
SCM/VCS allows teams to track and manage
changes (and changers) to source code over time.

SCM - History
-
Early Systems (1970s-1980s)
-
Source Code Control System (SCCS), Centralized, Obsolete
-
Revision Control System (RCS), Centralized, Limited Availability
-
1990s: Concurrent Development
-
Concurrent Versions System (CVS), Centralized, Replaced
-
Perforce (Helix Core), Centralized, Actively maintained
-
2000s: Distributed Revolution
-
Subversion (SVN), Centralized,  Active but declining
-
Git, Distributed, Active (Most)
-
Mercurial, Distributed, Declining
-
Modern Platforms
-
GitHub, Cloud, Active
-
GitLab, Cloud/Self, Active
-
Bitbucket (Git and Mercurial), Cloud/Self, Active
-
Azure DevOps (Formerly VSTS/TFS), Centralized and Distributed, Active

SCM - Centralized vs Distributed - 1 of 2
Tools
SVN, Perforce, CVS
Git, Mercurial, Fossil
Repository Architecture
Single central repository
Every user has a full copy (clone) of the repository
Internet/Network Dependency
Always required for most actions
Only needed to push/pull; full
(e.g., commits)
local operations possible
Commit Behavior
Commits go directly to central repo
Commits are local Ô¨Ārst, then pushed to remote
Cloning
Not applicable; only checkout supported
Cloning includes full history and branches
OÔ¨Ąine Work
Limited; commits need network
Fully functional (commit, branch, log, diÔ¨Ä, etc.)
Branching
Limited, often slow or painful (SVN)
Fast and cheap; branches are Ô¨Ārst-class citizens
Merging
Can be more complex/conÔ¨āict-prone
Sophisticated merge handling (esp. in Git)
History
Stored only on server
Fully replicated on every client

SCM - Centralized vs Distributed - 2 of 2
Tools
SVN, Perforce, CVS
Git, Mercurial, Fossil
Speed
Slower; depends on network/server
Fast local operations
ConÔ¨āict Resolution
Typically server-side or at commit time
Handled locally before pushing
Backup/Redundancy
Single point of failure unless server is backed up
Every clone is a full backup
Ease of Auditing
Simple central log history
Can be complex due to local
histories
Security (by default)
Easy to enforce with central repo and ACLs
More Ô¨āexible but needs extra
enforcement tools
Scalability for Teams
Easy to manage with strict roles & access
Needs conventions and tooling
to manage Ô¨āow
Best For
Teams with stable, centralized infrastructure
Agile teams, open-source,
distributed workforces

SCM - Which To Use
Project Type
Recommended SCM Platform/Tool(s)
Open-source software
Git (with GitHub or GitLab)
Large binary assets (e.g., games)
Perforce
Small team projects
Git, Mercurial
Legacy enterprise systems
Subversion (SVN)
DevOps & CD
GitLab, GitHub Actions, Azure DO
High compliance/security
Self-hosted GitLab, Azure DevOps,
Perforce

SCM Terminology - 1 of 2
Repository (Repo)
A storage location for code, history, and branches.
Remote
A version of your repository hosted elsewhere (e.g., GitHub).
Origin
The default name Git gives to the remote repository you cloned from.
Local
 Your working copy of the repository on your machine.
Commit
A saved snapshot of your changes. Each commit has a unique ID and message.
Branch
A separate line of development. Default branch is usually main or master.
Clone
Create a local copy of a remote repository.
Pull
Fetch and merge changes from a remote repository to your local branch.
Push
Send your local commits to the remote repository.
Merge
Combine changes from one branch into another.
Rebase
Replay commits from one branch onto another. It rewrites history.

SCM Terminology - 2 of 2
Fetch
Download commits and refs from a remote without merging them.
Pull Request (PR)
A request to merge changes from one branch into another (usually used on hosted platforms).
Tag
A named point in history, often used for marking releases.
Checkout
Switch to another branch or commit.
Staging Area (Index)
Where you prepare changes before committing.
ConÔ¨āict
Happens when Git can‚Äôt automatically merge changes.
DiÔ¨Ä
Shows diÔ¨Äerences between commits, branches, or the working directory.
Fork
A personal copy of a remote repository, often used to contribute to open source.
Cherry-pick
Apply a speciÔ¨Āc commit from one branch onto another.
Squash
Combine multiple commits into one before merging.
Fast-forward merge
A merge where the target branch is directly updated without a merge commit.

GreenÔ¨Āeld - What is it?
‚óŹImagine ‚Äúgreen Ô¨Āelds‚ÄĚ of land
‚óŹStarting a project from scratch
‚óčno existing systems, codebases, or constraints
‚óŹDevelopers have complete freedom in
technology stack, architecture, and design

BrownÔ¨Āeld - What is it?
‚óŹImagine ‚Äúbrown  Ô¨Āelds‚ÄĚ of land
- Previously developed/industrial land
‚óŹWorking within or building on top of an existing
system, often with legacy code, infrastructure, or
organizational processes
‚óŹInvolves refactoring, modernizing, integrating, or
extending existing solutions

GreenÔ¨Āeld vs BrownÔ¨Āeld - Comparison
Factor
GreenÔ¨Āeld
BrownÔ¨Āeld
Starting point
Blank slate
Legacy code/system constraints
Flexibility
High - free choice of
Low - must respect existing integrations
stack, design
Speed to market
Faster for MVPs, slower to
Slower at Ô¨Ārst, but leverages existing base
reach maturity
Risk
Market risk (will it succeed?)
Technical risk (can we modernize safely?)
Skill demands
Strong architecture or
Strong debugging/refactoring/system skills
design skills
Typical org
Startups, new product teams
Enterprises, mature industries

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: GreenÔ¨Āeld vs BrownÔ¨Āeld - Who does what?
slideIndex.extracted_text:
GreenÔ¨Āeld vs BrownÔ¨Āeld - Who does what?
‚óŹ
Junior Developers
‚óč
More common in greenÔ¨Āeld (startups, new teams) - learning modern
tools, working under senior guidance
‚óč
BrownÔ¨Āeld is harder for juniors because it requires deep domain +
debugging knowledge
‚óŹ
Mid-level Developers
‚óč
Split between both. GreenÔ¨Āeld lets them learn ownership, brownÔ¨Āeld
teaches them to handle constraints
‚óŹ
Senior Developers
‚óč
More often placed on brownÔ¨Āeld because experience is needed to:
‚Ė†
Untangle complex systems
‚Ė†
Decide what to refactor vs replace
‚Ė†
Ensure minimal disruption to business

GreenÔ¨Āeld vs BrownÔ¨Āeld - Industry
‚óŹEstablished Industries / Enterprises (banks, airlines, insurance,
manufacturing, government)
‚óčVast majority of dev work is brownÔ¨Āeld (70‚Äď90%)
‚óčSystems are decades old, mission-critical, can‚Äôt be replaced
wholesale
‚óŹStartups / Scale-ups
‚óčMajority is greenÔ¨Āeld (80‚Äď90%)
‚óčBuilding new products, no legacy baggage
‚óčBrownÔ¨Āeld may appear if they pivot and need to integrate
with third-party/partner systems or refactor their own MVP

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: GreenÔ¨Āeld Development - What do developers do?
slideIndex.extracted_text:
GreenÔ¨Āeld Development - What do developers do?
‚óŹ
Designing architecture from scratch (monolith vs microservices,
cloud-native, etc)
‚óŹ
Setting up new infrastructure, CI/CD pipelines, and deployment
workÔ¨āows
‚óŹ
Establishing coding standards, style guides, and conventions
‚óŹ
Rapid prototyping and building MVP features
‚óŹ
Selecting tech stack, frameworks, and libraries
‚óŹ
Creating brand-new data models and schemas
‚óŹ
Writing documentation and onboarding guides from the ground up
‚óŹ
Heavy focus on innovation, speed, and feature delivery

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: BrownÔ¨Āeld Development - What do developers do? 1 of 2.
slideIndex.extracted_text:
BrownÔ¨Āeld Development - What do developers do? 1 of 2.
‚óŹRefactoring legacy code - improving structure, readability, and
maintainability without changing external behavior
‚óŹBug Ô¨Āxing & regression management - diagnosing and
resolving issues, often in poorly documented or fragile systems
‚óŹSystem integration - connecting old systems to new APIs,
services, or platforms (via adapters, facades, middleware)
‚óŹMigration work - moving from legacy frameworks, databases,
or infrastructure (e.g., on-prem ‚Üí cloud, monolith ‚Üí
microservices)

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: BrownÔ¨Āeld Development - What do developers do? 2 of 2.
slideIndex.extracted_text:
BrownÔ¨Āeld Development - What do developers do? 2 of 2.
‚óŹ
Performance tuning - proÔ¨Āling and optimizing bottlenecks in aging codebases
‚óŹ
Technical debt reduction - gradually replacing ‚Äúquick hacks‚ÄĚ with more
sustainable solutions
‚óŹ
Compliance & security patching - ensuring outdated systems meet modern
security, regulatory, or auditing standards
‚óŹ
Knowledge recovery - reading old code, reverse-engineering undocumented
functionality, or interviewing domain experts
‚óŹ
Dual-running / shadow deployments - running new and old systems in parallel
until transition is safe
‚óŹ
Incremental modernization - applying patterns like strangler Ô¨Āg to replace
modules piece by piece

Monolith - What is it?
‚óŹA monolith is a software architecture style where the
entire application is built as a single, uniÔ¨Āed unit
‚óčOne codebase
‚óčOne executable (or deployable artifact)
‚óčOne database schema (usually tightly coupled)
‚óčDeployed and scaled as a whole, not in
independent parts

Monolith - Characteristics
‚óŹTightly coupled modules and layers (UI, business
logic, data access)
‚óŹShared memory and resources - all features typically
use the same database
‚óŹSingle deployment - any change (small or large)
requires redeploying the whole application
‚óŹCentralized scaling - to handle more load, you scale
the entire application, even if only one part needs it

Monolith - When to use one
‚óŹ
Early-stage startups / MVPs
‚óč
Speed > scalability. A single deployable unit gets you to market faster
‚óŹ
Small teams with limited resources (Ex. Portfolio Projects)
‚óč
Easier to develop, test, deploy, and maintain with a handful of developers
‚óŹ
Applications with a simple, well-deÔ¨Āned domain (Ex. gym)
‚óč
Business logic isn‚Äôt complex enough to justify microservices
‚óŹ
Low scalability or traÔ¨Éc expectations (Ex, Internal tools)
‚óč
The system doesn‚Äôt need to handle millions of concurrent users
‚óŹ
Tight deadlines (Ex. School app)
‚óč
Monoliths allow fast iteration and feature shipping when time-to-market is critical
‚óŹ
Strong need for consistency (Ex. Banking)
‚óč
Monoliths avoid distributed data challenges (e.g., eventual consistency in microservices)
‚óŹ
Team skillset is not ready for distributed systems (Ex. First Custom Tools)
‚óč
Microservices demand more expertise in DevOps, CI/CD, observability, and scaling
‚óŹ
Applications that won‚Äôt evolve dramatically (Ex. IRS tools)
‚óč
If the scope is Ô¨Āxed and unlikely to expand, a monolith avoids over-engineering

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Monolith - When NOT to use one
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Monolith - When NOT to use one
‚óŹ
Large, complex domains (Ex. E-Commerce)
‚óč
When business logic spans many subdomains that evolve at diÔ¨Äerent speeds
‚óŹ
High scalability needs (Ex. Video Streaming)
‚óč
When diÔ¨Äerent parts of the system scale diÔ¨Äerently (e.g., checkout vs browsing)
‚óŹ
Frequent, independent deployments (Ex. SaaS product)
‚óč
If teams need to release features on separate cadences without waiting for others
‚óŹ
Globally distributed systems (Ex. Ride Sharing)
‚óč
When latency and availability requirements demand services close to diÔ¨Äerent regions
‚óŹ
Polyglot tech stack requirements (Ex. FinTech with Fraud Detection)
‚óč
When diÔ¨Äerent components need diÔ¨Äerent technologies (e.g., Python for ML, Node.js for real-time APIs)
‚óŹ
Critical uptime and fault isolation (Ex. Airline reservation)
‚óč
A bug in one module shouldn‚Äôt bring down the entire system
‚óŹ
Regulatory or compliance isolation (Ex. Patient Data)
‚óč
When speciÔ¨Āc services require diÔ¨Äerent audit, security, or compliance scopes
‚óŹ
Very large development teams (Ex. Big Tech - FAANG)
‚óč
When hundreds of developers work on diÔ¨Äerent areas simultaneously, monolith merges and deployments become
unmanageable

Technical Debt - What is it?
- Coined by Ward Cunningham
- Wiki, Agile Manifesto
- Tech debt is the future cost of quick decisions
today - choosing a faster, simpler, or dirtier
solution now means extra work later when
refactoring, debugging, or scaling

Technical Debt - Cost
- Slower development velocity
- More bugs
- Harder onboarding for new developers
- Deployment delays
- Cost

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Technical Debt in GreenÔ¨Āeld vs BrownÔ¨Āeld
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Technical Debt in GreenÔ¨Āeld vs BrownÔ¨Āeld
‚óŹ
GreenÔ¨Āeld
‚óč
Temptation to incur debt for speed-to-market (MVP)
‚óč
Can start clean, but if unchecked, the system quickly becomes
tomorrow‚Äôs brownÔ¨Āeld
‚óč
Example: skipping test automation to ship faster
‚óŹ
BrownÔ¨Āeld
‚óč
Legacy systems are accumulated tech debt
‚óč
Most brownÔ¨Āeld strategies (refactor, strangler Ô¨Āg, adapters) exist
to pay down debt while keeping the business running
‚óč
Example: migrating a monolith to microservices piece by piece

Strategies for Managing Technical Debt
‚óŹExplicit tracking - treat tech debt as backlog items, not
invisible chores
‚óŹPrioritization - not all debt must be paid; focus on
high-interest areas (where change is frequent or bugs are
common)
‚óŹRefactoring as routine - adopt Boy Scout Rule: ‚ÄúLeave the
code cleaner than you found it.‚ÄĚ
‚óŹTimeboxing - allocate a portion of each sprint (e.g., 10‚Äď20%)
for debt reduction
‚óŹGovernance - establish coding standards, CI/CD pipelines, and
architecture reviews to prevent uncontrolled debt growth

Technical Debt - Startup vs Industry
‚óŹStartups
‚óčOften accept high levels of debt to gain speed
(survival mode)
‚óčHope to pay it oÔ¨Ä after product-market Ô¨Āt or
funding
‚óŹEstablished industries
‚óčDebt is already massive and deeply embedded
‚óčPaying it down is costly but necessary for
reliability, compliance, and scaling

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Technical Debt in GreenÔ¨Āeld/BrownÔ¨Āeld - The Hard Truth
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Technical Debt in GreenÔ¨Āeld/BrownÔ¨Āeld - The Hard Truth
‚óŹEvery greenÔ¨Āeld project will eventually become
brownÔ¨Āeld
‚óŹTech debt always worsens if not actively managed
‚óŹThe diÔ¨Äerence between a startup and an enterprise is
often just the age and size of their debt
‚óŹThe most well-thought designs eventually degrade
with upgrades, evolution of software technologies,
etc

Technical Debt - Developer Roles
‚óŹ
Juniors
‚óč
Often contribute to debt unknowingly (lack of experience)
‚óč
BeneÔ¨Āt from working on greenÔ¨Āeld to learn best practices
‚óŹ
Mid-level
‚óč
Start recognizing debt and Ô¨Āxing small parts
‚óč
Learn trade-oÔ¨Äs between shipping vs maintainability
‚óŹ
Seniors
‚óč
Make conscious debt decisions (e.g., ‚ÄúWe‚Äôll skip X now, Ô¨Āx later‚ÄĚ)
‚óč
Decide which debt to pay oÔ¨Ä vs live with
‚óč
Lead large-scale debt reduction (brownÔ¨Āeld modernization)

GreenÔ¨Āeld Development - Approach & Best Practices
‚óŹArchitecture-Ô¨Ārst approaches (domain-driven design,
microservices, modularity)
‚óŹMVP (minimum viable product) mindset: ship quickly, validate
ideas, then iterate
‚óŹModern stack adoption (cloud-native, containers, serverless,
NoSQL, etc)CI/CD from day one, often with full test
automation
‚óŹRapid prototyping ‚Üí pivot-friendly
‚óŹRisks: over-engineering, underestimating scale, lack of legacy
domain knowledge.

BrownÔ¨Āeld Development - Approach & Best Practices
‚óŹ
Strangler Fig pattern (gradually replace legacy components with
modern ones)
‚óŹ
Incremental refactoring (improve critical modules without destabilizing
whole system)
‚óŹ
Adapters and facades to integrate legacy systems with new components
‚óŹ
Dual-run / phased migration (old and new in parallel until stable)
‚óŹ
Prioritized technical debt reduction (focus on high-value pain points)
‚óŹ
Risks: regression bugs, hidden dependencies, cultural resistance to
change

CODE QUALITY
Best Practices for Writing & Maintaining code

WHAT IS CODE QUALITY?
A measure of how well code adheres to standards,
practices, and maintainability principles

WHAT IS CODE QUALITY?
‚óŹInternal quality
‚óčstructure, readability, maintainability
‚óŹExternal quality
‚óčcorrectness, performance, security, usability

ATTRIBUTES OF CODE QUALITY
Readability - self-documenting, meaningful names, consistent style
Maintainability - modular, loosely coupled, easy to change
without breaking
Reliability - fewer bugs, predictable behavior
Performance - optimized but not at the expense of clarity
Testability - easy to unit test, mock, and verify
Scalability - supports growth without rewrites
Appropriateness - use of patterns suitable to the code‚Äôs intents

MEASURING CODE QUALITY
‚óŹStatic analysis tools (SonarQube, ESLint, Pylint, PMD)
‚óŹTest Code coverage - unit, integration, end-to-end test
coverage - TBD Later
‚óŹCode review metrics - number of issues found, time to
review
‚óŹComplexity metrics
‚óčCyclomatic complexity
‚óčMaintainability index
‚óčCoupling and cohesion

GOAL: Low complexity, high maintainability, loose coupling, high

CODE SMELLS
Indicators in the source code that suggest potential
deeper problems in design, maintainability, or
readability
They are symptoms, not necessarily bugs - the
program still works, but the design may be weak

COMMON CODE SMELLS - 1 of 2
‚óŹLong methods - more than one responsibility
‚óŹLarge classes, God Objects - more than one responsibility
‚óŹToo many parameters to functions
‚óŹDuplicated code - YAGNI, DRY, WET
‚óŹFeature envy (methods using more of another class‚Äôs data
than their own)
‚óŹShotgun surgery (tiny changes require edits in many
places)

COMMON CODE SMELLS - 2 of 2
‚óŹLack of domain-speciÔ¨Āc types (Ex. Email)
‚óŹMagic Numbers
‚óŹHard-Coded Values - Use conÔ¨Āgurations
‚óŹGlobal Variables
‚óŹData Clumps - maybe use a struct or class
‚óŹDeeply Nested Conditionals
‚óŹBad Comments
‚óŹJust-In-Case Functions

PRACTICES FOR MAINTAINING QUALITY
‚óŹCoding standards & style guides (PEP8, Google Java
style, Airbnb JS style, K&R/Allman)
‚óŹPeer reviews / Pull requests - catch issues early
‚óŹRefactoring ‚Äď continuous improvement of existing
code
‚óŹTest-Driven Development (TDD) - ensures
correctness from the start
‚óŹCI/CD pipelines with automated quality gates

Software Patterns
Understanding Software Patterns: From Code to Architecture

What are software patterns?
‚óŹReusable solutions to recurring problems
‚óŹNot code copy-paste, but generalizable templates
‚óŹExist at multiple levels:
‚óčlow-level (syntax)
‚óčmid-level (design)
‚óčhigh-level (architecture)

The spectrum of software patterns
‚óŹLow-level
‚óčfor-loops, searches, double loops (control-Ô¨āow
idioms)
‚óŹMid-level
‚óčObserver, Factory, MVC (design patterns)
‚óŹHigh-level
‚óčMicroservices, CQRS, Onion Arch
(architecture patterns)

Idioms vs Patterns
Concept

Why use software patterns?
‚óŹAccelerate development

‚óŹImprove maintainability

‚óŹAvoid reinventing the wheel

Design Patterns - By the GoF

‚óŹOriginally 23 classic OO patterns
‚óŹMost used:
‚óčSingleton, Strategy, Factory, Observer

Patterns vs Anti-Patterns
‚óŹPattern: solves a recurring problem eÔ¨Äectively
‚óčExample: Adapter Pattern
‚óŹAnti-pattern: appears useful but causes harm
‚óčPractices that get the job done
‚óčTheir adverse eÔ¨Äects are not always obvious or immediate
‚óčExamples:
‚Ė†God Object
‚Ė†Spaghetti Code
‚Ė†Copy-Paste Programming

Common Pattern Categories
‚óŹCreational - how objects are created
‚óčSingleton, Factory, Builder
‚óŹStructural - how classes relate to each other
‚óčAdapter, Composite, Decorator
‚óŹBehavioral - how classes interact with each other
‚óčStrategy, Observer, Command
‚óŹArchitectural - High-level separation of concerns
‚óčMVC, Microservices, Clean Arch, CQRS

Common Pitfalls / Misuse
‚óŹOverengineering with too many patterns

Choosing the Right Pattern
‚óŹWhat problem are you solving?

‚óŹAny platform conventions to follow?

Choosing the Right Pattern - When To Use a Pattern
‚óŹ
DO USE a Pattern When‚Ä¶
‚óč
You see the same structure or logic repeating in multiple places
‚óč
You want to decouple logic or layers (e.g., UI from business, services from
data)
‚óč
You need to make the code testable (especially with TDD)
‚óč
The implementation may change, but the interface should stay stable
‚óč
DiÔ¨Äerent behaviors should be swappable at runtime
‚óč
Multiple components need to communicate in a structured way
‚óč
You‚Äôre about to write a lot of if/switch statements for behavior
‚óč
You're working on a team, and a common structure helps collaboration
‚óč
You want your code to be self-documenting using well-known structures

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Choosing the Right Pattern - When NOT To Use a Pattern
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Choosing the Right Pattern - When NOT To Use a Pattern
‚óŹDON‚ÄôT Use a Pattern When‚Ä¶
‚óčYou just want to ‚Äúlook smart‚ÄĚ
‚óčYou just read about it and want to try it
‚óčYou only need the pattern in one spot
(overkill!)
‚óčThe pattern adds more code than it simpliÔ¨Āes

Patterns and Test-Driven Development (TDD)
‚óŹ
Many design/architecture patterns support testability:
‚óč
Dependency Injection makes it easy to swap mocks/fakes
‚óč
Strategy and Observer allow injecting behavior for testing
‚óč
Repository enables test-friendly data abstraction
‚óŹ
TDD drives cleaner architecture:
‚óč
Leads to smaller, modular functions
‚óč
Promotes single responsibility, one of the SOLID principles
‚óŹ
Patterns you‚Äôll encounter during TDD:
‚óč
Factory: to create testable components
‚óč
Adapter: to test third-party APIs
‚óč
Decorator: to add testable behaviors without modifying base

Patterns in Practice
‚óŹReal-world examples:
‚óčReact + Redux + Hooks
‚óč.NET Clean Architecture (CQRS + UoW +
Mediator)
‚óčAWS Lambda + API Gateway + EventBridge
‚óčFlutter BLoC with MVVM

Summary
‚óŹPatterns are tools, not rules
‚óŹBest patterns are the ones that solve your
problems well
‚óŹProjects can and will use multiple patterns
‚óŹKeep patterns simple and contextual
‚óŹAdditional Reading
