Testing and Test-Driven
Development (TDD)
Concepts, Methods & Practices

What is TDD?
‚óŹWrite tests BEFORE writing code
‚óŹFollows the Red-Green-Refactor (RGR) cycle
‚óŹFocuses on small, testable units of code
‚óŹTDD Ô¨āips the traditional workÔ¨āow, tests are not
an afterthought
‚óŹEach test corresponds to a speciÔ¨Āc requirement
or function

History of TDD
‚óŹRooted in Extreme Programming (XP)
‚óŹInÔ¨āuenced Agile and CI/CD practices
‚óŹFoundation established in 2002
‚óčKent Beck - ‚ÄúTest-Driven Development: By
Example‚ÄĚ

Why use TDD?
‚óŹForces you to think about requirements Ô¨Ārst
‚óŹImproves code quality and design
‚óŹReduces bugs and defects early
‚óŹTDD encourages clean separation of concerns
‚óŹDevelopers write only what‚Äôs needed to fulÔ¨Āll a
test

When NOT to use TDD?
‚óŹExploratory or prototype development
‚óŹHighly visual or UI-intensive applications
‚óŹFrequently changing requirements
‚óŹTDD can slow down initial exploration
‚óŹUse with caution when high uncertainty exists

‚óŹGood Fit

‚óŹBad Fit:

Types of Testing
‚óŹUnit - functions and methods
‚óŹIntegration - the entire stack
‚óŹRegression - the entire application
‚óŹStress/Load - push it to its limits
‚óŹEnd-To-End (E2E) - check diÔ¨Äerent workÔ¨āows
‚óŹUI/Usability - user experiences

Types of Testing - Unit Testing
‚óŹTests individual functions or methods
‚óŹFast and isolated
‚óŹCore to TDD - this is the central idea of TDD
‚óŹA good unit test should be independent and fast
‚óŹShould not rely on external systems
‚óčTest framework is usually part of the
development framework

The Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) Pattern
‚óŹA testing pattern for structuring test methods
‚óŹDivides each test into 3 clear sections
‚óčArrange - Set up test data and environment
‚óčAct - Invoke the function/method being tested
‚óčAssert - Verify the result or outcome
‚óŹImproves readability, consistency and debugging

Mocking and Stubbing - DEEP DIVE, Mocks in Practice
‚óŹUse mocks to test behavior, not just state

‚óŹ

‚óč

‚óč

Is delivery Ô¨Āxed or Ô¨āuid?

Fixed - WF, Fluid - Agile
‚óŹ
Do we need sign-oÔ¨Ä at every step?
‚óč
Yes - WF, No - Agile
‚óŹ
Cross Functional Teams?
‚óč
Yes - Agile, No -  WF
‚óŹ
Is Client/Stakeholder available during development?
‚óč
Yes - Agile, No - WF (only at beginning and end)
‚óŹ
Is Budget/Scope locked down?
‚óč
Yes - WF, No - Agile

Summary & Takeaways
‚óŹThere‚Äôs no ‚Äúone best model‚ÄĚ
‚óŹChoose based on the project, team, and
environment
‚óŹBe realistic - even the best model fails without
discipline and communication

SDLC & ALM
Exploring Roles, Theories, Practices, and Real-World Applications

Introduction
‚óŹWhy understanding SDLC and ALM is essential
‚óŹIndustry demand for lifecycle management skills
‚óŹFocus of this lecture: roles, concepts,
comparisons, examples, best practices

DeÔ¨Ānitions
‚óŹSDLC (Software Development Life Cycle)
‚óčStructured methodology for developing software.
‚óŹALM (Application Lifecycle Management)
‚óčBroader discipline encompassing the complete
lifespan of an application.
‚óŹSDLC is a subset of ALM.

SDLC at a Glance
‚óŹGoal: Deliver quality software in structured stages
‚óŹKey Phases:
‚óčRequirements ‚Üí Design ‚Üí Implementation ‚Üí
Testing ‚Üí Deployment ‚Üí Maintenance
‚óŹModels: Waterfall, Agile, Spiral, Iterative, V-Model
‚óŹExample: Building a food delivery app with weekly
sprints

ALM at a Glance
‚óŹGoal: Manage all aspects of the application from
inception to retirement
‚óŹStages:
‚óčIdeation ‚Üí Planning ‚Üí Development (SDLC) ‚Üí
Release ‚Üí Operations ‚Üí Governance ‚Üí Sunset
‚óŹALM is both a technical and business strategy
‚óŹExample: Managing Spotify‚Äôs entire music
recommendation pipeline lifecycle

Real-World Analogy
SDLC = Designing and building a car in the factory
ALM = Managing the car‚Äôs full lifecycle:
manufacturing, selling, servicing, upgrading, and
decommissioning

Example Scenarios
‚óŹSDLC Use Case
‚óčBuilding a simple GPA calculator app as a
semester project
‚óŹALM Use Case
‚óčManaging the entire Google Classroom
platform for K‚Äď12 education over a decade
‚óŹHybrid: Startups using SDLC but gradually
maturing into ALM as products grow

BeneÔ¨Āts of SDLC
‚óŹClearly deÔ¨Āned phases
‚óŹProject tracking and accountability
‚óŹQuality assurance through testing
‚óŹEasier onboarding of developers
‚óŹExample: Mid-size SaaS startup launching their
MVP

BeneÔ¨Āts of ALM
‚óŹEnd-to-end traceability and accountability
‚óŹBetter visibility for product managers
‚óŹEnables auditing and regulatory compliance
‚óŹSupports product roadmap planning
‚óŹExample: Managing a banking platform across
multiple regulatory regions

SDLC Phases Explained
‚óŹRequirement  Analysis: Meetings, surveys, SRS
documents
‚óŹDesign : System architecture, ER diagrams, UI
mockups
‚óŹImplementation : Coding by dev teams, peer reviews
‚óŹTesting : Unit, integration, regression, UAT
‚óŹDeployment : CI/CD pipelines, version control
‚óŹMaintenance : Bug Ô¨Āxes, patches, scalability
‚óŹExample: University project submission portal

ALM Components
‚óŹPlanning and Estimation (Backlogs, user stories, budgets)
‚óŹDevelopment & Build Automation (SDLC integration)
‚óŹTesting and QA (Integrated with CI/CD)
‚óŹRelease Management (DevOps tools, rollback strategies)
‚óŹOperations and Monitoring (SLA tracking, logging)
‚óŹCompliance & Governance (Audit trails, approvals)
‚óŹSunset Strategy (Archival, replacement plans)
‚óŹExample: Government tax portal maintained for 15+ years

ALM Documents
‚óŹ
RFI (Request for Information) - Used to explore options and gather preliminary input
‚óŹ
RFQ (Request for Quotation) - Requests pricing from vendors for well-deÔ¨Āned requirements
‚óŹ
RFP (Request for Proposal) - Solicits comprehensive solutions from vendors
‚óŹ
BRD (Business Requirements Document) - Captures stakeholder goals and business needs
‚óŹ
FRD (Functional Requirements Document) - Details speciÔ¨Āc system behaviors and functionality
‚óŹ
SRS (Software Requirements SpeciÔ¨Ācation) - Combines BRD + FRD + constraints + edge cases
‚óŹ
MOU (Memorandum of Understanding) - Documents informal agreements or collaborations
‚óŹ
Traceability Matrix - Maps requirements to implementation and test coverage
‚óŹ
Test Plans & Reports - Integral for QA and audits
‚óŹ
Change Logs & Version Histories - Required in regulated ALM systems
These documents ensure traceability, clarity, and compliance in regulated industries

SDLC
ALM

Dev process
Full lifecycle mgmt

Ends

Devs, QA, PM, Ops, Legal
Tools
IDEs, Git, Jira
Azure DevOps, GitHub
Enterprise, IBM ELM

Team Roles Involved
‚óŹSDLC
‚óčDevelopers, QA testers, UI/UX designers
‚óŹALM
‚óčProduct managers, business analysts, release
engineers, operations, compliance teams
‚óŹExample: E-commerce platform like Amazon
requires both ALM and SDLC teams

Role Spotlight - The Product Owner
‚óŹ
The Product Owner bridges the gap between business goals and the
development team
‚óŹ
Responsibilities:
‚óč
DeÔ¨Āne and maintain the product backlog
‚óč
Prioritize features based on value, urgency, and dependencies
‚óč
Clarify requirements and user stories for developers
‚óč
Accept or reject deliverables in sprint reviews
‚óŹ
Critical in Agile/ALM environments for aligning vision and execution
‚óŹ
Works closely with stakeholders, customers, and Scrum teams
‚óŹ
Real-world example: Managing feature roadmap for a mobile banking
app

Role Spotlight - The Technical Analyst
‚óŹ
The Technical Analyst serves as the translator between technical teams and
business stakeholders
‚óŹ
Responsibilities:
‚óč
Analyze and document functional and non-functional requirements
‚óč
Assess technical feasibility and constraints
‚óč
Create system speciÔ¨Ācations, workÔ¨āows, and data models
‚óč
Collaborate with architects, developers, and testers
‚óŹ
Often involved in both SDLC and ALM processes
‚óŹ
Helps ensure that business goals are met by technical implementations
‚óŹ
Real-world example: Specifying data Ô¨āows and system behavior for a logistics
optimization tool

Role Spotlight - The Business Analyst
‚óŹ
The Business Analyst (BA) focuses on understanding the business
context and aligning it with technical solutions
‚óŹ
Responsibilities:
‚óč
Gather and reÔ¨Āne business requirements from stakeholders
‚óč
Model business processes (e.g., BPMN, use cases)
‚óč
Help deÔ¨Āne scope, KPIs, and success criteria
‚óč
Collaborate with product owners, technical analysts, and QA
‚óŹ
BAs often operate at the intersection of ALM planning and early
SDLC phases
‚óŹ
Real-world example: DeÔ¨Āning workÔ¨āows for a claims processing
system in the insurance industry

Role Spotlight - The System Architect
‚óŹ
The System Architect deÔ¨Ānes the high-level structure of the software
system
‚óŹ
Responsibilities:
‚óč
Design system architecture and technology stack
‚óč
Ensure scalability, security, performance, and integration
‚óč
Guide design decisions across modules and services
‚óč
Collaborate with analysts, dev leads, and ops engineers
‚óŹ
InÔ¨āuences both SDLC and ALM, especially during planning and
design phases
‚óŹ
Real-world example: Architecting a microservices backend for a global
e-commerce platform

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Collaboration Flow - How Roles Work Together
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Collaboration Flow - How Roles Work Together
‚óŹBusiness Analyst starts by gathering and modeling business

‚óŹProduct Owner deÔ¨Ānes product vision and priorities based on
business input
‚óŹTechnical Analyst translates business and product requirements
into technical speciÔ¨Ācations
‚óŹSystem Architect designs a scalable architecture to fulÔ¨Āll those
speciÔ¨Ācations
‚óŹDevelopers build the system based on specs and architecture

Role Interactions
‚óŹBA ‚ÜĒ PO: Align business needs with product priorities
‚óŹPO ‚Üí TA: ReÔ¨Āne stories and clarify intent
‚óŹTA ‚ÜĒ Architect: Validate feasibility, constraints, data Ô¨āow
‚óŹArchitect ‚Üí Devs: Provide architectural guidance and patterns
‚óŹTA ‚Üí Devs: Deliver detailed specs, edge cases, validation rules
‚óŹDevs ‚ÜĒ PO: Demo features, request clariÔ¨Ācation, conÔ¨Ārm
acceptance
‚óŹOutcome: Seamless translation of business needs ‚Üí usable

Skeleton Crews & Role Substitutions
‚óŹ
No Product Owner?
‚óč
The Business Analyst or Scrum Master may act as proxy for product decisions
‚óč
Sometimes the Tech Lead takes on backlog prioritization
‚óŹ
No Business Analyst?
‚óč
The Product Owner may gather and reÔ¨Āne business requirements
‚óč
Technical Analysts may work more closely with stakeholders
‚óŹ
No Technical Analyst?
‚óč
The System Architect or Senior Developer often writes specs and API deÔ¨Ānitions
‚óŹ

‚óč

‚óŹ
Takeaway
‚óč
Teams adapt by sharing responsibilities

RACI Matrix - Responsibility Assignment Matrix
‚óŹRACI stands for:
‚óčR - Responsible: Who actually performs the task
‚óčA - Accountable: Who is ultimately answerable
(approver)
‚óčC - Consulted: Who needs to be kept in the loop
(two-way communication)
‚óčI - Informed: Who needs to be updated (one-way
communication)

A/R C   C    I     I    I
Analyze system requirements
C   C   A    R     I    I
Build and code
I   I   I    C    A/R   I
Test feature
I   I   I    I     C   A/R
Update stakeholders
R   C   I    I     I    I
R-Responsible, A-Accountable, C-Consulted, I-Informed

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Build vs Buy Decisions in SDLC/ALM
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Build vs Buy Decisions in SDLC/ALM
‚óŹ
The Build vs Buy question is a strategic consideration present in both
SDLC and ALM
‚óŹ
In SDLC:
‚óč
Decision made during Requirement Analysis or Planning
‚óč
Drives timeline, budget, scope, and developer workload
‚óč
Example: Build custom authentication system or integrate Auth0?
‚óŹ
In ALM:
‚óč
Impacts long-term integration, compliance, vendor lock-in,
support, and upgrades
‚óč
Evaluated during ideation, sunset, or re-platforming phases
‚óč
Tied to procurement documents: RFIs, RFPs, MOUs, SLAs

Factors to Consider:

Cost vs Time
‚óč

‚óč
Customization needs
‚óč
Maintenance and lifecycle cost
‚óč
Security, compliance, and scalability

SDLC and ALM in the Industry
‚óŹ
SDLC is practiced universally in all software development

Agile and DevOps are modern, popular SDLC variants
‚óč
Even small apps go through SDLC phases
‚óŹ

Summary & Key Takeaways

Full Stack Development
Best Practices Across the Entire Stack

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: The Modern Full Stack : A Complete View of Software Systems
slideIndex.extracted_text:
The Modern Full Stack : A Complete View of Software Systems
‚óŹWhat is the ‚Äúfull stack?‚ÄĚ
‚óŹPreview: front-end, middle tier, back-end
‚óŹWhy this matters for modern teams

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Why Full Stack Development Matters : Why - 1 of 2
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Why Full Stack Development Matters : Why - 1 of 2
‚óŹ
Holistic Understanding of Systems
‚óč
Full-stack developers can see how layers interact - UI triggers API, API hits DB, DB
returns results
‚óč
This reduces blind spots between teams
‚óč
They make better architectural decisions because they understand dependencies and
bottlenecks
‚óŹ
Improved Collaboration
‚óč
Knowing all layers helps developers communicate eÔ¨Äectively across front-end, API,
and database teams
‚óč
Easier debugging and integration - they can trace an issue end-to-end
‚óŹ
Faster Prototyping & MVP Delivery
‚óč
For startups and small teams, a single developer or small group can produce a
working end-to-end prototype quickly
‚óč
One person can spin up UI + backend + database locally, test the Ô¨āow, and validate
the concept

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Why Full Stack Development Matters : Why - 2 of 2
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Why Full Stack Development Matters : Why - 2 of 2
‚óŹ
Better Troubleshooting and Debugging
‚óč
A full-stack mindset allows a developer to trace bugs through the entire request-response lifecycle
‚óč
Example: An API error might originate in a UI assumption, or a UI slowdown might come from
an ineÔ¨Écient query
‚óŹ
Context-Aware Optimization
‚óč
They can identify where performance improvements matter most (e.g., database indexing vs
front-end caching)
‚óč
Helps avoid premature optimization or narrow focus on only one layer
‚óŹ
Flexibility & Employability
‚óč
Full-stack developers can plug into more roles
‚óč
In modern teams, even specialized devs beneÔ¨Āt from broad stack literacy
‚óŹ
Product Ownership Mindset
‚óč
FSD encourages thinking like a product engineer - not just a coder
‚óč
Helps bridge technical and business goals, as they see the system as a product, not a codebase

Full-Stack Developer - Then and Now
‚óŹOld DeÔ¨Ānition (2010s):
‚óč‚ÄúOne person who builds the entire web app - HTML/CSS,
JS, backend, and database.‚ÄĚ
‚óŹModern DeÔ¨Ānition (2020s):
‚óč‚ÄúAn engineer who understands and can navigate multiple
layers of the stack, even if they specialize in one.‚ÄĚ
‚óŹTeams are often composed of T-shaped developers (broad
across layers, deep in one)
‚óŹ‚ÄúFull-stack‚ÄĚ now includes DevOps, CI/CD, and sometimes
cloud knowledge (FSD ‚Üí Full Stack + DevOps)

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Before the Full Stack - Predecessor Technologies
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Before the Full Stack - Predecessor Technologies
‚óŹ1970s - 1990s
‚óŹMonolithic applications (one-tier)
‚óŹClient-server model (two-tier, n-tier)
‚óŹExamples:
‚óčMainframe terminals ‚Üí central servers
‚óčDesktop apps with direct DB access
‚óŹEarly web: HTML + CGI scripts

Rise of the Full Stack
‚óŹ2000s - 2010s
‚óŹEmergence of dynamic web applications
‚óŹNeed for separation: presentation, logic, data
‚óŹRise of JS frameworks and REST APIs
‚óŹBirth of ‚ÄúFull Stack Developer‚ÄĚ role (~2010)

What Does ‚ÄėFull Stack‚Äô Mean?
‚óŹA system with three primary layers:
‚óčFront-end: UI/UX, interaction
‚óčMiddle tier: business logic, API routing
‚óčBack-end: data access, persistence
‚óŹDeveloper capable of navigating all layers
‚óŹTypical skills: JS/HTML/CSS + API + SQL/ORM

Classic Three-Tier Architecture
‚óŹPresentation Layer (UI)
‚óŹApplication Layer (Logic)
‚óŹData Layer (Database)
‚óŹEmphasize: separation of concerns, scalability,
reusability

The Front-End Layer
‚óŹ
Responsibilities:
‚óč
User interface & interaction
‚óč
Input validation, rendering, accessibility
‚óŹ
Technologies:
‚óč
HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript/TypeScript
‚óč
Frameworks: Next.js, React, Angular, Vue, Svelte, Solid.js, Qwik
‚óč
For mobile: Flutter, KMP, React Native, SwiftUI
‚óŹ
Best Practices:
‚óč
Responsive design
‚óč
Accessibility (WCAG)
‚óč
Component reuse

The Middle Tier (Middleware/API Layer)
‚óŹ
Responsibilities:
‚óč
Business logic, authentication, orchestration
‚óč
Data transformation and validation
‚óŹ
Technologies:
‚óč
Node.js (Express, NestJS), .NET Core, Spring Boot, Django,
Laravel
‚óč
GraphQL / REST APIs
‚óŹ
Best Practices:
‚óč
Maintain statelessness
‚óč
Use DTOs and models consistently
‚óč
Version your APIs

The Back-End Layer (Data & Persistence)
‚óŹ
Responsibilities:
‚óč
Data storage, retrieval, consistency, transactions
‚óč
Often includes caching, indexing, replication
‚óŹ
Technologies:
‚óč
SQL: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server
‚óč
NoSQL: MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra
‚óč
ORMs: Dapper, PetaPoco, Prisma, EF/EF Core, NHibernate
‚óŹ
Best Practices:
‚óč
Normalize where appropriate
‚óč
Use migrations
‚óč
Secure data (encryption, least privilege)

Common Full-Stack Combinations (Web)
‚óŹMERN: MongoDB, Express, React, Node
‚óŹMEAN: MongoDB, Express, Angular, Node
‚óŹLAMP: Linux, Apache, MySQL, PHP

Mobile and Cross-Platform Stacks

‚óŹKotlin + Ktor (Android), KMP
‚óŹNote: Still follows front ‚Üí middle ‚Üí back
structure, even if hosted diÔ¨Äerently

Beyond Web/Mobile: Other Full Stacks
‚óŹGame Development: Unity (C#), Unreal (C++)
‚óŹEmbedded Systems: Firmware (C/C++), hardware

‚óŹMachine Learning Pipelines: Data ingestion ‚Üí

‚óŹIoT Stacks: Sensor ‚Üí Edge ‚Üí Cloud ‚Üí Dashboard
‚óŹ(These often have no ‚Äúfront/middle/back‚ÄĚ distinction

‚óŹAI/LLM: Python +PyTorch (or TensorFlow/JAX)

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Data Flow in a Full Stack Application - 1 of 3
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Data Flow in a Full Stack Application - 1 of 3
‚óŹ
Front-End ‚Üí Middleware ‚Üí Back-End (Request Flow)
‚óč
User Action: A user submits a form, clicks a button, or triggers an
event
‚óŹ
Front-End Layer:
‚óč
Captures input (e.g., name, email)
‚óč
Formats data (JSON or XML)
‚óč
Sends request via HTTPS (fetch, axios, etc.)
‚óŹ
Middleware Layer:
‚óč
Receives request (API endpoint)
‚óč
Validates input and applies business logic
‚óč
Queries database via back-end

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Data Flow in a Full Stack Application - 2 of 3
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Data Flow in a Full Stack Application - 2 of 3
‚óŹBack-End ‚Üí Middleware ‚Üí Front-End (Response Flow)
‚óŹBack-End Layer:
‚óčRetrieves or modiÔ¨Āes data in the database
‚óčReturns structured data (e.g., JSON)
‚óŹMiddleware Layer:
‚óčMay transform or sanitize response data
‚óčAdds metadata (status codes, pagination, tokens)
‚óŹFront-End Layer:
‚óčUpdates the UI with new state or conÔ¨Ārmation message
‚óčHandles success/error rendering

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Data Flow in a Full Stack Application - 3 of 3
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Data Flow in a Full Stack Application - 3 of 3
‚óŹExample:
‚óčFront-end: User enters credentials ‚Üí sends POST
/login
‚óčMiddleware: Validates ‚Üí calls AuthService ‚Üí checks
DB
‚óčBack-end: VeriÔ¨Āes user record ‚Üí returns JWT token
‚óčMiddleware: Attaches token ‚Üí sends response
‚óčFront-end: Stores token ‚Üí updates UI to "logged in"

Data Flow Summary
[ User Interface ]
‚Üď
[ API / Middleware ]
‚Üď
[ Database / Storage ]
‚ÜĎ
(Response travels back)
‚óŹ
Data always Ô¨āows down (user ‚Üí DB) and up (DB ‚Üí user)
‚óŹ
Middleware acts as translator and enforcer between UI and DB
‚óŹ
Each layer should have a single responsibility in data Ô¨āow
‚óč
UI = presentation
‚óč
Middleware = business rules
‚óč
Back-end = persistence

SPAs (Single Page Applications)
‚óŹDeÔ¨Ānition: load once, dynamically update via JS
‚óŹExamples: Gmail, Notion, Trello, Slack
‚óŹPros: speed, smooth UX
‚óŹCons: SEO, complexity, initial load size
‚óŹArchitecture:
‚óčFront-end : JavaScript framework (React, Angular,
Vue)
‚óčBack-end : REST/GraphQL API (Node.js, .NET,
Django, etc.)

PWAs (Progressive Web Apps)
‚óŹWeb apps that behave like native mobile apps
‚óŹFeatures: oÔ¨Ąine caching, push notiÔ¨Ācations,
installable
‚óŹExample: Spotify Web
‚óŹBeneÔ¨Āts: cross-platform reach
‚óŹBuilt on service workers + manifest.json
‚óčService worker intercepts network requests,
manages caching, push notiÔ¨Ācations
‚óŹF/E layer behaves like a native mobile or desktop app

When Layers Blur
‚óŹMiddleware merging into backend (e.g., Node
app connected directly to DB)
‚óŹBackend serving front-end (SSR frameworks:
Next.js, Nuxt.js)
‚óŹEdge functions / serverless: combines middle +
back roles
‚óŹGraphQL often bridges middle & back layers

Separation of Concerns Revisited
‚óŹKeep presentation, logic, and data layers distinct
‚óŹBeneÔ¨Āts: maintainability, scalability, team
specialization
‚óŹUse APIs to bridge layers cleanly
‚óŹAvoid ‚Äúgod‚ÄĚ components or monolithic routes

Front-End Best Practices
‚óŹKeep UI stateless where possible
‚óčLocal storage
‚Ė†Store minimal info
‚Ė†Treat as temporary
‚Ė†Encrypt / obfuscate sensitive data
‚Ė†Graceful fallback when not available
‚óŹUse centralized state management (Redux, Bloc)
‚óŹOptimize asset loading and lazy loading
‚óŹSecurity: XSS prevention, sanitize inputs

Middleware Best Practices
‚óŹFollow REST/GraphQL standards
‚óŹUse structured error handling
‚óŹAdd logging and monitoring (OpenTelemetry,
Winston, Serilog, nLog)
‚óŹUse middleware pipelines for auth, rate-limiting,
and CORS
‚óŹLOTS of security practices

Backend Best Practices
‚óŹUse migrations and schema versioning
‚óŹMonitor query performance
‚óŹSecure DB credentials via environment variables
‚óŹBackups and disaster recovery planning
‚óŹUse of READ replicas
‚óčETL specialized versions of data

Common Pitfalls Across Layers
‚óŹTight coupling between layers
‚óŹDuplicated business logic (front + middle)
‚óčBut ‚Äúduplicated‚ÄĚ models are OK
‚óŹNo version control between deployments
‚óŹIgnoring API contracts and schema validation
‚óŹLack of testing at integration points

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Caveats / Limits of Full Stack Development
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Caveats / Limits of Full Stack Development
‚óŹAs systems scale, specialization becomes critical
(e.g., dedicated DB engineers, DevOps, frontend
UX experts)
‚óŹFSD doesn‚Äôt replace specialization - it
complements it
‚óŹDepth is as important as breadth - ‚Äúknowing
enough about everything‚ÄĚ must be balanced with
real skill in at least one domain

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Where is Full Stack Development Useful?
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Where is Full Stack Development Useful?
‚óŹStartups / Early-stage projects
‚óčSpeed, Ô¨āexibility, minimal overhead
‚óŹAcademic / Teaching environments
‚óčBuilds systems thinking
‚óŹHackathons / MVP building
‚óčQuick iteration, no handoÔ¨Ä delays
‚óŹSmall business or freelance projects
‚óčBudget-limited teams, single developer delivery
‚óŹTechnical leadership roles
‚óčUnderstanding all moving parts aids decision-making

Summary
‚óŹFull-stack = holistic understanding, not
one-size-Ô¨Āts-all
‚óŹKey takeaway: clear boundaries, consistent
contracts, scalability mindset
‚óŹDiscussion prompts:
‚óčWhich stack does your project resemble?
‚óčWhere do your layers merge or blur?
‚óčWhat trade-oÔ¨Äs did you make?

Application Programming
Interface (API)
Evolution, Practices & Pitfalls
CMPS 3350-MM

What is an API?
‚óŹAPI (Application Programming Interface): A deÔ¨Āned
way for software systems to communicate
‚óŹPurpose: Encapsulates complexity and exposes
controlled functionality
‚óŹAnalogy: Like a restaurant menu - it lists what you
can request, not how the kitchen works

Why APIs Matter
‚óŹEnable integration between apps, services, and
devices
‚óŹPromote reusability and scalability
‚óŹDrive platform ecosystems (Google Maps, Twitter,
OpenAI, etc.)
‚óŹCore enabler of microservices and cloud-native
systems

Pre-Web ‚ÄúAPI-like‚ÄĚ Technologies
Before the web era, systems still needed to communicate - but mostly within local networks or
mainframes, not browsers
RPC (Remote Procedure Call) : 1980s‚Äď90s
Allowed one program to execute code (a ‚Äúprocedure‚ÄĚ) on another machine as if it were local
Foundation for many distributed systems
CORBA (Common Object Request Broker Architecture) : Early 1990s
Language-agnostic object communication standard; used by enterprise systems before web APIs
DCOM (Distributed COM) : 1990s
Microsoft‚Äôs distributed object model; similar to CORBA but Windows-specific
RMI (Remote Method Invocation) : Late 1990s
Java‚Äôs object-level network communication mechanism - early step toward networked APIs
Socket Programming : 1980s onward
Low-level communication (TCP/UDP) - building blocks for all later API technologies

A Brief History of APIs
1990s - CGI (Common Gateway Interface)
Early web forms ‚Üí Perl scripts
Each request spawned a process
Early 2000s - SOAP
Microsoft, IBM
XML-based, verbose but structured
Mid 2000s - REST
Roy Fielding‚Äôs dissertation
Simpler, stateless HTTP-based
2010s - JSON / RESTful APIs
Twitter, GitHub
Human-readable, easy integration
2020s - GraphQL, gRPC
Facebook, Google
Structured querying, binary transport

Predecessor Technologies
‚óŹ
CGI (Common Gateway Interface):
‚óč
Each web request launched a new process ‚Üí slow and
memory-heavy
‚óč
Enabled Ô¨Ārst dynamic web content
‚óŹ
XML-RPC:
‚óč
Early remote procedure call using XML payloads over HTTP
‚óč
Inspired SOAP‚Äôs standardization
‚óŹ
SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol):
‚óč
Used XML + WSDL (Web Services Description Language)
‚óč
Strong typing and contracts but verbose and brittle

REST Emerges
‚óŹDeÔ¨Āned by: Roy Fielding in 2000
‚óŹStands for: REpresentational State Transfer
‚óŹPrinciples:
‚óčStateless communication
‚óčClient-server separation
‚óčUniform interface
‚óčResource-based (nouns, not verbs)
‚óčCacheable responses
‚óŹCommon verbs: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE

REST Example
GET /api/users/42
‚Üí 200 OK
{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Ava",
  "role": "admin"
}
‚óŹUses HTTP methods to represent actions
‚óŹData format: usually JSON
‚óŹDesigned to be simple and predictable

REST Verbs and CRUD Operations
‚óŹWhat is CRUD?
‚óčCREATE/READ/UPDATE/DELETE
‚óŹREST verbs align naturally with CRUD semantics
‚óŹIdempotency:
‚óčGET, PUT, and DELETE ‚Üí idempotent (same result on
repeat)
‚óŹPOST ‚Üí non-idempotent (creates new entries each time)
‚óŹPATCH is partially idempotent, depending on
implementation

JSON - In a Nutshell
‚óŹ
Created by: Douglas Crockford (early 2000s)
‚óŹ
Name: JavaScript Object Notation
‚óŹ
Goal: Provide a lightweight, human-readable format for data exchange
between web clients and servers
‚óŹ
Context:
‚óč
At the time, XML was dominant (used by SOAP)
‚óč
Developers wanted a simpler alternative that was easy for browsers to
parse natively
‚óč
JavaScript could directly parse() JSON ‚Üí no extra libraries or schemas
required
‚óŹ
JSON became the universal ‚Äúlanguage of APIs‚ÄĚ because it bridged humans and
machines - readable by both, easy for systems to produce and consume

XML vs JSON
Problem (pre-JSON)
JSON Solution
XML was verbose and required
Concise and directly mappable to
heavy parsing
JavaScript objects
SOAP payloads had high bandwidth
Reduced message size and network cost
overhead
Type handling in XML was cumbersome
Naturally represents objects, arrays,
numbers, strings
Developers had to write boilerplate
Integrates seamlessly in browsers and APIs
parsing code

JSON Best Practices
‚óŹAlways use UTF-8 encoding
‚óŹStick to lowercase keys with consistent naming (snake_case or
camelCase)
‚óŹAvoid deeply nested objects - Ô¨āatten when possible
‚óŹUse arrays for collections, not objects with numbered keys
‚óŹInclude metadata Ô¨Āelds (e.g., "timestamp", "version") when
needed
‚óŹValidate using JSON Schema to enforce structure
‚óŹAvoid comments - JSON does not support inline comments

Beyond REST: Modern Alternatives
‚óŹGraphQL - Query exactly what you need, nothing
more
‚óčFlexible but can hide expensive queries
‚óŹgRPC - High-performance RPC using HTTP/2 +
Protocol BuÔ¨Äers
‚óčBinary, strongly typed, ideal for
microservice-to-microservice communication
‚óŹWebSockets - Persistent full-duplex connections
‚óčReal-time updates for chat, dashboards, games

API Paradigms Comparison

Weakness

Full Stack Perspective
‚óŹAPIs act as the middleware between frontend
and backend
‚óŹFront End (FE): React, Angular, Vue, Flutter
‚óŹMiddleware: REST or GraphQL APIs
‚óŹBackend: Databases, cloud functions, business
logic
‚óŹNote: duplicated models across FE and API are
acceptable ‚Üí but avoid duplicated business logic

API Best Practices
‚óŹUse clear, predictable naming
‚óčExample: (/users, /users/{id})
‚óŹUse plural nouns for resources
‚óŹVersion your APIs (/v1/)
‚óŹReturn appropriate HTTP status codes
‚óŹValidate input and sanitize output
‚óŹSupport pagination, Ô¨Āltering, sorting
‚óŹInclude metadata (e.g., total_count)

Authenticating REST APIs
‚óŹ
Authentication - ‚ÄúWho are you?‚ÄĚ
‚óŹ
ConÔ¨Ārms identity of the client or user
‚óŹ
Common methods:
‚óč
API Keys - simple but static; not ideal for user-speciÔ¨Āc access
‚óč
JWT (JSON Web Tokens) - digitally signed, compact identity
tokens containing claims (user ID, roles, expiry)
‚óč
OAuth 2.0 - delegated authorization (e.g., ‚ÄúSign in with Google‚ÄĚ)
‚óč
Bearer Tokens - access tokens included in headers (usually JWTs)

Authenticating REST APIs - Bearer Tokens
‚óŹ‚ÄúBearer‚ÄĚ means whoever presents the token is trusted - no
password required once issued
‚óŹSent in request headers:
Authorization: Bearer <token>
‚óŹThe token is veriÔ¨Āed by the API server (signature + expiry
check)
‚óŹOften short-lived; refreshed periodically for security
‚óŹRisk: if stolen, anyone holding it can access the API until expiry
‚óčUse HTTPS and short token lifetimes.

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Authorizing REST APIs - What can you do?
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Authorizing REST APIs - What can you do?
‚óŹDeÔ¨Ānes permissions after authentication succeeds
‚óŹCommon models:
‚óčRBAC (Role-Based): Admin, Coach, Student, etc
‚óčScope-Based: Token contains permissions like
read:events, write:comments
‚óčABAC (Attribute-Based): Contextual rules (time,
location, device)

REST APIs - Auth Flow Example
‚óŹUser logs in ‚Üí receives JWT or OAuth2 Bearer token
‚óŹClient sends:
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJI...
‚óŹServer validates ‚Üí checks user role ‚Üí grants or denies access
‚óŹBest Practice:
‚óčUse JWT Bearer Tokens for REST APIs
‚óčUse refresh tokens or rotating tokens for long sessions
‚óčNever expose tokens in URLs, logs, or client-side code
comments

Security & Auth Best Practices
‚óŹAlways use HTTPS
‚óŹEnforce authentication (JWT, OAuth 2.0, API
keys)
‚óŹApply rate limiting and throttling
‚óŹBe CORS-aware when serving APIs to external
domains
‚óŹLog & monitor all access
‚óŹNever expose internal error stacks or secrets

Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)
‚óŹProblem:
‚óčFE and API on diÔ¨Äerent domains ‚Üí browser
blocks requests
‚óŹSolution:
‚óčCORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin)
‚óŹConÔ¨Āgure speciÔ¨Āc allowed origins, not *.
‚óŹImportant: Security feature, not a bug!

API Documentation & Discoverability
‚óŹUse Swagger / OpenAPI for live documentation
‚óŹProvide examples for every endpoint
‚óŹInclude error codes and response samples
‚óŹKeep docs in sync with implementation
‚óŹGood docs reduce support and onboarding time

Common Pitfalls to Avoid
‚óŹDuplicated business logic between FE and BE
‚óŹPoor versioning ‚Üí breaking clients
‚óŹIgnoring idempotency in PUT/PATCH requests
‚óŹReturning inconsistent data shapes
‚óŹNot handling HTTP errors gracefully
‚óŹOver-fetching or under-fetching data

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 1 of 3
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 1 of 3
‚óŹ
Synchronous (Sync) Calls - The client sends a request and waits until
the server responds
‚óŹ
Behavior: Blocking - client cannot continue until a response
(success/error) is received
‚óŹ
Use Cases:
‚óč
Standard CRUD operations (create/read/update/delete)
‚óč
APIs where immediate results are needed (e.g., login, form
submission)
‚óŹ
Pros: Simple to implement and reason about
‚óŹ
Cons: Slower under heavy load; wastes time waiting for long
operations

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 2 of 3
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 2 of 3
‚óŹ
Asynchronous (Async) Calls - The client sends a request and does not
wait for the server to Ô¨Ānish
‚óŹ
The server acknowledges receipt and processes in background
‚óŹ
Client may receive:
‚óč
202 Accepted (work queued)
‚óč
Callback/Webhook when complete
‚óč
Polling endpoint to check status
‚óŹ
Use Cases:
‚óč
Long-running operations (video processing, Ô¨Āle uploads, data
analysis)
‚óč
Event-driven systems or batch jobs

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 3 of 3
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 3 of 3
‚óŹREST itself is transport-agnostic - sync vs async
depends on implementation pattern, not
protocol
‚óŹAsync designs improve scalability and user
experience
‚óŹCombine async REST with WebSockets, SignalR,
or message queues for real-time updates

API Gateways & Middleware
‚óŹProvide single entry point for multiple services
‚óŹHandles:
‚óčRate limiting
‚óčAuthentication
‚óčRouting
‚óčLogging / Metrics
‚óŹExamples: Kong, AWS API Gateway, Azure API
Management, NGINX

Testing & Mocking APIs

Real-World API Stack Example

Takeaways:

Advanced Design Principles
DDD, SOLID

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Advanced Design Principles: Domain-Driven Design & SOLID
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Advanced Design Principles: Domain-Driven Design & SOLID
‚ÄúDesign is not just what it looks like and feels like.
Design is how it works.‚ÄĚ - Steve Jobs

Why Design Principles Exist
‚óŹReduce complexity
‚óŹImprove maintainability and scalability
‚óŹEnable team collaboration
‚óŹAvoid ‚Äúbig ball of mud‚ÄĚ architecture

Part I - Domain-Driven Design - Origins
‚óŹIntroduced by Eric Evans (2003) in
Domain-Driven Design: Tackling Complexity in
the Heart of Software
‚óŹCore Idea: Let the business domain drive
software structure, not technology or database.
‚óŹDDD is about modeling business logic

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Domain-Driven Design - DDD is not a pattern
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Domain-Driven Design - DDD is not a pattern
‚óŹDesign patterns are speciÔ¨Āc, reusable solutions to
common software problems.
‚óčUsed for: Repository Pattern, Factory Pattern,
Observer Pattern, Strategy Pattern, etc.
‚óŹDDD (Domain-Driven Design) is a strategic and
architectural philosophy - it tells you how to
organize your entire system around your business
domain.
‚óŹDDD uses many patterns, but it isn‚Äôt itself one

DDD vs Patterns
‚óŹDDD is the city plan, while design patterns are
the individual buildings.
‚óŹDDD says how neighborhoods connect, where
roads go, and who owns what
‚óŹPatterns are how each building is constructed
‚óŹDDD isn‚Äôt a pattern you ‚Äėapply.‚Äô It‚Äôs a philosophy
that tells you which patterns to use, where, and
why.‚ÄĚ

What Is a Domain?
‚óŹThe domain = the problem space or area of
knowledge your software models
‚óŹExample Domains: Tennis Matches, Banking,
E-Commerce
‚óŹFocus on ubiquitous language: shared vocabulary
between devs and domain experts

DDD Building Blocks
‚óŹEntities ‚Üí objects with identity (e.g., Player, Order)
‚óŹValue Objects ‚Üí immutable, identity by value (e.g.,
Money, Coordinates) - Best to enforce semantic
typing
‚óŹAggregates & Aggregate Roots ‚Üí clusters of entities
treated as one unit
‚óŹRepositories ‚Üí abstract storage access
‚óŹServices ‚Üí operations that don‚Äôt naturally belong to
entities

DDD Building Blocks - SimpliÔ¨Āed
‚óŹDDD lives on patterns:
‚óčEntities and Value Objects deÔ¨Āne what exists
‚óčAggregates deÔ¨Āne ownership and control
‚óčRepositories deÔ¨Āne access
‚óčFactories deÔ¨Āne creation
‚óčServices deÔ¨Āne behavior
‚óčEvents deÔ¨Āne what just happened

DDD Building Blocks - Tennis Example
‚óŹEntity: Player
‚óŹValue Object: Score
‚óŹAggregate Root: Match
‚óŹRepository: IMatchRepository
‚óŹFactory: MatchFactory
‚óŹDomain Service: MatchValidatorService
‚óŹApplication Service: MatchAppService
‚óŹEvent: MatchCompletedEvent

DDD Building Blocks - Analogy 1
‚óŹ
Domain - The overall company‚Äôs business - e.g., ‚ÄúTennis Tournament Management Inc.‚ÄĚ
‚óŹ
Bounded Context - Each department - HR, Finance, Scheduling, Player Registration
‚óŹ
Entity - A speciÔ¨Āc employee or record with an ID (e.g., Player #104, Invoice #3721)
‚óŹ
Value Object - An attribute or description with no independent identity (e.g., Address, DateRange)
‚óŹ
Service - A cross-department task that doesn‚Äôt belong to one entity - like Payroll or MatchScheduler
‚óŹ
Repository - The Ô¨Āling cabinet or database each department uses to store its entities
‚óŹ
Ubiquitous Language - The shared company vocabulary everyone agrees on (‚Äúplayer,‚ÄĚ ‚Äúmatch,‚ÄĚ ‚Äúset‚ÄĚ)
‚óŹ
Notes:
‚óč
Each department (bounded context) owns its part of the business and data
‚óč
They talk to each other through clear contracts (APIs). Nobody directly messes with another
department‚Äôs Ô¨Āles
‚óč
‚ÄúDon‚Äôt let HR reach into Finance‚Äôs drawers to pull salary sheets - they must request a report!‚ÄĚ

DDD Building Blocks - Analogy 2
‚óŹ
Think of your system as a city:
‚óč
The city = the entire domain (e.g., E-Commerce)
‚óč
Each district = a bounded context (Inventory, Orders, Payments,
Shipping)
‚óč
Each district uses its own rules and vocabulary (e.g., ‚Äúparcel‚ÄĚ in Shipping,
‚Äúorder‚ÄĚ in Sales).
‚óč
They connect through roads (APIs / integration events).
‚óŹ
Example:
‚óč
When an order is placed in the Sales District, it sends a message down a
‚Äúroad‚ÄĚ to Shipping saying,
‚óč
‚ÄúPrepare a package for Order #123.‚ÄĚ
‚óŹ
Each district is autonomous, yet together they make a coherent city.

DDD Building Blocks - Analogy 3
‚óŹ
In Ô¨Ālm production:
‚óč
Domain experts = directors, writers, cinematographers (know the story)
‚óč
Developers = crew members who turn the vision into concrete work
(lighting, sound, editing)
‚óč
The script and storyboards = ubiquitous language - a shared reference
everyone understands
‚óč
Scenes, actors, props = entities and value objects
‚óč
Editors and producers = services coordinating across parts
‚óŹ
If one team starts calling the ‚Äúhero‚ÄĚ the ‚Äúlead role‚ÄĚ while another calls him
‚Äúmain actor,‚ÄĚ confusion spreads
‚óŹ
DDD Ô¨Āxes this by enforcing one shared vocabulary - the ubiquitous language -
in both the code and meetings

Layered Architecture in DDD
‚óŹPresentation Layer (UI / API)
‚óŹApplication Layer (use-case or command
handling)
‚óŹDomain Layer (business rules & entities)
‚óŹInfrastructure Layer (database, external APIs)
‚óŹOuter Depends On Inner

Ubiquitous Language
‚óŹShared vocabulary between developers + domain
experts
‚óŹExample:
‚óčBusiness: ‚ÄúA match has a score.‚ÄĚ
‚óčCode: Match.UpdateScore()
‚óŹPrevents translation errors and technical drift

Bounded Contexts
‚óŹSplit large domains into bounded contexts (e.g.,
Billing, Inventory, Shipping)
‚óŹEach has its own model and language
‚óŹConnect contexts via integration contracts / APIs
‚óŹThink ‚Äúsmall kingdoms that agree on trade
rules.‚ÄĚ

DDD and APIs
‚óŹEach bounded context ‚Üí potential microservice
/ REST API
‚óŹKeep domain rules inside domain layer
‚óŹAPI merely exposes behavior - never business
logic leakage
‚óŹAligns perfectly with CQRS & Clean
Architecture

Part II - SOLID Principles - Overview
‚óŹCoined by Robert C. Martin (‚ÄúUncle Bob‚ÄĚ)
‚óŹNot a framework
‚óŹNot a pattern
‚óŹNot a library
‚óŹNot a formal standard

Part II - SOLID Principles - Overview
‚óŹSOLID is a set of principles for designing better
Object-Oriented Software
‚óŹMeaning:
‚óčSingle Responsibility
‚óčOpen/Closed
‚óčLiskov Substitution
‚óčInterface Segregation
‚óčDependency Inversion

SOLID Principles - The Goals
‚óŹWithout SOLID, typical OOP projects often suÔ¨Äer
from:
‚óčTight coupling
‚óčHard-to-change code
‚óčFragile inheritance chains
‚óčMassive ‚ÄúGod classes‚ÄĚ that do everything
‚óčHidden dependencies
‚óŹSOLID provides Ô¨Āve design guardrails that prevent
these problems

S - Single Responsibility Principle
‚óŹA class should do one thing, and do it well
‚óŹExample Bad: UserService handles auth + email
+ logging
‚óŹExample Good: AuthService, EmailService,
AuditLogger

O - Open/Closed Principle
‚óŹClasses should be open for extension, closed for
modiÔ¨Ācation
‚óŹFavor abstraction + inheritance over editing
existing code
‚óŹExample: Strategy pattern for diÔ¨Äerent payment
methods

L - Liskov Substitution Principle
‚óŹSubtypes must be substitutable for their base
types
‚óŹDerived classes shouldn‚Äôt break base contracts
‚óŹExample: Square shouldn‚Äôt inherit Rectangle if
area behavior changes

I - Interface Segregation Principle
‚óŹNo client should depend on methods it does not
use
‚óŹCreate smaller, focused interfaces
‚óŹExample: split IWorker into IWork + IEat if
robots don‚Äôt eat

D - Dependency Inversion Principle
‚óŹDepend on abstractions, not concretions
‚óŹHigh-level modules shouldn‚Äôt depend on
low-level details
‚óŹExample: inject INotiÔ¨ĀcationService rather than
EmailService
‚óŹEnables unit testing and Ô¨āexibility

SOLID in Practice
‚óŹUse Dependency Injection frameworks (e.g.,
ASP.NET Core DI)
‚óŹKeep controllers thin, services focused
‚óŹDesign for testability and future change

Connecting DDD and SOLID
DDD Concept
SOLID Alignment
Entity/Value Object
SRP + LSP
Service Layer
OCP + DIP
Repository
ISP + DIP
Bounded Context
SRP at system level

Common Pitfalls
‚óŹOver-engineering (‚ÄúDDD everywhere‚ÄĚ)
‚óčExample - auth, logging, uploads, email
sending
‚óŹIgnoring ubiquitous language
‚óŹMisusing inheritance
‚óŹPoor interface segregation (too many methods)

Summary & Discussion
‚óŹSOLID makes code evolvable and testable, good
OOP
‚óŹDDD models the rules and the problem space,
not the database. It organizes why and where to
apply OOP
‚óŹCombined ‚Üí robust, Ô¨āexible architecture
‚óŹPrompt: Which principle is most often violated
in student projects? Why?

The Persistence Layer
How software remembers

Introduction
‚óŹPersistence is the art of making data outlive the
program that created it.

Why We Need Persistence
‚óŹWhen a program ends, its variables disappear
‚óŹTo ‚Äúremember‚ÄĚ between runs, data must live
outside memory
‚óŹPersistence means saving state - users, settings,
progress, messages, etc
‚óŹExample:
‚óčyour phone remembers your contacts after
rebooting

Everyday Examples of Persistence
Example
What‚Äôs Being Remembered
How It‚Äôs Stored
Game saves
Player progress
File on disk
Contacts app
People‚Äôs names & numbers
Internal database
Notes app
Text notes
Cloud or file
Web browser
History & cookies
Local files or
databases

Volatile vs. Non-Volatile Memory
‚óŹVolatile: RAM - disappears when power is oÔ¨Ä
‚óŹNon-volatile: Disk, SSD, Ô¨āash storage - survives
power loss
‚óčCloud Storage feels diÔ¨Äerent in this regard
‚óŹPersistence relies on non-volatile storage

What the Persistence Layer Does
‚óŹManages where and how data is stored and
retrieved
‚óŹConverts in-memory objects ‚Üí storable formats
‚Üí back again
‚óŹHandles saving, updating, deleting, and querying
information
‚óŹProtects upper layers from knowing how storage
works

Persistence in Layered Architecture
‚óŹUI ‚Üí Application ‚Üí Domain ‚Üí Persistence‚Üí
Infrastructure
‚óŹUI asks the Application layer to ‚Äúsave‚ÄĚ or ‚Äúget‚ÄĚ
data
‚óŹApplication calls a repository
‚óŹRepository hides whether data lives in a Ô¨Āle,
database, or accessed via API

Where the Persistence Layer Lives
[ Presentation Layer ]     ‚Üí UI, screens, controllers
[ Application Layer ]      ‚Üí Orchestration, service logic
[ Domain Layer ]           ‚Üí Entities, value objects, business rules
[ Persistence Layer ]      ‚Üí Data access, repositories, mappers
[ Infrastructure Layer ]   ‚Üí External systems (DB, Ô¨Āle, APIs)
‚óŹ
The persistence layer usually sits within or just above the infrastructure layer
‚óŹ
It‚Äôs the bridge between the domain layer (your business logic) and the
database or storage (physical persistence)
‚óŹ
How it works:
‚óč
Domain says ‚ÄúI need to save this user.‚ÄĚ
‚óč
Persistence says ‚ÄúGot it - I‚Äôll handle how and where.‚ÄĚ
‚óč
Infrastructure says ‚ÄúI‚Äôll actually do the saving and retrieving‚ÄĚ

Persistence vs Infrastructure Layers
‚óŹ
Persistence Layer
‚óč
Handle how data is stored and retrieved (long-term memory)
‚óč
Uses repositories, data mappers, ORM classes, entity-to-table
converters
‚óč
Relies on Domain Models and Infrastructure
‚óŹ
Infrastructure Layer
‚óč
Provide low-level technical capabilities your app needs to function
‚óč
Relies on OS, DB clients & connections, Ô¨Āle I/O, HTTP clients,
email services, queues, logging, external API clients

Persistence Without Databases
‚óŹExamples:
‚óčSave to text or CSV Ô¨Āles
‚óčSerialize objects to JSON
‚óčWrite to logs or conÔ¨Āguration Ô¨Āles
‚óŹStore to a cloud service or cache
‚óŹDatabases are just one way to persist data - not
the only way

From Files to Databases
‚óŹFiles: simple but limited (no structure, no search
speed)
‚óŹDatabases: structured systems optimized for
storing and Ô¨Ānding data
‚óŹThink of a database as a library
‚óčEach table = shelf
‚óčEach row = book (record)
‚óčColumns = book properties

The Idea of a Query
‚óŹA query is a question we ask the data store
‚óŹ‚ÄúFind all users with name = 'Alice'.‚ÄĚ
‚óŹDoesn‚Äôt matter if it‚Äôs a Ô¨Āle, spreadsheet, or
database - the idea is the same
‚óŹQuerying means Ô¨Āltering or searching persisted
data

Introduction to SQL
‚óŹ
What Is SQL?
‚óč
SQL = Structured Query Language
‚óŹ
A standardized way to store, Ô¨Ānd, update, and delete data in relational
databases
‚óŹ
Think of SQL as a universal language for ‚Äúasking questions‚ÄĚ to structured data
tables
‚óŹ
BeneÔ¨Āts
‚óč
Clear structure and rules
‚óč
Powerful Ô¨Āltering, sorting, and joining
‚óč
Standardized - works across many systems (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL
Server, Oracle)
‚óč
Easier to reason about relationships (e.g., users ‚ÜĒ orders ‚ÜĒ products).

Data Retrieval Process
‚óŹApp asks for data (‚ÄúGet all users‚ÄĚ)
‚óŹPersistence layer queries storage
‚óŹData is read, converted into objects
‚óčMay use deserialization
‚óčHydration
‚óŹApp receives usable objects
‚óŹPersistence is the bridge between stored data and
live objects

Repositories
‚óŹDesign pattern that centralizes, encapsulates, and
abstracts the logic required to access data sources
‚óŹWhat does a repository do?
‚óčAbstraction of Data Access
‚óčActs like an In-Memory Collection
‚óčCentralized Logic
‚óčEnhanced Testability
‚óčReturns Domain Objects

Why Separate the Persistence Layer
‚óŹKeeps code modular and replaceable
‚óŹEasier to switch storage type later (Ô¨Āle ‚Üí DB)
‚óŹAllows mocking during testing
‚óŹFollows SOLID:
‚óčespecially Dependency Inversion

What‚Äôs Inside the Persistence Layer
‚óŹRepositories
‚óŹData mappers
‚óŹConnection logic (if using DBs)
‚óŹData format converters (CSV, JSON, XML)
‚óčSerialization / Deserialization
‚óčData Transforms

Databases and ORMs
‚óŹDBs handle large data, relationships, and
concurrency
‚óŹORMs map objects ‚ÜĒ tables
‚óŹYou will revisit this when you take a database
class or advanced module
‚óŹFor now, focus on the concept of persistence, not
the tools

Common Mistakes
‚óŹMixing Ô¨Āle I/O directly in business logic
‚óŹStoring data in memory only
‚óŹExposing raw storage details to higher layers
‚óŹForgetting to handle read/write errors
‚óŹUnder or over-retrieving data sets

Persistence - Summary
‚óŹPersistence = memory that survives program
shutdown
‚óŹThe persistence layer isolates storage concerns
‚óŹYou can persist data in Ô¨Āles, databases, or other
systems
‚óŹLater courses will teach databases - today you
understand why they exist

Challenges of Persistence in Databases
Why ‚Äúremembering things‚ÄĚ at scale isn‚Äôt as simple
as saving a Ô¨Āle

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Challenge #1: Data Models Don‚Äôt Always Match
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Challenge #1: Data Models Don‚Äôt Always Match
‚óŹPrograms store data as objects (like structs or classes)
‚óŹDatabases store tables and rows (like spreadsheets)
‚óŹMatching one to the other is not always easy
‚óŹExample:
‚óčUser object with a list of Friends doesn‚Äôt Ô¨Āt neatly into
one table
‚óŹSolution:
‚óčUse mapping logic (manual or ORM tools) to
translate between objects and tables

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Challenge #2: Many Users at the Same Time
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Challenge #2: Many Users at the Same Time
‚óŹSeveral people can read or write the same data at
once
‚óŹWithout control, one person‚Äôs update can erase
another‚Äôs
‚óŹSolution:
‚óčUse transactions or locking so operations happen
safely and in order
‚óčThink of a ‚Äúbusy checkout line‚ÄĚ - one person
Ô¨Ānishes before the next starts

Challenge #3: Keeping Data Reliable (ACID)
To keep data correct, databases follow the ACID principles:
Letter
Meaning Simple
Explanation
A ‚Äď Atomicity
All or nothing
Either all steps happen or none do
C ‚Äď Consistency
Always valid
Data follows the rules (no negatives,
no duplicates)
I ‚Äď Isolation
No interference
One user‚Äôs change doesn‚Äôt mess up another‚Äôs
D ‚Äď Durability
It stays saved
Once conÔ¨Ārmed, it won‚Äôt disappear
even if power fails
ACID = the promise that data is safe, consistent, and complete.

Challenge #4: Performance and Speed
‚óŹMore data ‚Üí slower reads and writes
‚óŹSearching big tables can take time
‚óŹSolutions:
‚óčAdd indexes (like book indexes)
‚óčStore frequent results in cache
‚óčFetch only what‚Äôs needed
‚óčCreate replicas (mirrors, read-only)

Challenge #5: Growth and Scaling
‚óŹAs users or data grow, a single machine may not be
enough
‚óŹExample: one Ô¨Āle cabinet turns into a whole room
‚óŹSolutions:
‚óčVertical scaling: bigger machine
‚óčHorizontal scaling: split data across several
‚óčUse connection pooling so multiple users share
resources eÔ¨Éciently

Challenge #6: Data Changes Over Time
‚óŹApplications evolve - new features need new
Ô¨Āelds or tables
‚óŹOld data may not Ô¨Āt new structures
‚óŹSolutions:
‚óčPlan schema changes carefully
‚óčUse migration scripts to update stored data
gradually
‚óčVersion changes to keep things consistent

Challenge #7: Security and Safety
‚óŹDatabases hold sensitive data - passwords, emails,
IDs
‚óŹA careless query or backup can expose everything
‚óŹSolutions:
‚óčEncrypt sensitive data or de-identify
‚óčRestrict access with permissions
‚óčStore passwords as hashes, never plain text
‚óčBack up data regularly

Challenge #8: Backups and Failures
‚óŹHardware crashes, bugs, or power loss can
destroy data
‚óŹSolutions:
‚óčRegular backups and restore tests
‚óčUse replication - copies of the same data on
other machines
‚óčRecover automatically after crashes (durability
again!)

Challenge #9: Balancing Simplicity and Power
‚óŹSimple designs are easy to build but may not
scale
‚óŹComplex ones scale but are harder to manage
‚óŹRule of Thumb:
‚óčStart simple, then evolve your persistence
strategy as your app grows
‚óčChoose the right type of database persistence
‚Ė†RDBMS, NoSQL, Other?

Who uses what?

Google
‚óč
Bigtable (wide-column),  Spanner (global-scale SQL)
‚óč
For massive scale: web indexing, global transactions, multi-region data
‚óŹ

‚óč

‚óŹ

‚óč

‚óŹ

‚óč

Column-oriented real-time analytics DB: ClickHouse or derived variant for analytics/warehouse
‚óŹ
Amazon
‚óč

Synchronous vs Asynchronous
APIs, Databases, and Concurrency - How systems handle tasks,
timing, and teamwork

Intro
‚óŹ‚Äúsynchronous‚ÄĚ and ‚Äúasynchronous‚ÄĚ are
fundamental patterns that shape how software
behaves, scales, and responds

What Is ‚ÄúSynchronous‚ÄĚ?
‚óŹTasks run one after another
‚óŹEach must Ô¨Ānish before the next starts
‚óŹSimple, predictable, but can be slow
‚óŹAnalogy: Waiting in line - one customer at a time

What Is ‚ÄúAsynchronous‚ÄĚ?
‚óŹTasks can start, then Ô¨Ānish later
‚óŹWork continues while waiting
‚óŹImproves responsiveness and eÔ¨Éciency
‚óŹAnalogy: Starting your laundry - you can do
other things while it‚Äôs washing

Comparing Sync vs Async

Synchronous

Blocking vs Non-Blocking
‚óŹBlocking: program halts until a task completes
‚óŹNon-blocking: can start other work while waiting
‚óŹAnalogy: One chef cooks while others wait
(blocking) vs everyone multitasking
(non-blocking)

AJAX: Asynchronous Web Requests
‚óŹAJAX = Asynchronous JavaScript and XML (today,
JSON)
‚óčUsed XMLHttpRequest (Response is XML)
‚óŹFetch data without reloading the page
‚óŹHas evolved to use fetch() (or Axios, React Query)
Example:
fetch("/api/data").then(r =>  r.json()).then(showData);
‚óŹAnalogy: Ask waiter for dessert while still eating dinner

What Is a Thread?
‚óŹA thread is the smallest unit of execution inside a
program
‚óŹThreads share the same memory, enabling fast
communication -  but risking conÔ¨āicts
‚óŹAnalogy:
‚óčA process is a restaurant; each thread is a chef
sharing the same kitchen

Single vs Multi-Threaded Processes
‚óŹ
Single-Threaded

‚óč

‚óč

‚óč

‚óŹ
Multi-Threaded
‚óč

‚óč

‚óč

‚óč
Example: Java, C#, C++, Python threads
‚óč
Analogy: Multiple chefs - faster, but collisions possible

‚óŹ
Async reuses one thread eÔ¨Éciently; multi-threading runs true parallel tasks

WebSockets: Persistent Async Connections
‚óŹMaintains a continuous two-way connection
‚óŹServer can push data anytime
‚óŹUsed in chat, games, live tracking
‚óŹAnalogy: A phone call instead of

‚óŹExamples: SignalR + .NET, Node.js + Socket.IO,

Async in Modern JS Frameworks

‚óŹReact - Async hooks (useEÔ¨Äect, Suspense)
‚óŹAngular - Observables (RxJS)

‚óŹTakeaway: Async design keeps modern UIs fast

Message Brokers & Pub/Sub
‚óŹEnable asynchronous communication between

‚óŹQueue: one-to-one. Pub/Sub: one-to-many
‚óŹExamples: RabbitMQ, Kafka, AWS SQS
‚óŹAnalogy: A post oÔ¨Éce - sender and receiver don‚Äôt

Preventing Race Conditions
‚óŹLocks / Mutexes - only one thread at a time
‚óŹAtomic Operations - all-or-nothing
‚óŹImmutability - don‚Äôt modify shared data
‚óŹMessage Passing - communicate instead of

‚óŹTransactions (ACID) - database safety
‚óŹSingle Event Loop - Node.js avoids data races
‚óŹTakeaway: Safety by design, not by chance

Advanced Async Patterns
‚óŹWhen to Work, Not Just How to Work

‚óŹExamples:
‚óčLazy loading ‚Üí only when needed

‚óŹAnalogy: A smart chef preps ingredients just in time

Summary: From Sync to Safe Concurrency
‚óŹSynchronous - Simple, sequential, blocking

Cross-Platform Development
Native, Virtual, and Hybrid Worlds

Introduction
‚óŹ‚ÄúHow software runs everywhere - from Java to WebAssembly.‚ÄĚ

The Big Question
‚óŹ‚ÄúWhy can‚Äôt one program just run everywhere?‚ÄĚ
‚óŹTargets: Windows, macOS, Android, iOS, Linux,
etc
‚óŹMotivation: reduce cost, unify code, speed
delivery.

The History of Portability
‚óŹ1970s - 80s: assembly ‚Üí hardware-speciÔ¨Āc
‚óŹC enabled ‚Äúcompile anywhere.‚ÄĚ
‚óŹ1995: Java & JVM ‚Üí Write once, run anywhere
‚óŹ2000s: .NET CLR, Flash, Qt
‚óŹ2010s - present: Flutter, React Native, WebAssembly
‚óŹTimeline: Assembly ‚Üí C ‚Üí Java ‚Üí .NET ‚Üí Flutter
‚Üí WASM

Native vs Cross-Platform
Aspect

‚óŹ
Examples:

‚óŹ

Bytecode, Native, and Interpreted
‚óŹNative: Compiled directly for each CPU (C/C++)
‚óŹBytecode: Intermediate form (Java, .NET)
executed by VM
‚óŹInterpreted: Read line-by-line (Python, JS)
‚óŹHybrid (JIT/AOT): Compiles some code at
runtime for speed

The JVM and Its Legacy
‚óŹJVM executes platform-neutral bytecode
‚óŹSupports Java, Kotlin, Scala, Groovy
‚óŹInspired:
‚óč.NET CLR ‚Üí Intermediate Language (IL)
‚óčAndroid ART/Dalvik ‚Üí mobile VM
‚óčLLVM IR ‚Üí universal compiler backend
‚óŹProgression: ‚ÄúLanguage ‚Üí Bytecode ‚Üí JVM ‚Üí
Native Execution.‚ÄĚ

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: How a Virtual Machine Enables Portability
slideIndex.extracted_text:
How a Virtual Machine Enables Portability
‚óŹVM = abstraction between app & hardware
‚óŹExecutes the same bytecode everywhere
‚óŹPlatform-speciÔ¨Āc VM implementations handle OS
details
‚óŹExamples: JVM, CLR, ART, WASM runtimes
‚óŹLayers:

OS/CPU

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Electron: Web Tech for the Desktop

Electron: Web Tech for the Desktop

‚óŹWrite in JS/HTML/CSS, package once, run everywhere

Notion, Postman, Figma
‚óŹHTML/CSS/JS ‚Üí Electron (Chromium + Node) ‚Üí
Windows/macOS/Linux Takeaway: ‚ÄúElectron turns the
web into a desktop platform.‚ÄĚ

Electron: Pros & Cons
‚óŹ
Advantages:
‚óč
One codebase (web stack)

‚óč
‚ÄúElectron trades eÔ¨Éciency for accessibility.‚ÄĚ

Challenges of Cross-Platform

When to Choose Cross-Platform

Refactor vs Rewrite
Fix the current system, or build a new one?

Objectives
‚óŹDeÔ¨Āne refactoring and rewriting
‚óŹIdentify when each is appropriate
‚óŹCompare their risks, costs, and outcomes
‚óŹRecognize technical debt symptoms
‚óŹEvaluate real-world case studies

What Is Refactoring?
‚óŹRefactoring = improving the internal structure of
code without changing its external behavior
‚óŹExamples:
‚óčSimplify complex functions
‚óčRename confusing variables
‚óčExtract classes or methods
‚óčAdd tests for safety
‚óŹ‚ÄúClean up the house without moving out.‚ÄĚ

What Is Rewriting?
‚óŹRewriting = rebuilding the system from scratch
(often with new tech or design principles)
‚óŹExamples:
‚óčReplacing PHP backend with Node.js
‚óčMigrating monolith ‚Üí microservices
‚óčSwitching from desktop app ‚Üí web app
‚óŹ‚ÄúDemolish and rebuild - same address, new
foundation.‚ÄĚ

Why Software Gets Refactored
‚óŹCode smells: long methods, duplication, tangled
logic
‚óŹHard to add new features safely
‚óŹPoor readability or inconsistent style
‚óŹNo tests or unreliable tests
‚óŹGradual performance degradation
‚óŹGoal: Incremental improvement without
breaking behavior

Why Teams Rewrite
‚óŹTech stack is obsolete
‚óŹArchitecture can‚Äôt scale or evolve
‚óŹLegacy frameworks / security issues
‚óŹTeam no longer understands old code
‚óŹIntegrating new platforms or APIs
‚óŹGoal: Modernization and future-prooÔ¨Āng

Decision Framework
‚óŹDoes it mostly work but looks ugly? ‚Üí Refactor
‚óŹIs it slower to Ô¨Āx than rebuild? ‚Üí Rewrite
‚óŹIs the tech stack dead or unsupported? ‚Üí Rewrite
‚óŹIs it harder to Ô¨Ānd people to support the existing
code? ‚Äď Which?
‚óŹCan you safely test improvements? ‚Üí Refactor
‚óŹDo you have tight deadlines? ‚Üí Refactor
‚óŹ‚ÄúIf it‚Äôs ugly but alive, don‚Äôt kill it.‚ÄĚ

Introducing Microservices (SOA)
‚óŹRefactor or Rewrite - into What?
‚óŹMany teams face this next-level question:
‚óčShould our refactor or rewrite move us toward
microservices?
‚óčMicroservices = small, independent services that
communicate via APIs
‚óŹThe challenge: deciding how to get there - incremental refactor
or full rewrite
‚óŹ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs not just whether to change - but how to evolve
architecture.‚ÄĚ

Refactoring Toward Microservices
‚óŹRefactoring into Microservices means:
‚óčExtracting parts of a working monolith into separate
services
‚óčKeeping business logic intact but improving boundaries
‚óŹUsing techniques like:
‚óčBounded Contexts (DDD)
‚óčAPI Gateways
‚óčThe Strangler Fig pattern
‚óŹIncremental evolution - modernize one module at a time

Rewriting as Microservices
‚óŹRewriting into Microservices means:
‚óčStarting fresh with a distributed architecture
‚óčRedesigning domains, APIs, and data ownership
‚óŹOften includes:
‚óčNew frameworks (e.g., .NET ‚Üí Node + Docker)
‚óčCloud-Ô¨Ārst deployment (Kubernetes, Azure,
AWS)
‚óčIndependent CI/CD per service
‚óŹHigh cost, high risk - but high potential scalability

Refactor vs Rewrite: Microservices Edition

When Refactoring Works Best
‚óŹYou can identify natural seams (User Auth,
Payments, NotiÔ¨Ācations)
‚óŹThe monolith is stable and testable
‚óŹYou can build new features as new services
‚óŹYour team can manage multiple deployments
‚óŹStart small - build new modules as services and
connect them gradually

When a Rewrite Is JustiÔ¨Āed
‚óŹThe monolith is:
‚óčOn an obsolete framework or language
‚óčImpossible to deploy or test safely
‚óčIntertwined across UI, logic, and data layers
‚óčBlocking scalability or modernization
‚óŹIf the system can‚Äôt evolve, replacing it may be the
only option

The ‚ÄúStrangler Fig‚ÄĚ Pattern
‚óŹGradually replace pieces of a monolith:
‚óčBuild a new service for one area
‚óčRedirect relevant requests to it
‚óčSlowly retire the old code
‚óŹAdvantages:
‚óčMinimal downtime
‚óčReversible steps
‚óčEasier stakeholder buy-in
‚óŹEvolution over revolution

Warning Signs for Each
‚óŹRefactor needed when:
‚óčAdding features gets slower
‚óčBugs multiply in one area
‚óčCode reviews take forever
‚óŹRewrite needed when:
‚óčCan‚Äôt deploy or test anymore
‚óčDependencies are abandoned
‚óčBusiness logic is entangled in UI

Beyond the Code: Organizational Factors

Considerations

Do you have DevOps and distributed-systems expertise?

Key Takeaways
‚óŹRefactor ‚Üí evolutionary; Rewrite ‚Üí revolutionary
‚óŹMicroservices magnify both beneÔ¨Āts and risks
‚óŹUse refactoring to migrate safely and learn the
boundaries
‚óŹRewrite only when the monolith blocks all progress
‚óŹCommunicate trade-oÔ¨Äs clearly to technical and
non-technical stakeholders
‚óŹ‚ÄúRefactor for growth. Rewrite for survival.‚ÄĚ

Case Study: Netscape - REWRITE FAIL
‚óŹNetscape 4 ‚Üí complete rewrite (‚ÄúNetscape 6‚ÄĚ)
‚óŹLost 2 years in rewrite
‚óŹInternet Explorer took over market share
‚óŹLesson: Full rewrites can be fatal if timing
matters

Case Study: Twitter - REFACTOR SUCCESS
‚óŹOriginally Ruby on Rails monolith
‚óŹGradually refactored into services
‚óŹScaled smoothly without halting development
‚óŹLesson: Incremental refactor can succeed

‚óŹ

Takeaway:

Rewrite justiÔ¨Āed when business goals fundamentally change

Case Study: Uber - REWRITE FAILURE-ish
‚óŹWhat happened:
‚óčAfter the successful rewrite into microservices (2014‚Äď2017),
Uber ended up with over 2,000 services, many with
overlapping responsibilities
‚óčDependency chaos, inconsistent observability, and ‚Äúservice
hell‚ÄĚ made debugging and deployment painful.
‚óŹOutcome:
‚óčBegan a re-centralization eÔ¨Äort - ‚Äúmicro-monoliths‚ÄĚ and

Refactor ‚Č† Microservices
‚óŹ
Refactor doesn‚Äôt always mean break apart - it can also mean tighten up
the existing system
‚óŹ
Many teams refactor toward a cleaner monolith, not microservices
‚óŹ
Problems often come from messy design, not monolithic deployment
‚óŹ
A well-structured monolith can be:
‚óč
Modular and layered
‚óč
Testable and maintainable
‚óč
Horizontally scalable
‚óŹ
‚ÄúMonolith‚ÄĚ isn‚Äôt a bad word - ‚Äúspaghetti‚ÄĚ is

The Modular (or Clean) Monolith
‚óŹ
Refactoring goals inside a monolith:
‚óč
Separate clear modules/domains (User, Orders, Billing)
‚óč
Add clean architecture layers: UI ‚Üí Application ‚Üí Domain ‚Üí
Infrastructure
‚óč
Enforce internal boundaries via interfaces
‚óč
Improve test coverage and dependency injection
‚óč
Introduce CQRS, event patterns, or feature toggles
‚óŹ
BeneÔ¨Āts:
‚óč
Simple deployments
‚óč
Lower operational cost
‚óč
Shared context and faster development
‚óč
Many microservice beneÔ¨Āts - without network overhead

Real-World ‚ÄúBetter Monoliths‚ÄĚ

Lesson

Modular Monoliths - FAILS
‚óŹ
Wordpress.com / Automattic (PHP Monolith Fatigue)

Decades-old PHP codebase (shared by self-hosted and cloud-hosted variants)
‚óč
Even after repeated ‚Äúmodular refactors‚ÄĚ, internal coupling made new feature integration painful
‚óč

‚óŹ
Atlassian Jira (Pre-Cloud Era Monolith)
‚óč
Jira‚Äôs early Java monolith grew huge. Teams attempted ‚Äúmodular refactors‚ÄĚ (plugins, OSGi bundles).
‚óč
It remained a massive, interdependent codebase with 1-hour+ build times and fragile tests.
‚óč
The internal APIs between modules were so coupled that teams couldn‚Äôt release independently

Reddit (Python Monolith Bottlenecks)

‚óč

‚óč
Adding new services or features (e.g., live chat, video) kept colliding with the monolith‚Äôs assumptions
‚óŹ
Slack (Pre-2020 Monolith Strain)

Early success built on one large PHP/Hack monolith
‚óč

‚óč

Practical Guidelines
‚óŹAlways refactor with automated tests
‚óŹTrack technical debt like real bugs
‚óŹDon‚Äôt rewrite for ‚Äúnew shiny tools‚ÄĚ
‚óŹPrototype before rewriting fully
‚óŹCommunicate clearly with stakeholders
‚óŹRefactoring improves velocity
‚óŹRewriting resets it - temporarily

Discussion
‚óŹGroup Prompt:
‚óčLook at your current team project
‚óčWhat are 2 - 3 areas that need refactoring?
‚óčWhat conditions might trigger a rewrite later?
‚óŹHow could your team plan gradual
improvements?
‚óŹShare one real-world example you‚Äôve seen of
either

Key Takeaways
‚óŹRefactoring = evolution, Rewriting = revolution
‚óŹRefactor when you can, rewrite when you must
‚óŹMeasure risk, cost, and stability
‚óŹTests are your safety net
‚óŹCommunicate tradeoÔ¨Äs early

Quote
‚ÄúThe only thing harder than starting from scratch
is living forever with a bad design.‚ÄĚ
- Anonymous Architect

Defensive Programming
Writing Code That Protects Itself

Introduction
‚óŹ‚ÄúHope for the best, code for the worst.‚ÄĚ
‚óŹPart of robust software engineering practices
‚óŹPrevents crashes, undeÔ¨Āned behavior, and misuse
‚óŹComplements testing, not replaces it

What Is Defensive Programming?
‚óŹA mindset and set of techniques that make
software behave predictably even under
unexpected conditions.
‚óŹGoal:
‚óčAnticipate failures and invalid inputs
‚óčPrevent or limit damage when something goes
wrong
‚óčCommunicate errors clearly

Why It Matters
‚óŹPrevents subtle bugs and crashes
‚óŹSaves time during debugging
‚óŹImproves maintainability
‚óŹReduces security vulnerabilities
‚óŹMakes code safer to modify or reuse
‚óŹGood code ‚Äúfails safe,‚ÄĚ not ‚Äúfails hard.‚ÄĚ

Key Principles
‚óŹValidate all inputs (from users, APIs, Ô¨Āles, etc.)
‚óŹAvoid assumptions about data, environment, or
state
‚óŹFail fast, fail clearly (use exceptions, assert)
‚óŹUse guard clauses instead of deep nesting
‚óŹHandle edge cases (nulls, empty arrays, limits)
‚óŹKeep functions predictable - minimize side
eÔ¨Äects

Fail-Safe Defaults
‚óŹWhen uncertain, prefer safe behavior
‚óčDeny access rather than allow by default
‚óčAssume missing input is invalid
‚óčDefault to ‚Äúno action‚ÄĚ instead of risky action
‚óŹExample:
if (!user?.hasPermission("delete")) return;
// deny by default

Defensive Programming vs Testing
‚óŹDefensive Programming
‚óčPrevents problems inside the code
‚óŹTesting
‚óčDetects problems after the code runs
‚óŹTogether
‚óčBuild resilient systems that handle both expected
and unexpected cases

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Common Anti-Patterns to watch out for
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Common Anti-Patterns to watch out for
‚óŹMagic numbers
‚óŹSilent catches (exception swallowing)
‚óŹAssuming valid input
‚óŹOvertrusting conÔ¨Āg or environment
‚óŹIgnoring null or error return codes

Real-World Connection
‚óŹEvery input Ô¨Āeld on a website needs validation
(front & back end) - Don‚Äôt trust users
‚óŹEvery API endpoint must check authentication,
authorization, and data type
‚óŹEvery command-line argument needs to be
veriÔ¨Āed before used
‚óŹDefensive programming = security, reliability,
and professionalism

Summary
‚óŹValidate all input
‚óŹFail fast and clearly
‚óŹUse guard clauses and assertions
‚óŹPrefer safe defaults
‚óŹDon‚Äôt assume - verify
‚óŹ‚ÄúCode should protect itself from both users and

Feature Flags
Controlling access, risk, rollout, and behavior in software systems

What is a Feature Flag?
‚óŹA feature Ô¨āag is a switch that controls whether a
feature is enabled
‚óŹThe code exists even when the feature is hidden
or disabled
‚óŹFlags can be changed by environment, user, role,
tier, region, or runtime condition
‚óŹThey help separate building a feature from
exposing it

Why Use Feature Flags?
‚óŹNot every feature is ready for all users at the
same time
‚óŹSome features are risky, experimental, premium,
or operationally sensitive
‚óŹTeams often need more control than ‚Äúdeployed‚ÄĚ
or ‚Äúnot deployed‚ÄĚ
‚óŹFlags provide a way to introduce change more
carefully

The Basic Concept
‚óŹWithout Ô¨āags, a deployed feature may become
immediately visible to everyone
‚óŹWith Ô¨āags, the feature can stay oÔ¨Ä until explicitly
enabled
‚óŹThis gives developers and operators more control
over timing and exposure
‚óŹExample idea:
‚óč‚ÄúIf the Ô¨āag is on, use the new path. Otherwise,
keep the current behavior.‚ÄĚ

Deployment vs Release
‚óŹDeployment means code is installed in an
environment
‚óŹRelease means users are allowed to access it
‚óŹFeature Ô¨āags let those happen at diÔ¨Äerent times
‚óŹThis distinction is one of the main reasons Ô¨āags
are so useful

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: Common reasons to use feature Ô¨āags
slideIndex.extracted_text:
Common reasons to use feature Ô¨āags
‚óŹHide incomplete work
‚óŹReduce risk during rollout
‚óŹRun experiments
‚óŹLimit features to certain users
‚óŹSupport premium or enterprise tiers
‚óŹDisable unstable functionality quickly

Common categories of Ô¨āags
‚óŹRelease Ô¨āags
‚óŹExperiment Ô¨āags
‚óŹOperations Ô¨āags
‚óŹPermission or entitlement Ô¨āags
‚óŹTier or subscription Ô¨āags
‚óŹKill switches

Release Ô¨āags
‚óŹUsed to hide new functionality until it is ready
‚óŹCommon for UI redesigns, workÔ¨āows, new
services, or new integrations
‚óŹAllows code to be merged and deployed before
public availability
‚óŹEspecially useful when large features take
multiple iterations

Experiment Ô¨āags
‚óŹUsed to compare multiple versions of a feature
‚óŹHelpful in A/B testing or controlled evaluation
‚óŹCan compare layouts, Ô¨āows, algorithms, or
engagement strategies
‚óŹLets teams observe behavior before choosing a
permanent approach

Operations Ô¨āags
‚óŹUsed to manage system behavior in production
‚óŹCan disable a costly, unstable, or overloaded
subsystem
‚óŹUseful when an external dependency becomes
unreliable
‚óŹGives operators a safer way to respond during
incidents

Kill switches
‚óŹA kill switch is a high-priority Ô¨āag used to shut
oÔ¨Ä a feature quickly
‚óŹOften used when a release causes failures,
latency, cost spikes, or bad user outcomes
‚óŹSafer than waiting for a full redeploy in some
situations
‚óŹOne of the most operationally valuable forms of
feature Ô¨āagging

Where feature Ô¨āags can live
‚óŹIn the frontend or UI
‚óŹIn the service or middle layer
‚óŹIn the backend or API
‚óŹIn conÔ¨Āguration services or Ô¨āag platforms
‚óŹIn data-driven policy or entitlement systems

Feature Ô¨āags in the UI
‚óŹHide or show buttons, pages, menus, and
workÔ¨āows
‚óŹSwitch between old and new interfaces
‚óŹReveal beta features only to selected users
‚óŹUseful for presentation and user experience
control

secure enforcement

Feature Ô¨āags in middle layers
‚óŹControl business workÔ¨āows and orchestration
‚óŹSelect between old and new service paths
‚óŹChange rules without exposing all logic in the UI
‚óŹKeep decisions closer to the application‚Äôs
business behavior

Feature Ô¨āags in backend layers
‚óŹEnable or disable API endpoints or logic paths
‚óŹGate premium, admin, or experimental features
at the source
‚óŹProtect resources even if a client tries to bypass
the UI
‚óŹBest place for strong enforcement when access
must be controlled

UI hiding is not security
‚óŹHiding a feature in the interface does not prevent
direct access
‚óŹA determined user may still call an API or
manipulate requests
‚óŹBackend checks are necessary when Ô¨āags aÔ¨Äect
access control or protected capability
‚óŹPresentation control and authorization are not
the same thing

Feature Ô¨āags and SaaS tiers
‚óŹFlags are often used to support product tiers
‚óŹFree, Basic, Pro, Business, and Enterprise plans
may expose diÔ¨Äerent capabilities
‚óŹA single codebase can support multiple
commercial oÔ¨Äerings
‚óŹFlags make it easier to control availability by plan
or entitlement

Tier-based examples
‚óŹFree users get limited reports
‚óŹPro users get advanced analytics
‚óŹEnterprise users get SSO, audit logs, and admin
controls
‚óŹSome integrations may only appear for higher
subscription levels
‚óŹKey point: tiers are often implemented with a mix of
Ô¨āags, permissions, and billing entitlements

Feature Ô¨āags and user segmentation
‚óŹFeatures may be enabled by:
‚óčuser role
‚óčRegion
‚óčdevice type
‚óčaccount age
‚óčinvitation status
‚óčinternal staÔ¨Ä membership
‚óŹThis allows more targeted rollout and more tailored
product behavior

Feature Ô¨āags in games
‚óŹEnable seasonal or event-based content
‚óŹRelease new modes gradually
‚óŹTest mechanics with a subset of players
‚óŹGate content by region, platform, or player
segment
‚óŹShut oÔ¨Ä problematic features without taking the
whole game down

Game-speciÔ¨Āc examples
‚óŹHoliday events activated for a short period
‚óŹNew matchmaking rules enabled for only part of
the player base
‚óŹBeta maps or limited-time modes exposed
selectively
‚óŹPremium passes, cosmetics, etc oÔ¨Äers controlled
dynamically

Best practices
‚óŹUse clear, descriptive names
‚óŹDocument purpose, owner, and intended
lifespan
‚óŹTest both ON and OFF states
‚óŹMake default behavior explicit
‚óŹMonitor impact after changing a Ô¨āag
‚óŹRemove Ô¨āags when they are no longer needed

Common problems and risks
‚óŹToo many Ô¨āags can make code harder to reason
about
‚óŹOld Ô¨āags create ‚ÄúÔ¨āag debt‚ÄĚ
‚óŹNested Ô¨āags multiply complexity
‚óŹDiÔ¨Äerent users may see inconsistent behavior
‚óŹDebugging becomes harder when runtime

slideIndex.slide_title_or_first_line: When not to use a feature Ô¨āag
slideIndex.extracted_text:
When not to use a feature Ô¨āag
‚óŹWhen the change is trivial and low risk
‚óŹWhen proper authorization is needed instead of
a temporary toggle
‚óŹWhen the team has no plan to manage or
remove the Ô¨āag
‚óŹWhen the Ô¨āag would create more confusion than

Feature Flags vs Feature ConÔ¨Āguration
‚óŹ
Not all gating is simple true/false
‚óŹ
A feature Ô¨āag usually answers:
‚óč
Is this enabled?
‚óŹ
A feature parameter or feature conÔ¨Āguration often answers:
‚óč

‚óŹ
A system may use:

‚óč
numeric limits
‚óč

‚óč
enum/string modes
‚óč
structured policy settings
‚óŹ
Key idea: Flags control availability. ConÔ¨Āguration controls behavior, scale,
limits, or variants.

Entitlements, Quotas, and Limits
‚óŹEntitlements deÔ¨Āne what a user, role, or tier is
allowed to access
‚óŹQuotas deÔ¨Āne how much of something is allowed
‚óŹLimits deÔ¨Āne boundaries for usage, capacity, or
frequency
‚óŹThese are often related to feature Ô¨āags, but they are
usually more expressive than a simple on/oÔ¨Ä switch

Entitlements, Quotas, and Limits - Examples
‚óŹ
UI layer
‚óč

‚óŹ

‚óč

‚óŹ

‚óč

Useful distinction
‚óč
Entitlement: ‚ÄúCan this account use the feature?‚ÄĚ
‚óč

‚óč

Closing takeaway

Re-Evaluating the MVP & Preparing for Code Freeze
‚óŹGoal: Deliver a working product matters more
than delivering everything (partially working)

The Late-Stage Trap
‚óŹCommon team behaviors near the end:
‚óčAdding ‚Äújust one more feature‚ÄĚ ‚Ä¶ HOWEVER
‚óčNeglecting documentation or polish
‚óčIgnoring testing debt. What hasn‚Äôt been tested

‚óčOver-optimizing things users will never see

What MVP Really Means (Now)

‚óŹMinimum: Only essential functionality for the

‚óŹViable: Reliable, stable, demonstrable

‚óŹAsk: ‚ÄúIf this were the only version that ever

How to Re-Evaluate Your MVP

‚óŹA smaller working product beats a large unÔ¨Ānished one
‚óŹSpeciÔ¨Ācally for this class: MORE deliverables - product,

Code Freeze: What It Is & Why It Matters
‚óŹDeÔ¨Ānition:
‚óčA period when no new features are added - only
Ô¨Āxes, tests, or documentation updates allowed
‚óŹPurpose:
‚óčStabilize the Ô¨Ānal build
‚óčPrevent last-minute chaos
‚óčAllow focused testing & polishing
‚óŹFreezing is about quality control, not stopping

Final Polish Checklist

‚óŹCritical tests pass
‚óŹDocumentation up to date
‚óŹREADME includes setup, known issues, and
credits (who did what)
‚óŹDemo script rehearsed - rehearsal is important,

The ‚ÄúHOWEVER‚ÄĚ - The WOW factor
‚óŹYour goal now is to remove the extra features you don‚Äôt need.
However‚Ä¶
‚óŹIs there something you can add, a feature, that can make your

‚óčBut be aware of the risk
‚óčDon‚Äôt sacriÔ¨Āce the rest of the product for the one cool thing
‚óčThe ‚Äúthing‚ÄĚ could simply be a problem you had, and that

Troubleshooting Methods
Debugging Code, Systems, People

Introduction
‚ÄúThink of a bug as a story - your job is to trace its
plot.‚ÄĚ

Objectives
‚óŹIdentify categories of software and process failures
‚óŹApply systematic debugging and diagnostic methods
‚óŹUse logs, tests, and tools to reproduce and isolate
issues
‚óŹConduct root-cause analysis (RCA) on technical and
human errors
‚óŹDevelop team-based triage and escalation workÔ¨āows

Why Troubleshooting Matters
‚óŹ70‚Äď80% of engineering time is spent diagnosing,
not coding
‚óŹEvery system failure is both a technical and a
communication issue
‚óŹGood troubleshooting = fewer regressions +
faster delivery
‚óŹCore skill for DevOps, QA, and leadership roles

Troubleshooting Mindset
‚óŹBe curious, not defensive
‚óŹAssume unknowns, not incompetence
‚óŹVerify assumptions with data, not anecdotes
‚óŹTreat symptoms as clues, not conclusions
‚óŹKeep notes - every Ô¨Āx is potential documentation
‚óŹThink outside of the box

Systematic Debugging Steps
‚óŹReproduce the issue reliably
‚óŹIsolate components or layers (frontend, API, DB)
‚óŹInstrument: add logs, breakpoints, telemetry
‚óŹHypothesize causes ‚Üí test each
‚óŹValidate the Ô¨Āx with regression tests
‚óŹDocument both the bug and the reasoning

Tools of the Trade
‚óŹDebugger: breakpoints, watches, step-through
‚óŹLogs: timestamped, structured (JSON preferred)
‚óŹPostman / Swagger: API veriÔ¨Ācation
‚óŹUnit tests: repeatable proof of correctness
‚óŹProÔ¨Ālers: detect memory or CPU hotspots
‚óŹVersion control: diÔ¨Ä & blame as history tools

Debugging Anti-Patterns
‚óŹPanic commits (‚Äútry this quick Ô¨Āx‚ÄĚ)
‚óŹGuessing without reproducing
‚óŹChanging multiple variables, methods at once
‚óŹIgnoring warnings and logs
‚óŹNot writing regression tests after Ô¨Āx
‚óŹIgnoring previous working versions

Root Cause Analysis (RCA)
‚óŹGoal: Ô¨Ānd the real source, not just the symptom
‚óŹCommon tools:
‚óč5 Whys: keep asking ‚Äúwhy?‚ÄĚ until you hit a root
cause
‚óčFishbone (Ishikawa) diagram: visualize causes
(People, Process, Tools, Environment)
‚óčTimeline reconstruction: map event sequence
‚óčChange correlation: ‚ÄúWhat changed right before
failure?‚ÄĚ

Root Cause Analysis (RCA) - 5 Whys
‚óŹ
Example: The API Outage
‚óŹ
Problem: The production API was down for 30 minutes
‚óč
Why? Because the application crashed
‚óč
Why? Because it ran out of memory
‚óč
Why? Because a background job loaded too much data into memory
‚óč
Why? Because the job wasn‚Äôt paginated
‚óč
Why? Because pagination wasn‚Äôt included in the design review process
‚óŹ
Root cause:
‚óč
A process gap - lack of technical review for data-intensive jobs
‚óč
Fix: Add a review checklist for jobs that query large datasets

‚óŹFocus on facts, not blame
‚óŹKeep the group small (2 - 4 people directly involved)
‚óŹWrite each ‚Äúwhy‚ÄĚ visibly (whiteboard or shared doc)
‚óŹStop when you reach something within your control
to Ô¨Āx
‚óŹCombine with other RCA tools (e.g., Fishbone
Diagram) for complex cases

Team-Based Troubleshooting
‚óŹConduct triage meetings: prioritize by severity &

Communication Under Pressure

Key Takeaways
‚óŹDon‚Äôt Ô¨Āx before you understand
‚óŹEvery bug has both a technical and process side
‚óŹDebugging ‚Č† blame; it‚Äôs investigation

Example 1 - ‚ÄúThe Vanishing Data‚ÄĚ
‚óŹScenario:
‚óčA user reports that after submitting a form, their

‚óŹSymptoms:

Example 5 - ‚ÄúThe Phantom Test Failure‚ÄĚ
