Example 6 - ‚ÄúThe API That Ghosted Us‚ÄĚ

Key Point: Semantic Versioning

Why Versioning Exists - Why Bother?
‚óŹEvery release should be traceable and
reproducible
‚óŹTeams, testers, and users need to know what
changed and when
‚óŹVersion numbers connect code ‚Üí commits ‚Üí

Semantic Versioning (SemVer) - read more
‚óŹSemantic Versioning: ‚ÄúA shared language for change‚ÄĚ
‚óŹContent:
‚óčFormat: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
‚óčExample: v2.4.6
‚óčMAJOR ‚Üí Breaking changes
‚óčMINOR ‚Üí New features, still compatible
‚óčPATCH ‚Üí Bug Ô¨Āxes, no behavior change
‚Ė†SuÔ¨Éxes: -alpha, -beta, -rc.1 for pre-release

‚óŹHelps CI/CD pipelines tag deployable builds
‚óŹEnables auto-updates for safe versions (^1.2.0 in

‚óŹVersioned API allows for backward compatibility
while encouraging upgrades
‚óŹEncourages teams to think about API stability
‚óŹLinks well to Git tags and milestones (v1.0.0, v1.1.0,
etc.)

Versioning APIs - Keeping Clients Stable
‚óŹWhy Version APIs
‚óčPrevents breaking existing client apps when
APIs evolve
‚óčAllows gradual rollout of new features
‚óčEnables teams to maintain multiple active
versions during migration
‚óčBuilds trust - clients know what to expect
from each version

‚óŹ

‚óŹ
WorkÔ¨āow:
‚óč
Label issues ‚Üí categorize impact (bug, enhancement, breaking)
‚óč
Group them under a milestone (v1.1.0)
‚óč
Fix and merge into main branch
‚óč
Tag the release (v1.1.0)
‚óč
Document changes in CHANGELOG.md
‚óŹ
Takeaway:
‚óč
‚ÄúVersioning tells what changed. Labels and milestones tell why
and how.‚ÄĚ

Final Thought
‚óŹAnswer two questions below:
‚óčWhat is the current version of your product
now?
‚óčWhat will be the anticipated version of your
product at the end of Sprint 6 (the demo
version)?

### Slide 2 of 26: WHAT IS SCM? Source Control Management

WHAT IS SCM? Source Control Management
Source Control Management (SCM), also known as
Version Control Systems (VCS), is a critical
component in modern software development.
SCM/VCS allows teams to track and manage
changes (and changers) to source code over time.

---

### Slide 3 of 26: SCM - History

SCM - History
-
Early Systems (1970s-1980s)
-
Source Code Control System (SCCS), Centralized, Obsolete
-
Revision Control System (RCS), Centralized, Limited Availability
-
1990s: Concurrent Development
-
Concurrent Versions System (CVS), Centralized, Replaced
-
Perforce (Helix Core), Centralized, Actively maintained
-
2000s: Distributed Revolution
-
Subversion (SVN), Centralized,  Active but declining
-
Git, Distributed, Active (Most)
-
Mercurial, Distributed, Declining
-
Modern Platforms
-
GitHub, Cloud, Active
-
GitLab, Cloud/Self, Active
-
Bitbucket (Git and Mercurial), Cloud/Self, Active
-
Azure DevOps (Formerly VSTS/TFS), Centralized and Distributed, Active

---

### Slide 4 of 26: SCM - Centralized vs Distributed - 1 of 2

SCM - Centralized vs Distributed - 1 of 2
Tools
SVN, Perforce, CVS
Git, Mercurial, Fossil
Repository Architecture
Single central repository
Every user has a full copy (clone) of the repository
Internet/Network Dependency
Always required for most actions
Only needed to push/pull; full
(e.g., commits)
local operations possible
Commit Behavior
Commits go directly to central repo
Commits are local Ô¨Ārst, then pushed to remote
Cloning
Not applicable; only checkout supported
Cloning includes full history and branches
OÔ¨Ąine Work
Limited; commits need network
Fully functional (commit, branch, log, diÔ¨Ä, etc.)
Branching
Limited, often slow or painful (SVN)
Fast and cheap; branches are Ô¨Ārst-class citizens
Merging
Can be more complex/conÔ¨āict-prone
Sophisticated merge handling (esp. in Git)
History
Stored only on server
Fully replicated on every client

---

### Slide 5 of 26: SCM - Centralized vs Distributed - 2 of 2

SCM - Centralized vs Distributed - 2 of 2
Tools
SVN, Perforce, CVS
Git, Mercurial, Fossil
Speed
Slower; depends on network/server
Fast local operations
ConÔ¨āict Resolution
Typically server-side or at commit time
Handled locally before pushing
Backup/Redundancy
Single point of failure unless server is backed up
Every clone is a full backup
Ease of Auditing
Simple central log history
Can be complex due to local
histories
Security (by default)
Easy to enforce with central repo and ACLs
More Ô¨āexible but needs extra
enforcement tools
Scalability for Teams
Easy to manage with strict roles & access
Needs conventions and tooling
to manage Ô¨āow
Best For
Teams with stable, centralized infrastructure
Agile teams, open-source,
distributed workforces

### Slide 7 of 26: SCM Terminology - 1 of 2

SCM Terminology - 1 of 2
Repository (Repo)
A storage location for code, history, and branches.
Remote
A version of your repository hosted elsewhere (e.g., GitHub).
Origin
The default name Git gives to the remote repository you cloned from.
Local
 Your working copy of the repository on your machine.
Commit
A saved snapshot of your changes. Each commit has a unique ID and message.
Branch
A separate line of development. Default branch is usually main or master.
Clone
Create a local copy of a remote repository.
Pull
Fetch and merge changes from a remote repository to your local branch.
Push
Send your local commits to the remote repository.
Merge
Combine changes from one branch into another.
Rebase
Replay commits from one branch onto another. It rewrites history.

---

### Slide 8 of 26: SCM Terminology - 2 of 2

SCM Terminology - 2 of 2
Fetch
Download commits and refs from a remote without merging them.
Pull Request (PR)
A request to merge changes from one branch into another (usually used on hosted platforms).
Tag
A named point in history, often used for marking releases.
Checkout
Switch to another branch or commit.
Staging Area (Index)
Where you prepare changes before committing.
ConÔ¨āict
Happens when Git can‚Äôt automatically merge changes.
DiÔ¨Ä
Shows diÔ¨Äerences between commits, branches, or the working directory.
Fork
A personal copy of a remote repository, often used to contribute to open source.
Cherry-pick
Apply a speciÔ¨Āc commit from one branch onto another.
Squash
Combine multiple commits into one before merging.
Fast-forward merge
A merge where the target branch is directly updated without a merge commit.

### Slide 16 of 31: Monolith - What is it?

### Slide 17 of 31: Monolith - Characteristics

‚óŹTightly coupled modules and layers (UI, business

‚óŹShared memory and resources - all features typically

‚óŹSingle deployment - any change (small or large)

‚óŹCentralized scaling - to handle more load, you scale
the entire application, even if only one part needs it

### Slide 18 of 31: Monolith - When to use one

Monolith - When to use one
‚óŹ
Early-stage startups / MVPs
‚óč
Speed > scalability. A single deployable unit gets you to market faster
‚óŹ
Small teams with limited resources (Ex. Portfolio Projects)
‚óč
Easier to develop, test, deploy, and maintain with a handful of developers
‚óŹ
Applications with a simple, well-deÔ¨Āned domain (Ex. gym)
‚óč
Business logic isn‚Äôt complex enough to justify microservices
‚óŹ
Low scalability or traÔ¨Éc expectations (Ex, Internal tools)
‚óč
The system doesn‚Äôt need to handle millions of concurrent users
‚óŹ
Tight deadlines (Ex. School app)
‚óč
Monoliths allow fast iteration and feature shipping when time-to-market is critical
‚óŹ
Strong need for consistency (Ex. Banking)
‚óč
Monoliths avoid distributed data challenges (e.g., eventual consistency in microservices)
‚óŹ
Team skillset is not ready for distributed systems (Ex. First Custom Tools)
‚óč
Microservices demand more expertise in DevOps, CI/CD, observability, and scaling
‚óŹ
Applications that won‚Äôt evolve dramatically (Ex. IRS tools)
‚óč
If the scope is Ô¨Āxed and unlikely to expand, a monolith avoids over-engineering

---

‚óŹ

When diÔ¨Äerent parts of the system scale diÔ¨Äerently (e.g., checkout vs browsing)
‚óŹ

‚óč
If teams need to release features on separate cadences without waiting for others
‚óŹ
Globally distributed systems (Ex. Ride Sharing)
‚óč
When latency and availability requirements demand services close to diÔ¨Äerent regions
‚óŹ
Polyglot tech stack requirements (Ex. FinTech with Fraud Detection)
‚óč
When diÔ¨Äerent components need diÔ¨Äerent technologies (e.g., Python for ML, Node.js for real-time APIs)
‚óŹ
Critical uptime and fault isolation (Ex. Airline reservation)

A bug in one module shouldn‚Äôt bring down the entire system

Regulatory or compliance isolation (Ex. Patient Data)
‚óč
When speciÔ¨Āc services require diÔ¨Äerent audit, security, or compliance scopes

solution now means extra work later when
refactoring, debugging, or scaling

together toward a single purpose (best)
‚óčSequential Cohesion - Output of one part is
input to another
‚óčCoincidental Cohesion - Unrelated logic
thrown together in the same module (worst)

‚óč

‚óč

‚óč

### Slide 4 of 26: Language Idioms - ‚ÄúPatterns‚ÄĚ at the code level

‚óŹ

‚óŹ

// Non-Idiomatic
// Idiomatic

// Older
if (inputName !== undefined &&
let username = inputName || "Guest";
      inputName !== null) {
// ES2020+
  username = inputName;
let username = inputName ?? "Guest";
} else {

  username = "Guest";
}
C++:

// Idiomatic, modern
for (int i = 0; i < vec.size(); i++) {
for (const auto& item : vec) {
  std::cout << vec[i] << std::endl;
  std::cout << item << std::endl;
}
}

### Slide 11 of 35: RED - Write a failing test

RED - Write a failing test
public class PrimeCheckerTests
{
    [Fact]
    public void IsPrime_Of2_ReturnsTrue()
    {
  // arrange
  int input = 2;
  // act - This method doesn‚Äôt exist yet
  result = PrimeChecker.IsPrime(input);
  // assert
  Assert.True(result);
    }
}

---

### Slide 12 of 35: GREEN - Write enough code to pass the test

GREEN - Write enough code to pass the test
// add the missing function, write enough code to pass
public static class PrimeChecker
{
    public static bool IsPrime(int n)
    {
        return n == 2;
    }
}
// the test passes because 2 is hardcoded

---

### Slide 13 of 35: REFACTOR - Complete the function to be tested

REFACTOR - Complete the function to be tested
public static class PrimeChecker
{
    public static bool IsPrime(int n)
    {
        if (n <= 1) return false;
        if (n == 2) return true;
        if (n % 2 == 0) return false;
        for (int i = 3; i <= Math.Sqrt(n); i += 2)
        {
            if (n % i == 0) return false;
        }
        return true;
    }
}
// refactor to make the function actually do its job

---

### Slide 14 of 35: REFACTOR - Add real, add more tests

REFACTOR - Add real, add more tests
public class PrimeCheckerTests
{
    // arrange
    [Theory]
    [InlineData(2, true)]
    [InlineData(3, true)]
    [InlineData(4, false)]
    [InlineData(5, true)]
    [InlineData(9, false)]
    [InlineData(13, true)]
    [InlineData(1, false)]
    [InlineData(0, false)]
    public void IsPrime_TestsVariousNumbers(int input, bool expected)
    {
   // act
        var result = PrimeChecker.IsPrime(input);
   // assert
        Assert.Equal(expected, result);
    }
}
// all tests pass using different possibilities

### Slide 29 of 35: Mocking and Stubbing

Mocking and Stubbing
‚óŹWhat is a mock?
‚óčA mock is a fake object that records how it was used
‚óŹMocks simulate real dependencies
‚óčExamples: databases, logging, messaging, notiÔ¨Ācations
‚óŹStubs return Ô¨Āxed results
‚óŹHelps isolate logic in tests

‚óŹExamples: Moq (.NET), Mockito (Java), unittest.mock
(Python)

### Slide 30 of 35: Mocking and Stubbing - DEEP DIVE, Mocks in Practice

Mocking and Stubbing - DEEP DIVE, Mocks in Practice
‚óŹUse mocks to test behavior, not just state
‚óŹVerify interactions (e.g., method calls, arguments)
‚óŹAvoid over-mocking: mock what you don‚Äôt own (or have easy
access to)
‚óŹMocks are essential in verifying side eÔ¨Äects: ‚ÄúDid we send the
email?‚ÄĚ, ‚ÄúWas this logged?‚ÄĚ
‚óŹA mock lets you focus on how code behaves under interaction -
not just what it returns
‚óŹBe careful: too many mocks = fragile tests. Favor real objects
where practical

### Slide 3 of 30: Why Full Stack Development Matters : Why - 1 of 2

Why Full Stack Development Matters : Why - 1 of 2
‚óŹ
Holistic Understanding of Systems
‚óč
Full-stack developers can see how layers interact - UI triggers API, API hits DB, DB
returns results
‚óč
This reduces blind spots between teams
‚óč
They make better architectural decisions because they understand dependencies and
bottlenecks
‚óŹ
Improved Collaboration
‚óč
Knowing all layers helps developers communicate eÔ¨Äectively across front-end, API,
and database teams
‚óč
Easier debugging and integration - they can trace an issue end-to-end
‚óŹ
Faster Prototyping & MVP Delivery
‚óč
For startups and small teams, a single developer or small group can produce a
working end-to-end prototype quickly
‚óč
One person can spin up UI + backend + database locally, test the Ô¨āow, and validate
the concept

---

### Slide 4 of 30: Why Full Stack Development Matters : Why - 2 of 2

Why Full Stack Development Matters : Why - 2 of 2
‚óŹ
Better Troubleshooting and Debugging
‚óč
A full-stack mindset allows a developer to trace bugs through the entire request-response lifecycle
‚óč
Example: An API error might originate in a UI assumption, or a UI slowdown might come from
an ineÔ¨Écient query
‚óŹ
Context-Aware Optimization
‚óč
They can identify where performance improvements matter most (e.g., database indexing vs
front-end caching)
‚óč
Helps avoid premature optimization or narrow focus on only one layer
‚óŹ
Flexibility & Employability
‚óč
Full-stack developers can plug into more roles
‚óč
In modern teams, even specialized devs beneÔ¨Āt from broad stack literacy
‚óŹ
Product Ownership Mindset
‚óč
FSD encourages thinking like a product engineer - not just a coder
‚óč
Helps bridge technical and business goals, as they see the system as a product, not a codebase

Before the web era, systems still needed to communicate - but mostly within local networks or
mainframes, not browsers
RPC (Remote Procedure Call) : 1980s‚Äď90s
Allowed one program to execute code (a ‚Äúprocedure‚ÄĚ) on another machine as if it were local
Foundation for many distributed systems
CORBA (Common Object Request Broker Architecture) : Early 1990s
Language-agnostic object communication standard; used by enterprise systems before web APIs
DCOM (Distributed COM) : 1990s
Microsoft‚Äôs distributed object model; similar to CORBA but Windows-specific
RMI (Remote Method Invocation) : Late 1990s
Java‚Äôs object-level network communication mechanism - early step toward networked APIs
Socket Programming : 1980s onward
Low-level communication (TCP/UDP) - building blocks for all later API technologies

### Slide 26 of 38: Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 1 of 3

Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 1 of 3
‚óŹ
Synchronous (Sync) Calls - The client sends a request and waits until
the server responds
‚óŹ
Behavior: Blocking - client cannot continue until a response
(success/error) is received
‚óŹ
Use Cases:
‚óč
Standard CRUD operations (create/read/update/delete)
‚óč
APIs where immediate results are needed (e.g., login, form
submission)
‚óŹ
Pros: Simple to implement and reason about
‚óŹ
Cons: Slower under heavy load; wastes time waiting for long
operations

---

### Slide 27 of 38: Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 2 of 3

Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 2 of 3
‚óŹ
Asynchronous (Async) Calls - The client sends a request and does not
wait for the server to Ô¨Ānish
‚óŹ
The server acknowledges receipt and processes in background
‚óŹ
Client may receive:
‚óč
202 Accepted (work queued)
‚óč
Callback/Webhook when complete
‚óč
Polling endpoint to check status
‚óŹ
Use Cases:
‚óč
Long-running operations (video processing, Ô¨Āle uploads, data
analysis)
‚óč
Event-driven systems or batch jobs

---

### Slide 28 of 38: Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 3 of 3

Synchronous vs Asynchronous REST Calls - 3 of 3
‚óŹREST itself is transport-agnostic - sync vs async
depends on implementation pattern, not
protocol
‚óŹAsync designs improve scalability and user
experience
‚óŹCombine async REST with WebSockets, SignalR,
or message queues for real-time updates

### Slide 11 of 29: DDD Building Blocks - Analogy 1

‚óŹ

‚óŹ

‚óŹ

‚óŹ

‚óŹ

‚óŹ

‚óŹ

‚óŹ
Notes:
‚óč

‚óč

‚óč

### Slide 13 of 29: DDD Building Blocks - Analogy 3

‚óŹ

### Slide 24 of 33: Challenge #1: Data Models Don‚Äôt Always Match

### Slide 25 of 33: Challenge #2: Many Users at the Same Time

‚óŹSolution:

Ô¨Ānishes before the next starts

### Slide 26 of 33: Challenge #3: Keeping Data Reliable (ACID)

Challenge #3: Keeping Data Reliable (ACID)
To keep data correct, databases follow the ACID principles:
Letter
Meaning Simple
Explanation
A ‚Äď Atomicity
All or nothing
Either all steps happen or none do
C ‚Äď Consistency
Always valid
Data follows the rules (no negatives,
no duplicates)
I ‚Äď Isolation
No interference
One user‚Äôs change doesn‚Äôt mess up another‚Äôs
D ‚Äď Durability
It stays saved
Once conÔ¨Ārmed, it won‚Äôt disappear
even if power fails
ACID = the promise that data is safe, consistent, and complete.

---

### Slide 27 of 33: Challenge #4: Performance and Speed

‚óŹMore data ‚Üí slower reads and writes

‚óčStore frequent results in cache
‚óčFetch only what‚Äôs needed
‚óčCreate replicas (mirrors, read-only)

‚óŹSolutions:

## Deck 17: Defensive Programming

**Source PDF:** `Defensive Programming.pdf`  
**Slides:** 18

### Slide 1 of 18: Defensive Programming

Defensive Programming
Writing Code That Protects Itself

---

### Slide 2 of 18: Introduction

Introduction
‚óŹ‚ÄúHope for the best, code for the worst.‚ÄĚ
‚óŹPart of robust software engineering practices
‚óŹPrevents crashes, undeÔ¨Āned behavior, and misuse
‚óŹComplements testing, not replaces it

---

### Slide 3 of 18: What Is Defensive Programming?

What Is Defensive Programming?
‚óŹA mindset and set of techniques that make
software behave predictably even under
unexpected conditions.
‚óŹGoal:
‚óčAnticipate failures and invalid inputs
‚óčPrevent or limit damage when something goes
wrong
‚óčCommunicate errors clearly

---

### Slide 4 of 18: Why It Matters

Why It Matters
‚óŹPrevents subtle bugs and crashes
‚óŹSaves time during debugging
‚óŹImproves maintainability
‚óŹReduces security vulnerabilities
‚óŹMakes code safer to modify or reuse
‚óŹGood code ‚Äúfails safe,‚ÄĚ not ‚Äúfails hard.‚ÄĚ

---

### Slide 5 of 18: Key Principles

Key Principles
‚óŹValidate all inputs (from users, APIs, Ô¨Āles, etc.)
‚óŹAvoid assumptions about data, environment, or
state
‚óŹFail fast, fail clearly (use exceptions, assert)
‚óŹUse guard clauses instead of deep nesting
‚óŹHandle edge cases (nulls, empty arrays, limits)
‚óŹKeep functions predictable - minimize side
eÔ¨Äects

---

### Slide 6 of 18: Example: C++ (Command-line tool)

Example: C++ (Command-line tool)
int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {
    if (argc < 2) {
        std::cerr << "Usage: app <filename>\n";
        return 1;
// Defensive: avoid accessing argv[1] blindly
    }
    std::ifstream file(argv[1]);
    if (!file) {
        std::cerr << "Error: Cannot open file '" << argv[1] << "'\n";
        return 2;
// Defensive: check for I/O failure
    }
    std::string line;
    while (std::getline(file, line)) {
        if (line.empty()) continue;
// Defensive: skip blank lines
        std::cout << line << "\n";
    }
    return 0;
}

---

### Slide 7 of 18: Example: C++ (Command-line tool)

Example: C++ (Command-line tool)
int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {
    if (argc < 2) {
        std::cerr << "Usage: app <filename>\n";
        return 1;
    }
    std::ifstream file(argv[1]);
    if (!file) {
        std::cerr << "Error: Cannot open file '" << argv[1] << "'\n";
        return 2;
    }
    std::string line;
    while (std::getline(file, line)) {
        if (line.empty()) continue;
        std::cout << line << "\n";
    }
    return 0;
}

### Slide 8 of 18: Example: JavaScript (Web Input)

{

### Slide 10 of 18: Guard Clauses vs Deep Nesting

Guard Clauses vs Deep Nesting
Not defensive:

    if (user.IsActive) {
        if (user.Role == "Admin") {
            PerformAction();
        }
    }
}

---

### Slide 11 of 18: Guard Clauses vs Deep Nesting

Guard Clauses vs Deep Nesting
Defensive (guard clauses):
if (user == null) return;
if (!user.IsActive) return;
if (user.Role != "Admin") return;
PerformAction();
Why it‚Äôs better:
Easier to read and maintain
Minimizes nesting and risk of missing else branches

---

### Slide 12 of 18: Assertions

Assertions
Assertions help catch programmer errors early. A fail-fast mechanism.
C++ example
#include <cassert>
int divide(int a, int b) {
  assert(b != 0 && "b must not be zero");
  return a / b;
}
C# example:
Debug.Assert(value != null, "Value should never be null");
JS (runtime style):
console.assert(Array.isArray(items), "items must be an array");

---

### Slide 13 of 18: Fail-Safe Defaults

Fail-Safe Defaults
‚óŹWhen uncertain, prefer safe behavior
‚óčDeny access rather than allow by default
‚óčAssume missing input is invalid
‚óčDefault to ‚Äúno action‚ÄĚ instead of risky action
‚óŹExample:
if (!user?.hasPermission("delete")) return;
// deny by default

---

### Slide 14 of 18: Handling Exceptions Gracefully

Handling Exceptions Gracefully
‚óŹ
Catch only what you can handle
‚óŹ
Don‚Äôt suppress or ignore errors
‚óŹ
Log unexpected conditions
‚óŹ
Keep user-facing messages clear and non-technical
‚óŹ
C++ example:
try {
    riskyOperation();
} catch (const std::exception& e) {
    std::cerr << "Operation failed: " << e.what() << '\n';
}

---

### Slide 15 of 18: Defensive Programming vs Testing

Defensive Programming vs Testing
‚óŹDefensive Programming
‚óčPrevents problems inside the code
‚óŹTesting
‚óčDetects problems after the code runs
‚óŹTogether
‚óčBuild resilient systems that handle both expected
and unexpected cases

---

### Slide 16 of 18: Common Anti-Patterns to watch out for

Common Anti-Patterns to watch out for
‚óŹMagic numbers
‚óŹSilent catches (exception swallowing)
‚óŹAssuming valid input
‚óŹOvertrusting conÔ¨Āg or environment
‚óŹIgnoring null or error return codes

---

### Slide 17 of 18: Real-World Connection

Real-World Connection
‚óŹEvery input Ô¨Āeld on a website needs validation
(front & back end) - Don‚Äôt trust users
‚óŹEvery API endpoint must check authentication,
authorization, and data type
‚óŹEvery command-line argument needs to be
veriÔ¨Āed before used
‚óŹDefensive programming = security, reliability,
and professionalism

---

### Slide 18 of 18: Summary

Summary
‚óŹValidate all input
‚óŹFail fast and clearly
‚óŹUse guard clauses and assertions
‚óŹPrefer safe defaults
‚óŹDon‚Äôt assume - verify

### Slide 4 of 13: Semantic Versioning (SemVer) - read more

Semantic Versioning (SemVer) - read more
‚óŹSemantic Versioning: ‚ÄúA shared language for change‚ÄĚ
‚óŹContent:
‚óčFormat: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
‚óčExample: v2.4.6
‚óčMAJOR ‚Üí Breaking changes
‚óčMINOR ‚Üí New features, still compatible
‚óčPATCH ‚Üí Bug Ô¨Āxes, no behavior change
‚Ė†SuÔ¨Éxes: -alpha, -beta, -rc.1 for pre-release
builds

### Slide 5 of 13: Applying SemVer in Real Projects

‚óŹTells others what kind of changes to expect

while encouraging upgrades
‚óŹEncourages teams to think about API stability
